topics.title: Air Pollution - Sources, Effects, Classification of Pollutants, Atmospheric Transport, Wind Profiles, Atmospheric Stability, Inversion, Turbulence, Dispersion and Diffusion, Gaussian Plume Dispersion Model; Principles and Techniques of Ambient Air and Stack Emission Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT602 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND TOXIC RISKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Air Pollution - Sources, Effects, Classification of Pollutants, Atmospheric Transport, Wind Profiles, Atmospheric Stability, Inversion, Turbulence, Dispersion and Diffusion, Gaussian Plume Dispersion Model; Principles and Techniques of Ambient Air and Stack Emission Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Air Pollution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Pollution (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Air pollution is defined as the presence of one or more contaminants (solid, liquid, or gaseous) in the atmosphere in such quantities and duration that they are, or tend to be, injurious to human health, animal life, vegetation, or property, or which interfere with the comfortable enjoyment of life or property or the conduct of business.*

According to the **Indian National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS, 2009)**, air pollutants refer to any solid, liquid, or gaseous substance (including noise) present in the atmosphere in concentrations that may be harmful to living beings, property, or climate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Reference (CPCB):**
> The **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** under the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981** is the apex regulatory body in India for air quality management. The **National Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Programme (NAMP)** operates **680+ monitoring stations** across India.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **🍵 The Tea Cup Analogy for Air Pollution:**
> Imagine a closed room (the atmosphere) where you spray perfume. The scent (pollutant) spreads through the room. If you keep spraying, the concentration builds up because the room has limited "carrying capacity." Similarly, Earth's atmosphere can absorb/dilute a certain amount of pollution — when emissions exceed this **assimilative capacity**, air pollution occurs.
>
> **🚗 The Highway Analogy for Dispersion:**
> Picture cars on a highway. If traffic is light and orderly, it flows smoothly (stable atmosphere → poor dispersion). If traffic is heavy and chaotic, cars spread out (unstable/turbulent atmosphere → good dispersion). Wind acts like a "conveyor belt" transporting pollutants away from the source.

---

### 1.3 Core Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Plain English | Formal Definition |
|------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Emission** | Smoke coming out | Release of pollutants from a source into atmosphere |
| **Ambient Air** | The air we breathe | Outdoor air to which the general public has access |
| **Primary Pollutant** | Released directly (CO from cars) | Emitted directly from identifiable source |
| **Secondary Pollutant** | Forms in air (Ozone from NOₓ + VOCs) | Formed by chemical reactions in atmosphere |
| **Plume** | Smoke trail from chimney | Geometric pattern of pollutant dispersal |
| **Receptor** | Person/animal exposed | Location where pollutants are measured/impacted |
| **AQI** | Air quality index (0-500) | Composite indicator of air pollution levels |
| **PM₂.₅** | Fine dust | Particulate matter with diameter ≤ **2.5 μm** |
| **PM₁₀** | Coarse dust | Particulate matter with diameter ≤ **10 μm** |
| **BPL** | Below Poverty Line | Socioeconomic classification for emissions impact |

---

### 1.4 Major Historical Air Pollution Disasters (Context for KTU)

> [!CAUTION]
> **Mandatory Historical Context (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> - **Bhopal Disaster (1984)**: Methyl Isocyanate (MIC) leak from Union Carbide plant; **3,800+ deaths**, 500,000+ affected.
> - **London Smog (1952)**: Coal + SO₂ + fog → **4,000+ deaths** in 5 days.
> - **Great Smog of Donora (1948)**: Zn smelter + weather inversion → 20 deaths, 6,000+ hospitalized.
> - **Delhi Air Crisis (Nov 2017)**: Crop stubble burning → PM₂.₅ = **999 μg/m³** (vs. safe 60 μg/m³).

---

### 1.5 The Atmosphere: A Quick Profile

The atmosphere is divided into layers based on temperature gradients:

> [!NOTE]
> **Atmospheric Layers (Bottom to Top):**
> 1. **Troposphere** (0–11 km): Where all weather and pollution occurs. Temperature decreases at **6.5 °C/km** (Environmental Lapse Rate).
> 2. **Stratosphere** (11–50 km): Contains ozone layer. Temperature increases with altitude.
> 3. **Mesosphere** (50–85 km): Temperature decreases.
> 4. **Thermosphere** (85+ km): Contains ionosphere.

The **mixing height** (mixing depth) is the vertical extent of the troposphere where pollutants can disperse — typically **1,000–2,000 m** during the day and **100–300 m** at night.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Air Pollution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Categorization:**
> Sources are classified based on **origin**, **nature**, and **spatial distribution**.

#### A. Natural Sources
- **Volcanic eruptions**: SO₂, H₂S, CO₂, dust
- **Forest fires**: CO, CO₂, NOₓ, PM
- **Biological decay**: CH₄, H₂S, NH₃
- **Sea spray**: Marine aerosols, salt
- **Pollen, dust storms**: Natural PM
- **Biogenic VOCs**: Terpenes from trees (e.g., isoprene from oak)

#### B. Anthropogenic (Man-Made) Sources

| Category | Examples | Major Pollutants |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| **Stationary** | Power plants, refineries | SO₂, NOₓ, PM, CO₂, Mercury |
| **Mobile** | Cars, trucks, aircraft | CO, NOₓ, HC, PM, Pb |
| **Area Sources** | Dry cleaners, gas stations | VOCs, CFCs |
| **Line Sources** | Highways, rail corridors | CO, NOₓ, noise |
| **Biogenic** | Landfills, wastewater | CH₄, H₂S, NH₃ |

> [!TIP]
> **Six Common Criteria Air Pollutants (US-EPA / CPCB) — MEMORIZE for KTU:**
> 1. **CO** (Carbon Monoxide) — colorless, odorless
> 2. **SO₂** (Sulphur Dioxide) — pungent, acidic
> 3. **NO₂** (Nitrogen Dioxide) — reddish-brown
> 4. **PM** (Particulate Matter) — PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅
> 5. **Pb** (Lead) — neurotoxic
> 6. **O₃** (Ground-level Ozone) — secondary

---

### 2.2 Effects of Air Pollution

#### A. Effects on Human Health

| Pollutant | Health Effect | Critical Concentration |
|-----------|---------------|------------------------|
| **CO** | Binds Hb → carboxyhemoglobin; hypoxia | 9 ppm (8-hr) |
| **SO₂** | Bronchoconstriction, asthma | 80 μg/m³ (24-hr) |
| **NO₂** | Respiratory infections, smog | 80 μg/m³ (24-hr) |
| **PM₂.₅** | Lung cancer, CVD mortality | 60 μg/m³ (24-hr) |
| **O₃** | Chest pain, lung damage | 100 μg/m³ (8-hr) |
| **Pb** | CNS damage in children, IQ loss | 1.0 μg/m³ (24-hr) |

#### B. Effects on Environment
- **Acid Rain**: SO₂ + NOₓ + H₂O → H₂SO₄ / HNO₃ (pH < 5.6)
- **Global Warming**: CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, CFCs (greenhouse gases)
- **Ozone Layer Depletion**: CFCs, Halons (release Cl/Br radicals)
- **Photochemical Smog**: NOₓ + VOCs + sunlight → O₃, PAN
- **Eutrophication**: NH₃ deposition → algal blooms
- **Visibility Reduction**: PM scatters light (haze)
- **Material Damage**: Corrosion of metals, stone, monuments (Taj Mahal)

#### C. Effects on Vegetation
- **Stomatal damage**, reduced photosynthesis
- **Yield reduction** in crops (10–20% in high-pollution zones)
- **Visible injury**: Chlorosis, necrosis of leaves

---

### 2.3 Classification of Pollutants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The classification table below is **guaranteed** in either Part A or Part B.

#### A. Based on Origin
- **Primary Pollutants**: Emitted directly (CO, SO₂, NO, PM, HC)
- **Secondary Pollutants**: Formed in atmosphere (O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄, smog)

#### B. Based on State of Matter
- **Gaseous**: CO, SO₂, NOₓ, O₃, VOCs
- **Particulate**: Dust, smoke, fumes, mist, fog
- **Combined**: Smog (smoke + fog), aerosol

#### C. Based on Source
- **Natural** vs. **Anthropogenic**

#### D. Based on Effects
- **Conventional (Criteria)**: CO, SO₂, NO₂, PM, O₃, Pb
- **Hazardous (Toxic)**: As, Cd, Hg, asbestos, benzene
- **Radioactive**: Rn-222, I-131, Sr-90

#### E. Further Particulate Sub-Classification
- **Total Suspended Particulates (TSP)**: ≤ 30–50 μm
- **PM₁₀ (Thoracic)**: ≤ 10 μm (inhalable)
- **PM₂.₅ (Respirable)**: ≤ 2.5 μm (alveolar deposition)
- **Ultrafine**: ≤ 0.1 μm

---

### 2.4 Meteorological Parameters Affecting Air Pollution

#### A. Wind

> [!NOTE]
> **Wind Drives Transport and Dilution.**

**Wind Direction**: Determines *where* pollutants go.
**Wind Speed (u)**: Determines *how fast* they disperse. **Wind rose diagrams** are used to show prevailing wind patterns.

**Wind Rose Components:**
- **Frequency Rose**: How often wind blows from each direction
- **Wind Speed Rose**: Average speed from each direction
- **Pollution Rose**: Concentration from each direction
- **Calm**: Wind speed < 0.5 m/s (poor dispersion)

#### B. Temperature Lapse Rate

$$\Gamma = -\frac{dT}{dz} \quad [\text{°C/m or °C/km}]$$

Where $T$ is temperature and $z$ is height.

- **Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR)**: Actual atmospheric lapse rate
- **Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR)**: $9.86$ °C/km (≈ $10$ °C/km)
- **Saturated Adiabatic Lapse Rate (SALR)**: $4$–$6$ °C/km

#### C. Atmospheric Stability

The atmosphere is classified into **6 Pasquill stability classes (A–F)**:

| Class | Stability | Description | Conditions |
|-------|-----------|-------------|------------|
| **A** | Very Unstable | Strong convection, vigorous mixing | Strong insolation, low wind |
| **B** | Moderately Unstable | Moderate mixing | Moderate sun, low wind |
| **C** | Slightly Unstable | Weak mixing | Moderate sun, moderate wind |
| **D** | Neutral | Mechanical turbulence only | Overcast, any wind |
| **E** | Slightly Stable | Inversion onset | Slight insolation, low wind |
| **F** | Very Stable | Strong inversion, poor mixing | Clear night, low wind |

> [!CAUTION]
> **The "A" through "F" Trick (KTU Mnemonic):**
> **A** = Absolutely unstable (sunny + calm)
> **D** = Definitely neutral
> **F** = Frozen fog (clear night + calm) → STABLE

---

### 2.5 Inversion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Inversion = Temperature INCREASES with altitude (opposite of normal).**
> This is critical because it acts as a **"lid"** trapping pollutants below.

#### Types of Inversions

| Type | Cause | Typical Height | Duration |
|------|-------|----------------|----------|
| **Radiation Inversion** | Night-time radiative cooling of ground | 0–500 m | Few hours to overnight |
| **Subsidence Inversion** | Subsiding air mass in high-pressure systems | 1,000–3,000 m | Days |
| **Frontal Inversion** | Warm air overriding cold front | Variable | Days |
| **Advection Inversion** | Warm air moving over cold surface | Variable | Hours to days |
| **Valley Inversion** | Cold air pooling in valleys | Topographic | Overnight |

---

### 2.6 Turbulence and Dispersion

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Engines of Atmospheric Dispersion:**
> 1. **Mechanical Turbulence**: Caused by wind shear, surface roughness, buildings.
> 2. **Convective Turbulence (Thermal)**: Caused by solar heating of ground.

**Eddy Diffusivity (K)**: A measure of dispersion intensity. Higher K → more dilution.

**Plume Types Based on Stability:**

| Stability | Plume Type | Behavior |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Unstable (A, B) | **Looping** | Erratic, sinuous |
| Neutral (D) | **Coning** | Cone-shaped, smooth |
| Stable (E, F) | **Fanning** | Spread vertically only |
| Subsidence Inversion | **Lofting** | Spreads upward only (good!) |
| Ground Inversion | **Fumigation** | Trapped, high ground concentration (BAD!) |
| Inversion + convection | **Trapping** | Bounded between layers |

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CHEAT SHEET — MEMORIZE FOR KTU BOARD EXAM**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $u_z = u_r \left(\dfrac{z}{z_r}\right)^p$ | **Power Law Wind Profile** (u_r = ref speed at z_r) | m/s |
| 2 | $u_z = \dfrac{u_*}{k} \ln\left(\dfrac{z}{z_0}\right)$ | **Logarithmic Wind Profile** (u_* = friction velocity, k = 0.4) | m/s |
| 3 | $H_{eff} = H_s + \Delta H$ | **Effective Stack Height** (H_s = stack, ΔH = plume rise) | m |
| 4 | $\Delta H = \dfrac{v_s \cdot d}{u}\left(1.5 + 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \cdot P \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_s} \cdot d\right)$ | **Holland's Plume Rise Formula** | m |
| 5 | $C(x,y,z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[\exp\left(-\dfrac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\dfrac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$ | **GAUSSIAN PLUME MODEL** (with ground reflection) | g/m³ |
| 6 | $C(x,0,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | **Ground-level centerline concentration** | g/m³ |
| 7 | $\sigma_y = a \cdot x^b$, $\sigma_z = c \cdot x^d$ | **Pasquill-Gifford dispersion coefficients** | m |
| 8 | $X_{max} = \sigma_z \cdot \sqrt{2}$ | **Distance of maximum ground concentration** | m |
| 9 | $C_{max} = \dfrac{2Q}{e \pi u H^2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_z}{\sigma_y}$ | **Maximum ground concentration** | g/m³ |
| 10 | $C(x,y,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | **Ground level concentration (off-axis)** | g/m³ |

**Where:**
- $Q$ = emission rate (g/s)
- $u$ = wind speed (m/s)
- $H$ = effective stack height (m)
- $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ = horizontal & vertical dispersion coefficients (m)
- $x, y, z$ = downwind, crosswind, vertical distances (m)
- $v_s$ = stack gas exit velocity (m/s)
- $d$ = stack diameter (m)
- $P$ = atmospheric pressure (mbar)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference (K)
- $T_s$ = stack gas temperature (K)
- $k$ = von Karman constant = **0.41**
- $e$ = Euler's number ≈ **2.718**

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Application

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this important in industry?**
> - **Power Plants**: Designers use Gaussian plume models to set minimum stack heights (typically 200–300 m for thermal plants).
> - **City Planning**: Helps determine setback distances for industrial zones.
> - **Disaster Management**: Bhopal-type incidents require dispersion models for evacuation planning.
> - **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)**: Mandatory under EPA 1986 for industries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Power Law Wind Profile

> [!NOTE]
> **Background:** This is the most common equation for estimating wind speed at a given height, especially useful when only one reference wind speed is available.

**Statement:** Wind speed at height $z$ can be estimated from a known wind speed at reference height $z_r$ using a power-law exponent $p$.

**Starting Postulate (Empirical):** The wind speed ratio equals the height ratio raised to an exponent $p$:

$$\frac{u_z}{u_r} = \left(\frac{z}{z_r}\right)^p$$

**Step 1:** Rearranging:

$$u_z = u_r \cdot \left(\frac{z}{z_r}\right)^p$$

**Step 2 — Exponent $p$ values (Pasquill):**

| Stability | $p$ |
|-----------|-----|
| A (Very Unstable) | 0.07 |
| B | 0.07 |
| C | 0.10 |
| D (Neutral) | 0.15 |
| E | 0.35 |
| F (Very Stable) | 0.55 |

**Step 3 — Worked Example (KTU Style):**
Given: $u_r = 4$ m/s at $z_r = 10$ m, neutral stability (D), find $u_z$ at $z = 100$ m.

**Solution:**

$$u_{100} = 4 \cdot \left(\frac{100}{10}\right)^{0.15}$$

$$u_{100} = 4 \cdot (10)^{0.15}$$

$$u_{100} = 4 \cdot 1.412 = 5.65 \text{ m/s}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Logarithmic Wind Profile

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the more theoretically sound profile, valid only under neutral conditions (D class).**

**Starting Postulate (Prandtl's Mixing Length Theory):**
The turbulent momentum flux $\tau$ is constant with height near the ground (within the **surface layer**, typically the lowest 10% of the planetary boundary layer).

$$\tau = \rho \cdot K_z \cdot \frac{du}{dz} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 1:** Define friction velocity:

$$u_* = \sqrt{\frac{\tau}{\rho}}$$

**Step 2:** By Prandtl's mixing length hypothesis:

$$K_z = k \cdot u_* \cdot z$$

where $k$ = von Karman constant (≈ 0.41).

**Step 3:** Substitute back:

$$u_*^2 = k \cdot u_* \cdot z \cdot \frac{du}{dz}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$u_* = k \cdot z \cdot \frac{du}{dz}$$

**Step 5:** Separate variables and integrate from $z_0$ to $z$:

$$\int_{z_0}^{z} \frac{du_*}{u_*} = \int_{z_0}^{z} \frac{dz}{k z}$$

**Step 6:** Integration:

$$\ln\left(\frac{u_z}{u_0}\right) = \frac{1}{k} \ln\left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Solving for $u_z$ (where $u_0 = 0$ at $z = z_0$):

$$u_z = \frac{u_*}{k} \ln\left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **$z_0$ is the roughness length** (sand: 0.0001 m, grass: 0.05 m, forest: 1–2 m, urban: 1–10 m).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Gaussian Plume Dispersion Model

> [!CAUTION]
> **This is the most important derivation for KTU 2024 — practice it 3+ times before the exam.**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state conditions ($\partial C/\partial t = 0$)
2. Homogeneous, isotropic turbulence
3. Wind blows in $+x$ direction with mean speed $u$
4. Pollution source is continuous, point, at height $H$
5. No chemical reaction, no deposition, no gravitational settling
6. Coordinate system origin at ground beneath the source

**Step 1 — Starting Point: 3D Advection-Diffusion Equation:**

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_x \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} + K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

**Step 2 — Neglect Downwind Diffusion:**
Turbulent transport in $x$ is much smaller than advection, so $K_x \partial^2 C / \partial x^2 \approx 0$:

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

**Step 3 — Assume Homogeneous Diffusion:**

Let $K_y$ and $K_z$ be constant.

**Step 4 — Solve the Equation Using Gaussian Function:**
The fundamental solution to this PDE is a Gaussian:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{A}{x} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{B_x} - \frac{z^2}{C_x}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Apply Conservation of Mass:**
The total mass flux through any vertical plane $\int \int C \cdot u \, dy \, dz = Q$ (emission rate).

This yields (after detailed integration):

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \frac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Source at Height $H$:**
Shift the $z$-coordinate: substitute $(z - H)$ for $z$:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Account for Ground Reflection:**
The ground is a perfect reflector (no flux through $z = 0$). The "image source" is placed at $z = -H$:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[\exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

**Step 8 — Ground-Level Concentration ($z = 0$):**

$$C(x,y,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Step 9 — Centerline Ground Concentration ($y = 0$):**

$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Holland's Plume Rise Formula

> [!NOTE]
> **Used to find ΔH (plume rise) for buoyancy-dominated plumes from hot stacks.**

**Starting Equation:**

$$\Delta H = \frac{v_s \cdot d}{u} \left(1.5 + 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \cdot P \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_s} \cdot d\right)$$

**Worked Example:**
Given:
- Stack gas exit velocity: $v_s = 20$ m/s
- Stack diameter: $d = 3$ m
- Wind speed: $u = 5$ m/s
- Atmospheric pressure: $P = 1013$ mbar
- $\Delta T = 100$ K, $T_s = 400$ K

**Solution:**

$$\Delta H = \frac{20 \times 3}{5} \left(1.5 + 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \times 1013 \times \frac{100}{400} \times 3\right)$$

$$\Delta H = 12 \left(1.5 + 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \times 1013 \times 0.25 \times 3\right)$$

$$\Delta H = 12 \left(1.5 + 2.04\right) = 12 \times 3.54 = 42.5 \text{ m}$$

If stack height $H_s = 100$ m, then $H_{eff} = 100 + 42.5 = 142.5$ m.

---

### 3.5 KTU Analytical Matrix: Plume Behavior vs. Stability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compare this table for KTU board questions — examiners often ask "Sketch and explain plume patterns."**

| Stability Class | Plume Name | Vertical Spread | Visual | Dilution |
|-----------------|------------|-----------------|--------|----------|
| **A (Unstable)** | Looping | High, irregular | Sinuous coils | Excellent |
| **B (Unstable)** | Looping | Moderate | Wavy | Very good |
| **C** | Coning | Moderate | Cone | Good |
| **D (Neutral)** | Coning | Smooth cone | Conical | Moderate |
| **E (Stable)** | Fanning | Negligible vertical | Horizontal fan | Poor |
| **F (Stable)** | Fanning | None | Ribbon | Very poor |
| Inversion top | Lofting | Upward only | Rising dome | Best |
| Inversion bottom | Fumigation | Downward only | Coning down | Worst |
| Both inversions | Trapping | Bounded | Sandwich | Limited |

---

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Problem: Gaussian Plume

**Given:**
- $Q = 100$ g/s (SO₂ emission)
- $u = 4$ m/s
- $H = 50$ m
- $x = 500$ m (downwind)
- Stability: B (unstable)
- $\sigma_y = 0.22 \cdot x \cdot \tan(T)$ … using Pasquill-Gifford at $x = 500$ m, B class:
  - $\sigma_y \approx 35$ m
  - $\sigma_z \approx 25$ m

**Find:** Ground-level centerline concentration.

**Step 1:** Apply centerline ground concentration formula:

$$C = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$C = \frac{100}{\pi \cdot 4 \cdot 35 \cdot 25} \exp\left(-\frac{50^2}{2 \cdot 25^2}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Compute denominator:

$$\pi \cdot 4 \cdot 35 \cdot 25 = 10995.6$$

**Step 4:** Compute exponential term:

$$-\frac{2500}{2 \cdot 625} = -\frac{2500}{1250} = -2$$

$$e^{-2} = 0.1353$$

**Step 5:** Final:

$$C = \frac{100}{10995.6} \times 0.1353 = 9.09 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1353 = 1.23 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{C = 1230 \text{ μg/m}^3}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Compare to NAAQS:** NAAQS 24-hr SO₂ = 80 μg/m³. This emission **violates** the standard by ~15x. The plant needs taller stack or scrubber.

---

### 3.7 Ambient Air Monitoring Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **NAAQS (CPCB) — 2009 — Major Pollutants:**

| Pollutant | Averaging Period | Concentration | Method |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|--------|
| PM₂.₅ | 24-hr / Annual | 60 / 40 μg/m³ | Gravimetric / β-attenuation |
| PM₁₀ | 24-hr / Annual | 100 / 60 μg/m³ | Gravimetric |
| SO₂ | 24-hr / Annual | 80 / 50 μg/m³ | West-Gaeke (pararosaniline) |
| NO₂ | 24-hr / Annual | 80 / 40 μg/m³ | Jacob & Hochheiser (Saltzman) |
| CO | 8-hr / 1-hr | 4 / 10 mg/m³ | Non-dispersive IR (NDIR) |
| O₃ | 8-hr / 1-hr | 100 / 180 μg/m³ | Chemiluminescence / UV photometry |
| Pb | 24-hr / Annual | 1.0 / 0.5 μg/m³ | AAS / ICP-MS |
| NH₃ | 24-hr / Annual | 400 / 100 μg/m³ | Indophenol blue |
| Benzene | Annual | 5 μg/m³ | Gas chromatography |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Analytical Methods:**
> **"W**est-**G**aeke **G**ives **S**O₂" → SO₂ = West-Gaeke
> **"S**altzman **N**oses **N**O₂" → NO₂ = Saltzman/Jacob-Hochheiser
> **"C**hemiluminescence catches **O**zone" → O₃ = Chemiluminescence

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Format:** 2-mark conceptual, 3-mark explanatory. Aim for crisp answers (60–100 words each).

---

#### **Question A1** (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> **Differentiate between primary and secondary air pollutants. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Primary Pollutants:** These are emitted directly into the atmosphere from identifiable sources in a form that is harmful. They retain their chemical identity after emission.

*Examples:* (i) Carbon monoxide (CO) from vehicle exhaust, (ii) Sulphur dioxide (SO₂) from coal-fired power plants, (iii) Particulate matter (PM₁₀) from construction.

**Secondary Pollutants:** These are not emitted directly; they form in the atmosphere through chemical reactions between primary pollutants and/or natural atmospheric constituents.

*Examples:* (i) Ground-level ozone (O₃) formed by photolysis of NO₂ in presence of VOCs and sunlight, (ii) Photochemical smog, (iii) PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate), (iv) Sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) aerosols from SO₂ oxidation.

| Feature | Primary | Secondary |
|---------|---------|-----------|
| Origin | Direct source | Atmospheric reaction |
| Examples | CO, SO₂, NO, PM | O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄ |
| Control | Source control | Both source and atmospheric control |

---

#### **Question A2** (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
> **What is atmospheric inversion? Explain radiation inversion with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

**Atmospheric Inversion:** A condition in which the normal temperature decrease with height reverses, i.e., temperature **increases** with elevation, creating a stable layer that suppresses vertical mixing and traps pollutants below it.

**Radiation Inversion (Most Common Type):**

**Mechanism:**
1. After sunset, the ground surface loses heat by long-wave (terrestrial) radiation.
2. The air layer in immediate contact with the ground cools first.
3. Cooling progressively extends upward, building a temperature inversion layer.
4. By dawn, the inversion may extend from ground to **100–500 m**.
5. After sunrise, ground heating erodes the inversion from below, often causing **fumigation** with high ground-level concentrations.

**Conditions Favoring Radiation Inversion:**
- Clear skies (no cloud blanket to retain heat)
- Calm or low wind speeds
- Long nights (winter)
- Snow-covered ground (high albedo)

**Significance:** Traps pollutants; often causes winter smog in cities like Delhi, Beijing.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Format:** You must answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** in full.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the various sources and effects of air pollution. Discuss the major air pollution disasters in India and abroad. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the Gaussian Plume Dispersion Model. Derive the expression for ground-level centerline concentration. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Sources, Effects, and Disasters of Air Pollution (7 Marks)**

**Sources of Air Pollution:**

**(i) Natural Sources:**
- **Volcanic eruptions**: Emit SO₂, H₂S, CO₂, dust, ash
- **Forest fires**: CO, CO₂, NOₓ, particulate matter
- **Biological decay**: CH₄, H₂S, NH₃
- **Sea salt spray**: Marine aerosols
- **Pollen and dust storms**: Biogenic particulates
- **Biogenic VOCs**: Isoprene, terpenes from forests

**(ii) Anthropogenic (Man-Made) Sources:**

| Category | Examples | Major Pollutants |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| Stationary | Power plants, refineries, smelters | SO₂, NOₓ, PM, CO₂, Hg |
| Mobile | Automobiles, aircraft, ships | CO, NOₓ, HC, PM, Pb |
| Area | Dry cleaners, gas stations, degreasing | VOCs, CFCs |
| Line | Highways, railways | CO, NOₓ, noise |
| Indoor | Cooking, smoking, building materials | CO, PM, Rn, formaldehyde |

**Effects of Air Pollution:**

**(1) On Human Health:**
- **CO**: Binds hemoglobin (240× more than O₂) → carboxyhemoglobin → tissue hypoxia, headache, death.
- **SO₂**: Causes bronchitis, asthma, aggravates heart disease.
- **NO₂**: Respiratory infections, lung damage; precursor to ozone.
- **PM₂.₅**: Penetrates alveoli → lung cancer, cardiovascular mortality, stroke.
- **O₃**: Chest pain, coughing, reduced lung function.
- **Pb**: CNS damage in children, IQ loss, anemia.

**(2) On Environment:**
- **Acid rain** (pH < 5.6) — damages forests, lakes, monuments
- **Global warming** — CO₂, CH₄, N₂O trap IR radiation
- **Ozone depletion** — CFCs release Cl/Br radicals
- **Photochemical smog** — PAN, O₃ in summer
- **Eutrophication** — NH₃ deposition
- **Material damage** — corrosion of metals, stone (Taj Mahal yellowing)
- **Reduced visibility** — light scattering by PM

**(3) On Vegetation:**
- Stomatal blockage, reduced photosynthesis
- Yield loss 10–20% in industrial zones
- Chlorosis, necrosis

**Major Air Pollution Disasters:**

| Disaster | Year | Pollutant | Deaths/Impact |
|----------|------|-----------|---------------|
| Meuse Valley, Belgium | 1930 | SO₂ + fog | 60 deaths |
| Donora, USA | 1948 | Smog (Zn smelter) | 20 deaths, 6,000 sick |
| London Smog | 1952 | SO₂ + smoke | 4,000+ deaths in 5 days |
| Bhopal, India | 1984 | MIC leak | 3,800+ deaths, 500,000 affected |
| Chernobyl, USSR | 1986 | Radioactive | 30+ direct, long-term cancer |
| Delhi Crisis | 2017 | PM₂.₅ | AQI 999, school closures |
| Kerala Endosulfan | 2000s | Pesticide | Birth defects, cancer |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write *disasters* without specifying the **pollutant type** and **timeline**. Always mention the **year, location, pollutant, and death toll**.

---

**(b) Gaussian Plume Dispersion Model (7 Marks)**

**Definition:** The Gaussian Plume Model is a steady-state dispersion model that describes the concentration of a pollutant downwind from a continuous, elevated point source, assuming that the dispersion follows a normal (Gaussian) distribution in the crosswind ($y$) and vertical ($z$) directions.

**Sketch:**

```
                            y
                            |
                            |
            ___..............|................
       ___/                   |                \___
     /                        |                     \
    |        σ_y ←——————→  u (wind)                |
     \___                    |                    ___/
         \___                |                ___/
             \_______________|________________/
                            | (source)
                            | z
                            |
                          [Stack]
                             |
                          ───H───
                             |
                          ─────── Ground
                             |
                             x
```

**Coordinate System:**
- $x$: downwind distance from source
- $y$: crosswind distance from plume centerline
- $z$: vertical distance from ground
- $H$: effective stack height

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state continuous emission
2. Homogeneous, isotropic turbulence
3. Mean wind speed $u$ in $+x$ direction
4. No chemical reaction or deposition
5. Source at height $H$ above ground
6. Coordinate origin at ground beneath source

**Derivation (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** Advection-Diffusion Equation:

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

**Step 2:** Neglect downwind diffusion ($K_x \approx 0$ since advection dominates).

**Step 3:** Solve PDE with Gaussian trial solution. Applying mass conservation $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} uC\,dy\,dz = Q$ yields:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[\exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

The two exponential terms represent the **real source** at $+H$ and its **image source** at $-H$ (to enforce zero-flux boundary at ground).

**Step 4:** Ground-level ($z = 0$) centerline ($y = 0$) concentration:

$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Dispersion coefficients (Pasquill-Gifford):

$$\sigma_y = a \cdot x^{0.894}$$
$$\sigma_z = c \cdot x^{d}$$

where $a, c, d$ are stability-class dependent (tabulated in textbooks).

**Step 6:** Maximum ground concentration occurs at:

$$x_{max} = \frac{H}{\sigma_z} \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

$$C_{max} = \frac{2Q}{e \pi u H^2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_z}{\sigma_y}$$

**Limitations:**
- Only valid for distances 0.1–10 km
- Cannot handle calm wind conditions
- Assumes flat terrain
- No building downwash correction

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget the **ground reflection term** ($e^{-(z+H)^2/2\sigma_z^2}$). Also, $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ are functions of stability class — must be **tabulated**, not assumed constant.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Alternate]**
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the principles of ambient air quality monitoring. List the CPCB-NAMP monitoring stations and describe the analytical methods for SO₂, NO₂, and PM₂.₅. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Explain atmospheric stability with Pasquill-Gifford classification. With sketches, describe the various plume behaviors (looping, coning, fanning, fumigation, lofting, trapping). (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Principles of Ambient Air Quality Monitoring (7 Marks)**

**Definition:** Ambient air quality monitoring is the **systematic, long-term measurement** of pollutant concentrations in outdoor air to compare with NAAQS, identify trends, and inform the public.

**Objectives:**
1. Determine compliance with NAAQS
2. Identify spatial and temporal patterns
3. Track long-term pollution trends
4. Provide data for epidemiological studies
5. Support emergency response during pollution episodes
6. Validate dispersion models

**Network Design (CPCB-NAMP):**
- **680+ monitoring stations** across India (as of 2024)
- Operated by CPCB, State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs), and NEERI
- Classified as **Industrial**, **Residential**, **Rural**, **Background**

**Site Selection Criteria (IS 5182 Part 14):**
- Height: 3–15 m above ground
- Distance: 50 m from road
- No nearby tall buildings
- Open, well-ventilated
- Represents the area being studied

**Analytical Methods:**

| Pollutant | Standard Method | Principle | Detection Limit |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------|-----------------|
| **SO₂** | West-Gaeke (IS 5182 Part 2) | SO₂ absorbed in **sodium tetrachloromercurate (TCM)** → forms dichlorosulfitomercurate complex. Reacts with **pararosaniline** + formaldehyde → red-violet color. Measured at **548 nm** spectrophotometrically. | 4 μg/m³ |
| **NO₂** | Jacob & Hochheiser (IS 5182 Part 6) | NO₂ absorbed in **sodium hydroxide + sodium arsenite** → forms azo dye. Read at **540 nm** spectrophotometrically. (Modified Saltzman method.) | 9 μg/m³ |
| **PM₂.₅** | Gravimetric (IS 5182 Part 23) | Air drawn through **size-selective inlet** (cuts at 2.5 μm at 16.67 L/min) and collected on **47-mm PTFE or quartz filter**. Weighed before/after on **microbalance** (1 μg sensitivity). | 5 μg/m³ |
| **CO** | Non-Dispersive IR (NDIR) | CO absorbs IR at 4.6 μm; Beer-Lambert law. | 0.5 mg/m³ |
| **O₃** | Chemiluminescence / UV Photometry | O₃ + ethylene → chemiluminescence at 350–600 nm. | 5 μg/m³ |

**Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Stations (CAAQMS):**
- Real-time analyzers
- Telemetry to CPCB server
- Display AQI in real time
- Example: SAFAR (System of Air Quality and Weather Forecasting) by IITM Pune

**Quality Assurance/QC:**
- Calibration with certified gas standards
- Field blanks, duplicates, spiked samples
- Audit by CPCB every 6 months

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students confuse **station types**. NAMP uses **manual stations** (3-hourly sampling, 8-hour analysis). CAAQMS uses **continuous analyzers**. Both are operational in India.

---

**(b) Atmospheric Stability & Plume Behavior (7 Marks)**

**Definition:** Atmospheric stability refers to the atmosphere's tendency to **resist** or **enhance** vertical motion. A parcel of air displaced vertically will either return to its original position (stable), continue moving (unstable), or do neither (neutral).

**Determination Methods:**
1. **Temperature Lapse Rate Method**: Compare ELR with DALR/SALR.
2. **Pasquill Method**: Based on incoming solar radiation (day) or cloud cover (night) and wind speed.
3. **σ_E Method**: Direct measurement of σ_θ (standard deviation of wind direction).
4. **Richardson Number Method**: $R_i = \frac{(g/T)(\partial\theta_v/\partial z)}{(\partial u/\partial z)^2}$

**Pasquill-Gifford Stability Classes:**

| Class | Stability | Solar Insolation (Day) | Cloud Cover (Night) | Wind Speed |
|-------|-----------|----------------------|--------------------|-----------:|
| A | Very Unstable | Strong | — | <2 m/s |
| B | Unstable | Moderate | — | <3 m/s |
| C | Slightly Unstable | Slight | — | <5 m/s |
| D | Neutral | — | Overcast | Any |
| E | Slightly Stable | — | >50% cloud | <3 m/s |
| F | Very Stable | — | Clear | <2 m/s |

**Plume Behavior (With Sketches):**

**(1) Looping Plume** (Unstable, A–B)
- Strong convective turbulence
- Sinuous, looping pattern
- Excellent vertical mixing
- Ground concentrations erratic but generally well-diluted

**(2) Coning Plume** (Neutral, D)
- Symmetric cone shape
- Equal spread in all directions
- $\sigma_y \approx \sigma_z$ proportions
- Moderate dilution

**(3) Fanning Plume** (Stable, E–F)
- Negligible vertical spread
- Spreads horizontally like a ribbon
- Poor vertical mixing
- High concentrations at plume height

**(4) Lofting Plume** (Inversion above source)
- Plume rises freely but cannot descend
- Best case scenario
- No ground impact
- Common in early morning after inversion breaks

**(5) Fumigation Plume** (Inversion below plume)
- Plume trapped above, ground inversion erodes
- Pollutant brought rapidly to ground
- **Worst case** — high ground concentrations
- Common after sunrise in winter

**(6) Trapping Plume** (Inversion above AND below)
- Plume sandwiched between two inversions
- Limited vertical mixing
- Slow horizontal dispersal
- Common in valleys

**Mathematical Link:** Pasquill stability classes determine the dispersion coefficients $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ in the Gaussian plume model. More stable (F) → smaller $\sigma_z$ → higher ground concentrations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Confusing **Fanning** (no vertical spread) with **Fumigation** (vertical spread DOWN to ground). Draw clearly with axis labels.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 TOP 10 WAYS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN THIS MODULE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing primary vs secondary pollutants** — Memorize the 6 criteria pollutants and which are primary/secondary.
> 2. **Forgetting the ground reflection term** in Gaussian model — drop the second exponential, lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong units in plume rise** — $\Delta H$ is in **meters**, $Q$ in **g/s**, $u$ in **m/s**.
> 4. **Stating inversion wrongly** — Inversion = temperature INCREASES with altitude. Many write it decreases.
> 5. **Skipping the assumptions** of Gaussian model — Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for assumptions.
> 6. **Mixing up power-law $p$ and exponent $b$** in $\sigma$ formulas.
> 7. **Drawing plume shapes without labeling axes** $x, y, z$ and stack height.
> 8. **Forgetting Pasquill-Gifford table** — Examiner expects you to refer to specific class.
> 9. **Not mentioning CPCB-NAAQS values** — Always cite the standard while comparing.
> 10. **Skipping the derivation** — Even if you write the formula, the **step-by-step derivation** carries the marks.

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Final Night Before Exam**

#### ✅ Sources & Effects
- [ ] Natural: Volcanic, forest fires, decay, sea salt, biogenic VOCs
- [ ] Anthropogenic: Stationary, mobile, area, line, indoor
- [ ] 6 Criteria Pollutants: CO, SO₂, NO₂, PM, O₃, Pb
- [ ] Major Disasters: Bhopal 1984, London 1952, Donora 1948

#### ✅ Classification of Pollutants
- [ ] Primary (direct emission): CO, SO₂, NO, PM
- [ ] Secondary (atmospheric formation): O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄
- [ ] PM₁₀ ≤ 10 μm, PM₂.₅ ≤ 2.5 μm, Ultrafine ≤ 0.1 μm

#### ✅ Atmospheric Transport
- [ ] Wind: Speed (dilution) + Direction (transport)
- [ ] Wind Rose: Frequency, speed, pollution roses
- [ ] Power Law: $u_z = u_r(z/z_r)^p$
- [ ] Log Law: $u_z = (u_*/k)\ln(z/z_0)$, k = 0.41

#### ✅ Atmospheric Stability
- [ ] 6 Pasquill Classes: A (very unstable) → F (very stable)
- [ ] DALR = 9.86 °C/km; SALR = 4–6 °C/km
- [ ] Class D = Neutral (default for many models)

#### ✅ Inversion
- [ ] Temperature increases with altitude
- [ ] 5 types: Radiation, Subsidence, Frontal, Advection, Valley
- [ ] Radiation inversion: Night cooling, 0–500 m, erodes at sunrise

#### ✅ Turbulence & Plume Behavior
- [ ] Mechanical + Convective = Turbulence
- [ ] Looping (A-B), Coning (D), Fanning (E-F)
- [ ] Lofting (inversion above), Fumigation (inversion below), Trapping (both)

#### ✅ Gaussian Plume Model
- [ ] Formula with both real + image source terms
- [ ] Ground centerline: $C = (Q/\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z) e^{-H^2/2\sigma_z^2}$
- [ ] $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ from Pasquill-Gifford curves
- [ ] Assumptions: steady, homogeneous, no chemistry, flat terrain
- [ ] Holland's plume rise for $\Delta H$

#### ✅ Ambient Air Monitoring
- [ ] 680+ NAMP stations in India (CPCB)
- [ ] SO₂ = West-Gaeke; NO₂ = Saltzman/Jacob-Hochheiser
- [ ] PM = Gravimetric; CO = NDIR; O₃ = Chemiluminescence
- [ ] 8 NAAQS pollutants with limits

#### ✅ Stack Emission Monitoring
- [ ] Isokinetic sampling (probe velocity = stack gas velocity)
- [ ] Components: Probe → Filter → Impinger → Pump → Meter
- [ ] CEMS = Continuous Emission Monitoring System
- [ ] Parameters: PM, SO₂, NOₓ, CO, opacity, flow, temperature

#### ✅ NAAQS Key Values (Memorize)
- [ ] PM₂.₅: 60 μg/m³ (24-hr); 40 μg/m³ (annual)
- [ ] PM₁₀: 100 μg/m³ (24-hr); 60 μg/m³ (annual)
- [ ] SO₂: 80 μg/m³ (24-hr); 50 μg/m³ (annual)
- [ ] NO₂: 80 μg/m³ (24-hr); 40 μg/m³ (annual)
- [ ] CO: 4 mg/m³ (8-hr); 10 mg/m³ (1-hr)
- [ ] O₃: 100 μg/m³ (8-hr); 180 μg/m³ (1-hr)
- [ ] Pb: 1.0 μg/m³ (24-hr); 0.5 μg/m³ (annual)
- [ ] NH₃: 400 μg/m³ (24-hr); 100 μg/m³ (annual)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Particulate Matter (PM) Control?

Particulate Matter (PM) control refers to the systematic removal of solid or liquid suspended particles from industrial gas streams (flue gases, exhaust air, process emissions) before they are discharged into the atmosphere. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *PCSFT602*, this is the cornerstone of air pollution control engineering, particularly in thermal power plants, cement kilns, steel plants, incinerators, and chemical process industries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Particulate control equipment (also termed *Gas-Solid Separation Devices* or *Air Pollution Control Devices*) are engineered systems designed to separate and capture airborne particulates from a gas stream using physical, mechanical, electrostatic, or fluid-dynamic principles while allowing the cleaned gas to pass through.

The five major categories mandated in the KTU Module 1 syllabus are:

| S.No | Equipment | Operating Principle |
|------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Gravity Settlers | Gravitational sedimentation |
| 2 | Cyclones | Centrifugal / Inertial separation |
| 3 | Wet Scrubbers | Liquid droplet scrubbing |
| 4 | Fabric Filters | Surface filtration |
| 5 | Electrostatic Precipitators | Electrostatic attraction |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are walking in a dusty construction site. To protect yourself, you could: **(a)** stand still and let dust slowly settle at your feet (Gravity Settler), **(b)** spin around rapidly so dust flies outward into a wall (Cyclone), **(c)** spray water from a hose to wash dust out of the air (Wet Scrubber), **(d)** wear a cloth mask (Fabric Filter), or **(e)** use a statically charged balloon to attract dust (Electrostatic Precipitator). Each method has a different efficiency, cost, and applicability.

### 1.3 Why Study PM Control?

- **Health Impact:** PM₂.₅ (particles < 2.5 μm) penetrates deep into human lungs, causing respiratory and cardiovascular diseases.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** The **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** under the CPCB mandate PM₁₀ ≤ 60 μg/m³ and PM₂.₅ ≤ 40 μg/m³ (24-hour average, India).
- **Industrial Necessity:** Industries cannot legally emit raw particulate-laden streams.
- **Recovery Value:** Captured particulates (fly ash, cement dust, metal oxides) are often recyclable.

### 1.4 Particle Size Classification

The selection of control equipment is critically dependent on particle aerodynamic diameter:

- **Coarse Particles:** > 10 μm — easily removed by simple devices.
- **Fine Particles:** 1–10 μm — require moderate-efficiency equipment.
- **Ultrafine Particles:** < 1 μm — require high-efficiency devices (ESPs, HEPA fabric filters).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Physical Constant to Memorize:** The terminal settling velocity under Stokes' regime is governed by **μ_air = 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s** at STP, and **g = 9.81 m/s²**.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Gravity Settlers (Settling Chambers)

**Working Principle:** A large empty chamber reduces the gas velocity so that particles, under gravity, settle to the floor before the gas exits. This is the **simplest and cheapest** PM control device.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. Dust-laden gas enters a horizontal chamber of large cross-section.
2. Cross-sectional area increases → gas velocity decreases.
3. Particles settle under gravity at their terminal settling velocity (v_t).
4. Cleaned gas exits from the far end; collected dust is removed from hoppers below.

**Design Criteria:**
- Residence time of gas (t_g) ≥ Time required for particle to settle (t_s).
- Chamber length: **L = v_g × H / v_t**, where H is chamber height.

**Limitations:**
- Very large floor space required.
- Inefficient for particles < 50 μm.
- Typical efficiency: **20–60%** for particles > 50 μm.

### 2.2 Cyclones

**Working Principle:** Tangential entry of gas creates a **spiral vortex** inside a cylindrical-conical body. Centrifugal force (much stronger than gravity — up to **500g to 5000g**) flings particles to the wall, where they slide down into the hopper.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Gas enters tangentially through the inlet duct.
2. Vortex motion established inside the cylindrical body.
3. Larger particles (by inertia) strike the wall first.
4. Particles slide down the conical wall into the dust hopper.
5. Cleaned gas spirals upward through the central vortex finder and exits at top.

**Types:** High-efficiency (small diameter), High-throughput (large diameter), Stairmand, Stairmand high-efficiency, Swift design.

**The Cut Diameter (d₅₀):** The particle size collected with **50% efficiency** is the **most critical KTU parameter**:

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu B}{2 \pi N_e \rho_p v_i}}$$

where B = inlet width, N_e = effective number of turns (~5), ρ_p = particle density, v_i = inlet velocity.

### 2.3 Wet Scrubbers

**Working Principle:** The dirty gas stream is brought into contact with a **liquid spray** (usually water). Particles are captured by the droplets through **impaction, interception, and diffusion** mechanisms and are then drained as slurry.

**Major Types (KTU syllabus focus):**
- **Spray Tower:** Low-energy, low-pressure spray nozzles.
- **Venturi Scrubber:** High-velocity gas through a throat atomizes liquid — highest efficiency.
- **Packed Bed Scrubber:** Gas passes through irrigated packing material.
- **Cyclonic Spray Scrubber:** Combines centrifugal + spray mechanisms.

**Capture Mechanisms:**
- **Inertial Impaction:** Dominant for particles > 1 μm.
- **Brownian Diffusion:** Dominant for particles < 0.1 μm.
- **Interception:** For particles 0.1–1 μm.

**Scrubber Efficiency** depends on the **Inertial Impaction Number (N_I)**:

$$N_I = \dfrac{d_p^2 \, \rho_p \, v \, C}{18 \, \mu \, d_d}$$

where d_p = particle diameter, d_d = droplet diameter, C = Cunningham slip correction factor.

### 2.4 Fabric Filters (Baghouses)

**Working Principle:** Dust-laden gas is forced through a **porous fabric** (woven or felt). Particles are trapped on the fabric surface, forming a **filter cake** that progressively improves filtration efficiency. Periodic cleaning restores permeability.

**Operational Cycle:**
1. **Filtration Phase:** Gas passes through fabric; dust accumulates as cake.
2. **Cleaning Phase:** Mechanical shaking, reverse air, or pulse-jet removes cake.
3. **Dust Disposal:** Cake falls into hopper for collection.

**Cleaning Mechanisms:**
- Mechanical Shaking (oldest, low energy)
- Reverse Air (gentle, suited for delicate fabrics)
- Pulse-Jet (most common, online cleaning)

**Key Design Parameters:**
- **Air-to-Cloth Ratio (A/C):** Volume of gas per unit fabric area per minute, typically **0.5–2.5 m³/min/m²**.
- **Pressure Drop:** 0.5–1.5 kPa (clean) up to 2.5 kPa (dirty).
- **Efficiency:** Often exceeds **99.9%** even for sub-micron particles.

### 2.5 Electrostatic Precipitators (ESPs)

**Working Principle:** Particles are **electrically charged** by a high-voltage **corona discharge** (negative wire electrodes) and then migrate to oppositely charged **collecting plates** (grounded anodes). Collected dust is rapped off periodically.

**Two Main Configurations:**
- **Single-Stage (Cottrell):** Charging and collection in same zone. Industrial use.
- **Two-Stage:** Charging in first zone, collection in second. Used in air conditioning.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Discharge electrodes emit corona (≈ −30 to −70 kV DC).
2. Gas ions attach to particles, giving them negative charge.
3. Charged particles migrate to grounded collection plates.
4. Collected dust layer is rapped off into hoppers.
5. Cleaned gas exits the precipitator.

**Critical Limitation: Particle Resistivity (ρ).**
- **Low resistivity (< 10⁴ Ω·cm):** Particles re-entrain easily.
- **Optimum (10⁴ – 2 × 10¹⁰ Ω·cm):** Easy to collect.
- **High resistivity (> 10¹⁰ Ω·cm):** Back corona reduces efficiency.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---------|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes) | $v_t = \dfrac{g(\rho_p - \rho_g) d_p^2}{18\mu}$ | g = 9.81 m/s², ρ in kg/m³, d_p in m, μ in Pa·s | Gravity Settlers |
| Settling Chamber Length | $L = \dfrac{v_g \cdot H}{v_t}$ | L, H in m; v in m/s | Gravity Settlers |
| Cyclone Cut Diameter | $d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9\mu B}{2\pi N_e \rho_p v_i}}$ | B in m; N_e ≈ 5 | Cyclones |
| Impaction Parameter | $N_I = \dfrac{d_p^2 \rho_p v C}{18\mu d_d}$ | C = Cunningham factor | Wet Scrubbers |
| Pressure Drop (Venturi) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{v_{th}^2 \rho_L L/G}{2}$ | ρ_L = liquid density | Wet Scrubbers |
| Deutsch-Anderson (ESP) | $\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{A \, w_e}{Q}\right)$ | A = m²; w_e = m/s; Q = m³/s | ESP Efficiency |
| Migration Velocity | $w_e = \dfrac{0.118 \times 10^{-3} \, k \, E_c \, E_p}{\mu}$ | k = dielectric constant | ESP Physics |
| Air-to-Cloth Ratio | $A/C = \dfrac{Q}{A_{cloth}}$ | m³/min per m² | Fabric Filters |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real plant design, these formulas are used by environmental consultants to size stack heights, predict outlet emissions, and ensure compliance with PCB consent conditions. The Deutsch-Anderson equation alone decides whether an ESP will satisfy the PM emission limit of 30 mg/Nm³ in modern Indian thermal plants.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Terminal Settling Velocity from Stokes' Law (Used in Gravity Settlers)

This derivation shows why gravity settlers fail for fine particles — the velocity scales with **d²**, making small particles settle extremely slowly.

We begin with a free-body diagram on a single spherical particle falling under gravity in a quiescent gas:

$$\sum F_z = F_{gravity} - F_{buoyancy} - F_{drag} = m_p \dfrac{dv}{dt}$$

For steady-state (terminal) settling, the acceleration term equals zero:

$$F_{gravity} = F_{buoyancy} + F_{drag}$$

$$\rho_p \, V_p \, g = \rho_g \, V_p \, g + C_D \, A_p \, \dfrac{\rho_g \, v_t^2}{2}$$

The volume of a sphere is $V_p = \pi d_p^3 / 6$ and the projected area is $A_p = \pi d_p^2 / 4$. Substituting:

$$\rho_p \, \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \, g = \rho_g \, \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \, g + C_D \, \dfrac{\pi d_p^2}{4} \, \dfrac{\rho_g \, v_t^2}{2}$$

Divide every term by $(\pi d_p^3 / 6) \cdot g$:

$$\rho_p = \rho_g + \dfrac{3 C_D \rho_g v_t^2}{4 d_p g}$$

Rearranging for $v_t^2$:

$$v_t^2 = \dfrac{4 g d_p (\rho_p - \rho_g)}{3 C_D \rho_g}$$

For the laminar (viscous) regime, Re_p < 0.1 and the drag coefficient simplifies to $C_D = 24/Re_p$. Because $Re_p = \rho_g v_t d_p / \mu$, substituting $C_D$:

$$C_D = \dfrac{24\mu}{\rho_g v_t d_p}$$

Insert into the expression for $v_t^2$:

$$v_t^2 = \dfrac{4 g d_p (\rho_p - \rho_g)}{3 \rho_g} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_g v_t d_p}{24\mu}$$

$$v_t^2 = \dfrac{g d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) v_t}{18\mu}$$

Dividing both sides by $v_t$:

$$\boxed{v_t = \dfrac{g (\rho_p - \rho_g) d_p^2}{18\mu}}$$

This is the **Stokes terminal settling velocity**. Since $v_t \propto d_p^2$, decreasing the particle size from 10 μm to 1 μm reduces the settling velocity by a factor of **100** — explaining why gravity settlers are useless for fine dust.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cyclone Cut Diameter ($d_{50}$)

In a cyclone, the gas enters at velocity $v_i$ through an inlet of width $B$ and height $H$. It spirals with effective number of turns $N_e$ (typically 5) before exiting. The radial distance traveled by a particle before gas exit is $R = N_e \pi B$.

For a particle to reach the wall by centrifugal action, its radial velocity $v_r$ must equal its terminal radial settling velocity in the centrifugal field. In a cyclone, the acceleration is $v_\theta^2 / r$, replacing $g$ in Stokes' law:

$$v_r = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) v_\theta^2}{18 \mu r}$$

Equating the radial drift distance to the gas residence distance gives the cut diameter when collection efficiency = 50%:

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu B}{2 \pi N_e \rho_p v_i}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Deutsch-Anderson Equation for ESP Efficiency

This is the **single most important equation for ESP design** in KTU Module 1.

Consider an ESP section of length L (gas flow direction) and height H (perpendicular to plates). The total collection plate area is $A = 2 \times N \times L \times H$ (factor 2 because gas flows between two plates).

A particle of charge q experiences a Coulomb force $F = q E_c$ toward the collection plate. The drag force balances this, defining the **migration (drift) velocity** $w_e$:

$$q E_c = 3 \pi \mu d_p w_e \quad \Rightarrow \quad w_e = \dfrac{q E_c}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$$

Now consider a thin slice of gas at distance y from the collection plate. Let $n(y)$ be the particle number density. As the gas advances a distance $dx$, the particles in this slice move toward the plate at $w_e$:

$$\dfrac{dn}{dx} = -\dfrac{w_e}{v_g} \cdot \dfrac{n}{S}$$

where S is the spacing between plates. Integrating:

$$\dfrac{n(y)}{n_0} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{w_e L}{v_g S}\right)$$

Total collection efficiency $\eta$ for the section is found by averaging over all particles entering at various $y$ from 0 to $S$. The gas velocity profile is assumed uniform:

$$\eta = \dfrac{1}{S}\int_0^S \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{w_e L}{v_g S}\right)\right] dy = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{w_e L}{v_g S}\right)$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $H$:

$$\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{w_e \, L H}{v_g \, S H}\right)$$

Note that $v_g \cdot S \cdot H = Q$ (volumetric gas flow rate), and $L \cdot H$ is the plate area per wire-duct. Summing over all wire-ducts:

$$\boxed{\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{A \, w_e}{Q}\right)}$$

This is the **Deutsch-Anderson Equation**. It tells us that ESP efficiency depends **exponentially** on the specific collection area (A/Q).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Hot-Topic)

| Equipment | Particle Size Range (μm) | Typical Efficiency | Pressure Drop (Pa) | Operating Cost | Water Use | Recovers Product? |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------|-----------|-------------------|
| Gravity Settler | > 50 | 20–60% | 50–150 | Very Low | None | Sometimes |
| Cyclone | 5–200 | 70–95% | 500–1500 | Low | None | Yes |
| Wet Scrubber | 0.5–50 | 80–99% | 500–4000 | High (pumping) | High | No (slurry) |
| Fabric Filter | 0.1–100 | 99–99.9%+ | 1000–2500 | Medium | None | Yes (dry) |
| ESP | 0.01–50 | 95–99.9%+ | 100–250 | Medium-Low | None | Yes (dry) |
