## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a cyclone separator. What is meant by the "cut diameter" (d₅₀) of a cyclone?**

**Model Answer:**
A cyclone separator is a dry inertial PM control device that removes particulate matter from a gas stream using centrifugal force generated by a tangential inlet. The gas spirals inside a cylindrical-conical body, throwing particles to the wall by inertia, where they slide down into a hopper.

The **cut diameter (d₅₀)** is the particle diameter at which the cyclone achieves exactly **50% collection efficiency**. It is the most important design parameter. Smaller d₅₀ indicates higher cyclone efficiency. Mathematically:

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu B}{2 \pi N_e \rho_p v_i}}$$

(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark for d₅₀ explanation + 1 mark for equation)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State Stokes' Law for terminal settling velocity. Why are gravity settlers inefficient for sub-micron particles?**

**Model Answer:**
Stokes' Law gives the terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle in the laminar regime:

$$v_t = \dfrac{g(\rho_p - \rho_g) d_p^2}{18\mu}$$

Since $v_t \propto d_p^2$, reducing particle size from 10 μm to 1 μm reduces settling velocity by a factor of 100. Sub-micron particles thus settle negligibly, requiring impractically long chambers. Additionally, Brownian motion and gas convection currents keep fine particles suspended indefinitely, making gravity settlers unsuitable for fine PM control.

(3 marks: 1.5 marks formula + 1.5 marks explanation)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently **(a)** confuse single-stage with two-stage ESPs, **(b)** forget units in Deutsch-Anderson equation, **(c)** write $w_e$ as weight instead of migration velocity, and **(d)** skip particle resistivity discussion. Each omission typically costs **2–3 marks**.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the working principle of an Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) with a neat sketch. Derive the Deutsch-Anderson equation for ESP efficiency. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
- A high negative DC voltage (~30–70 kV) is applied between thin **discharge electrodes (wires)** and grounded **collecting plates**.
- A **corona discharge** forms around the wires, emitting electrons and negative gas ions.
- These ions attach to passing dust particles, giving them a net negative charge.
- The negatively charged particles migrate toward the positively grounded collection plates under the influence of the electric field.
- Particles deposit as a layer on the plates. Periodic **rapping** knocks the dust layer into hoppers below.
- Cleaned gas exits the ESP outlet.

**Derivation of Deutsch-Anderson Equation (3 marks):**

The migration (drift) velocity of a charged particle is:

$$w_e = \dfrac{q E_c}{3\pi \mu d_p}$$

For an ESP section with plate area A, gas flow rate Q, the fraction of particles remaining after one pass:

$$\dfrac{dn}{n} = -\dfrac{w_e}{Q} \cdot dA$$

Integrating from inlet to outlet:

$$\int_{n_0}^{n} \dfrac{dn}{n} = -\dfrac{w_e}{Q}\int_0^A dA$$

$$\ln\dfrac{n}{n_0} = -\dfrac{w_e A}{Q}$$

Since collection efficiency $\eta = 1 - n/n_0$:

$$\boxed{\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{A \, w_e}{Q}\right)}$$

---

**(b) Discuss the effect of particle resistivity on ESP performance. Compare the relative advantages of ESPs over fabric filters. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Particle Resistivity Effects (3.5 marks):**

Particle resistivity (ρ, in Ω·cm) is the resistance of a particle layer 1 cm thick to electrical current flow. It critically affects ESP performance:

- **Low resistivity (< 10⁴ Ω·cm):** Particles arrive at collection plates but immediately lose their charge and re-entrain into the gas stream. Efficiency drops.

- **Optimum resistivity (10⁴ – 2 × 10¹⁰ Ω·cm):** Particles hold their charge long enough to be firmly collected. Maximum efficiency.

- **High resistivity (> 10¹⁰ Ω·cm):** The particle layer does not conduct charge to the plate. Charge accumulates, creating a local **back corona** — positive ions neutralize incoming negative ions, drastically reducing efficiency and sparking.

Conditioning agents (SO₃, NH₃, water spray) are used to bring high-resistivity fly ash into the optimum range.

**ESP vs Fabric Filter Comparison (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | ESP | Fabric Filter |
|-----------|-----|---------------|
| Pressure Drop | Very Low (100–250 Pa) | High (1000–2500 Pa) |
| Fan Power | Low | High |
| Temperature Limit | Up to 400–500°C | Limited by fabric (~200°C) |
| Maintenance | Less frequent | Bag replacement |
| Particle Size | Best for fine PM | All sizes (especially fine) |
| Wet/Dry Operation | Dry | Dry |
| Capital Cost | Higher for very high efficiency | Lower for high efficiency |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Past Paper Pattern]**

**(a) Explain the working principle of a fabric filter (baghouse) with a neat sketch. Discuss the various cleaning mechanisms used. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (3.5 marks):**
A fabric filter uses a **porous fabric** (woven or felt) supported on a wire cage. Dust-laden gas passes through the fabric, and particles are captured on the upstream surface. As particles accumulate, they form a **filter cake** that progressively enhances collection efficiency (often > 99.9%). When pressure drop becomes excessive (~1.5–2.5 kPa), the cake is removed by cleaning, and the cycle repeats.

**Cleaning Mechanisms (3.5 marks):**

1. **Mechanical Shaking (Shake-Deflate):** The bag cage is shaken vigorously using a motor-cam mechanism. Cake detaches and falls. Offline cleaning (filtration stops during shaking).

2. **Reverse Air:** Clean air is blown backwards through the fabric, gently dislodging the cake. Suitable for delicate fabrics like fiberglass. Mostly offline.

3. **Pulse-Jet Cleaning (Most Common):** Compressed air (0.4–0.6 MPa) is injected through a venturi nozzle into the top of the bag for 50–100 ms. The shock wave travels down the bag, flexing it and breaking off the cake. Online cleaning (filtration continues in adjacent compartments).

The choice of cleaning depends on fabric type, gas temperature, dust characteristics, and required uptime.

---

**(b) Explain the working of a venturi scrubber with a neat sketch. List its advantages and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working (4 marks):**
A venturi scrubber consists of three sections: (1) converging section, (2) throat, (3) diverging section. Dirty gas enters the converging section where its velocity increases. At the throat (velocity typically 60–180 m/s), liquid is injected through sprays. The high-velocity gas shears the liquid into fine droplets (10–50 μm). Particles in the gas impact these droplets and are captured by **inertial impaction**. In the diverging section, velocity decreases and the droplets (now carrying dust) agglomerate. A downstream cyclonic separator or mist eliminator removes the droplets from the gas stream. The dust-laden slurry is collected and sent to wastewater treatment.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- Very high efficiency (> 99%) even for sub-micron particles.
- Compact equipment, small footprint.
- Can handle hot, corrosive, or explosive dusts safely.
- Simultaneous removal of gases (SO₂, HCl) and particulates.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- High water consumption; wastewater disposal problem.
- High pressure drop (1000–5000 Pa) means high fan power.
- Corrosion of internal parts.
- Cannot operate below freezing without anti-freeze additives.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Gravity Settlers** rely on $v_t \propto d_p^2$ — useless for fine dust; use them as **pre-cleaners** upstream of more efficient devices.
- [ ] **Cyclones** give the best ROI for **5–50 μm** dust; d₅₀ depends on $\sqrt{1/v_i}$ — higher inlet velocity reduces d₅₀ (but limited by erosion).
- [ ] **Wet Scrubbers** use the impaction parameter $N_I$; venturi scrubbers achieve 99%+ efficiency at the cost of high pressure drop.
- [ ] **Fabric Filters** depend on **filter cake formation**; air-to-cloth ratio and pressure drop are the controlling parameters.
- [ ] **ESPs** follow the **Deutsch-Anderson equation**: $\eta = 1 - \exp(-A w_e / Q)$. Efficiency depends on specific collection area (A/Q).
- [ ] **Particle Resistivity** is the single biggest variable affecting ESP performance; conditioning with SO₃ is a common fix for high-resistivity fly ash.
- [ ] **Two-stage ESPs** are used in ventilation; **single-stage Cottrell ESPs** are used in industrial dust collection.
- [ ] **Pulse-jet** is the modern fabric filter cleaning standard; allows online cleaning.
- [ ] Always report **efficiencies** at a stated particle size — efficiency alone is meaningless.
- [ ] Conversion factor: 1 mg/Nm³ = 1 ppm assumption holds only for PM at standard conditions.
- [ ] KTU expects you to know **both qualitative sketches AND quantitative equations** for each device.
- [ ] The **Cunningham slip correction factor (C)** must be included in Stokes' law when $d_p < 1$ μm.
- [ ] CPCB 2024 emission standards for thermal power plants: PM ≤ 30 mg/Nm³ — achievable only by ESP + baghouse combinations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gaseous Control Methods - An Overview of Absorption, Adsorption and Combustion Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT602 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND TOXIC RISKS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Gaseous Control Methods - An Overview of Absorption, Adsorption and Combustion Methods ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Definition and Scope **Gaseous Control Methods** are the set of unit operations used in Environmental Engineering to capture, neutralize, or destroy airborne pollutants (gaseous and vapor-phase contaminants) emitted from industrial stacks, process vents, and ambient air streams. The three principal categories covered in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus are **Absorption (Wet Scrubbing)**, **Adsorption (Solid-Surface Contact)**, and **Combustion (Thermal / Catalytic Oxidation)**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (PCSFT602 – Module 1):** > *"An overview of Absorption, Adsorption and Combustion methods for the control of gaseous pollutants – principles, equipment, design parameters, and selection criteria."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition | Method | One-Line Intuition | Real-World Analogy | |---|---|---| | **Absorption** | Gas gets *dissolved* into a liquid | Like opening a fizzy soda bottle — CO₂ leaves the liquid; in absorption, a *pollutant* gas **enters** the liquid (scrubbing solution) | | **Adsorption** | Gas molecules *stick* to a solid surface | Like the way silica-gel packets inside a new shoe-box *trap* moisture from the surrounding air | | **Combustion** | Burn the pollutant into harmless products | Like burning a mosquito coil — the smoke (VOCs) is oxidized into CO₂ + H₂O at high temperature | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Distinction (Board-Favourite):** > - **Absorption** → Pollutant is *absorbed into the bulk* of a liquid (penetration into volume). > - **Adsorption** → Pollutant is *adsorbed onto the surface* of a solid (surface accumulation only). > - The mnemonic: **"Absorption = A lake swallows you whole; Adsorption = A wall stops you on the surface."** ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Gaseous Control The following engineering quantities are universally used for designing all three systems: - **Volumetric flow rate (Q)** measured in **m³/s** or **ACMH** (Actual Cubic Meters per Hour) - **Gas-phase concentration (Y or C_g)** — mol/m³ or ppm (parts per million by volume) - **Liquid-phase concentration (X or C_l)** — mol/m³ or mass fraction - **Pressure (P)** in **Pa** (or kPa), Temperature (T) in **K** - **Universal Gas Constant R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** - **Standard conditions: 1 atm, 25 °C (298 K)** unless otherwise stated ### 1.4 When is Each Method Used? > [!TIP] > **Quick Selection Rule (for board exams):** > - Use **Absorption** for **water-soluble gases** (SO₂, HCl, NH₃, HF). > - Use **Adsorption** for **dilute, hazardous, or recovery-worthy organics** (VOCs, mercury vapour, odours). > - Use **Combustion** for **organic vapours with high calorific value** (solvent fumes, hydrocarbons, CO) — when recovery is not economical. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 ABSORPTION (Wet Scrubbing) — Principles Absorption is a **mass-transfer operation** in which one or more soluble components of a gas mixture are dissolved into a *non-volatile absorbent liquid*. The driving force is the **concentration gradient** between the gas and the liquid at the interface. **Mechanism – Two-Film Theory (Whitman, 1923):** 1. Pollutant gas diffuses from bulk gas → through **gas film** → to gas-liquid interface. 2. At the interface, gas dissolves into the liquid (assumed equilibrium). 3. Dissolved species diffuses through **liquid film** → into bulk liquid. The **overall resistance** is the sum of the two film resistances: $$ \frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L} $$ where: - $K_G$ = overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient (mol/m²·s·Pa) - $k_G$ = individual gas-film coefficient - $k_L$ = individual liquid-film coefficient - $m$ = slope of equilibrium line (Henry's constant, dimensionless) **Equipment:** Packed column, Plate column, Venturi scrubber, Spray tower. ### 2.2 ADSORPTION — Principles Adsorption is a **surface phenomenon** where molecules of gas, vapour, or dissolved solute **accumulate on the surface of a solid (adsorbent)**. The solid is typically **porous** with very high internal surface area. **Two Physical Forms:** - **Physisorption** – Weak van der Waals forces; reversible; low temperature. - **Chemisorption** – Chemical bonds; often irreversible; high temperature. **Common Adsorbents:** Activated Carbon, Silica Gel, Activated Alumina, Zeolites (Molecular Sieves). ### 2.3 COMBUSTION (Thermal Oxidation) — Principles Combustion destroys gaseous organic pollutants by **high-temperature oxidation** to CO₂, H₂O, SO₂, and other oxidized products. It is governed by the **3-T Rule**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three T's of Combustion (often asked for 3 marks):** > 1. **T**emperature — typically **760 °C – 980 °C** (or up to 1200 °C for toxic halogenated VOCs). > 2. **T**ime (Residence Time) — **0.5 to 2 seconds** in the combustion chamber. > 3. **T**urbulence — Good mixing ensures complete combustion (ΔP, high velocity). **Two Equipment Types:** - **Thermal Incinerator** (afterburner) – Direct flame, high T, no catalyst. - **Catalytic Oxidizer** – Lower T (~300–500 °C), uses Pt/Pd catalyst, lower fuel cost. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master Cheat Sheet — Print and memorize.** | # | Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Henry's Law | $y_A = m \, x_A$ | $m$ = Henry's constant (dimensionless) | | 2 | Gas Solubility (Mol Fraction form) | $P_A = H \, x_A$ | $H$ = Henry's constant in **atm** | | 3 | Molar Flux (Two-Film) | $N_A = K_G \, (P - P^{*}_A)$ | mol / (m²·s) | | 4 | Counter-current Operating Line | $Y_{n+1} - Y^{*} = \frac{L}{G}\,(X_n - X_{n+1})$ | L = liquid flow (mol/s), G = gas flow (mol/s) | | 5 | Minimum L/G Ratio | $\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X^{*}_1 - X_2}$ | Used to size solvent flow | | 6 | Height of Packing | $Z = H_{OG} \, N_{OG}$ | $H_{OG}$ = HTU (m), $N_{OG}$ = NTU | | 7 | NTU (Overall Gas) | $N_{OG} = \int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \frac{dY}{Y - Y^{*}}$ | Dimensionless | | 8 | HTU Relation | $H_{OG} = H_G + m \, \frac{G}{L} \, H_L$ | Wetted-wall derived | | 9 | Freundlich Isotherm | $\frac{x}{m} = K_F \, C_e^{1/n}$ | $x$ = mass adsorbed, $m$ = mass of adsorbent | | 10 | Langmuir Isotherm | $q = \frac{q_m \, b \, P}{1 + b \, P}$ | $q_m$ = max capacity, $b$ = affinity | | 11 | BET Isotherm | $\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{(C-1)}{V_m C}\cdot\frac{P}{P_0}$ | Multi-layer adsorption | | 12 | Stoichiometric O₂ | $O_2^{stoich} = a + \frac{b}{2} - c$ | for CₐHᵦOᵧNᵨSᵩ | | 13 | Excess Air % | $EA\% = \frac{O_2^{actual} - O_2^{stoich}}{O_2^{stoich}} \times 100$ | Standard 10–25 % | | 14 | Combustion Residence Time | $\tau = \frac{V_{chamber}}{Q_{gas}}$ | seconds | | 15 | Kremser Equation | $\frac{Y_{N+1} - Y^{*}}{Y_1 - Y^{*}} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$ | A = L/(mG) | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in the design of **Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD)** units, **volatile organic compound (VOC) recovery systems**, and **hazardous waste incinerators** — all covered under the Environmental Protection Act of India. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Operating Line for a Counter-Current Absorber **Given:** A gas stream containing a soluble pollutant enters the **bottom** of a packed column and rises, while a liquid absorbent enters the **top** and falls. Let: - $G$ = molar flow rate of *inert gas* (mol/s) — assumed constant - $L$ = molar flow rate of *inert liquid* (mol/s) — assumed constant - $Y$ = mol pollutant / mol inert gas (gas-phase ratio) - $X$ = mol pollutant / mol inert liquid (liquid-phase ratio) **Step 1 — Component Mass Balance around the top section of the column (envelope cuts stage n+1):** $$ \text{Input (gas)} = \text{Output (liquid)} + \text{Accumulation} $$ For steady-state with no accumulation: $$ G \cdot Y_{n+1} + L \cdot X_n = G \cdot Y_n + L \cdot X_{n+1} $$ **Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate the gas terms on one side:** $$ G (Y_{n+1} - Y_n) = L (X_{n+1} - X_n) $$ **Step 3 — Define operating line by expressing $Y_{n+1}$ as a function of $X_{n+1}$:** $$ Y_{n+1} = \frac{L}{G}(X_{n+1} - X_n) + Y_n $$ **Step 4 — Sum over the entire column (from top stage to bottom):** $$ \int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} dY = \frac{L}{G} \int_{X_2}^{X_1} dX $$ $$ Y_1 - Y_2 = \frac{L}{G}(X_1 - X_2) $$ **Step 5 — Final Operating Line Equation (slope = L/G):** $$ \boxed{Y = \frac{L}{G}X + \left(Y_2 - \frac{L}{G}X_2\right)} $$ This straight line on the **Y vs X diagram** passes through $(X_2, Y_2)$ and $(X_1, Y_1)$ and is the fundamental design equation for counter-current gas absorption. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm **Assumptions:** 1. Fixed number of adsorption sites ($q_m$). 2. Each site holds exactly **one** molecule (monolayer). 3. All sites are energetically equivalent. 4. No interaction between adsorbed molecules. **Step 1 — Write the dynamic adsorption–desorption equilibrium:** Adsorption rate $\propto$ gas pressure $P$ and vacant sites $(q_m - q)$: $$ r_{ads} = k_a \, P \, (q_m - q) $$ Desorption rate $\propto$ occupied sites $q$: $$ r_{des} = k_d \, q $$ **Step 2 — At equilibrium, $r_{ads} = r_{des}$:** $$ k_a \, P \, (q_m - q) = k_d \, q $$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $q$:** $$ k_a \, P \, q_m - k_a \, P \, q = k_d \, q $$ $$ k_a \, P \, q_m = q \, (k_d + k_a \, P) $$ **Step 4 — Divide numerator and denominator by $k_d$ and define $b = k_a / k_d$:** $$ \boxed{q = \frac{q_m \, b \, P}{1 + b \, P}} $$ > **Limiting Cases:** > - At low P ($bP \ll 1$): $q \approx q_m \, b \, P$ → **Henry's Law** (linear isotherm). > - At high P ($bP \gg 1$): $q \approx q_m$ → **saturation monolayer** (constant). --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Excess Air for Combustion For a generic hydrocarbon fuel with formula $C_x H_y S_z O_w$: **Step 1 — Stoichiometric Combustion Reaction:** $$ C_x H_y S_z O_w + O_2 \longrightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O + z\,SO_2 $$ **Step 2 — Required Oxygen (theoretical):** $$ O_2^{stoich} = x + \frac{y}{4} + z - \frac{w}{2} $$ > All values in mol O₂ per mol of fuel. **Step 3 — Actual oxygen supplied with excess air fraction $e$:** $$ O_2^{actual} = (1 + e) \, O_2^{stoich} $$ **Step 4 — Define % Excess Air (%EA):** $$ \boxed{\%EA = \frac{A_{actual} - A_{stoich}}{A_{stoich}} \times 100} $$ Where $A$ is the actual molar air supplied. **Step 5 — Typical design value:** Industrial thermal oxidizers use **10–25 % excess air** to ensure complete combustion. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (for Board Viva) | Parameter | Absorption | Adsorption | Combustion | |---|---|---|---| | **Phase involved** | Gas → Liquid | Gas → Solid | Gas → Flame (thermal) | | **Driving Force** | Concentration gradient | Surface tension / affinity | Heat release (ΔH < 0) | | **Removal mechanism** | Dissolution | Surface adhesion | Oxidation | | **End products** | Solvent + pollutant (recoverable) | Spent adsorbent (regenerable) | CO₂ + H₂O (final, harmless) | | **Energy input** | Low (pumps) | Low (fans) | High (burners) | | **Temperature** | Ambient | Ambient | 760–1200 °C | | **Recovery possible?** | Yes (solvent stripping) | Yes (steam regeneration) | No | | **Best suited for** | SO₂, HCl, HF, NH₃ | VOCs, Hg, odour | CO, hydrocarbons, odours | | **Key equipment** | Packed tower, Venturi | Fixed bed, Fluidized bed | Afterburner, catalytic oxidizer | | **Capital cost** | Medium | Medium | High (fuel + refractory) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Gas Absorption System ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Polluted Gas Inlet
Y1 high] --> B1[Fans and Ductwork] B1 --> C1[Pre-Cooling Section
optional] C1 --> D1[Packed Tower Bottom
Gas Riser] D1 --> E1[Packing Section
Raschig Rings] E1 --> F1[Liquid Distributor
Top of Tower] F1 --> G1[Demister Pad] G1 --> H1[Clean Gas Outlet
Y2 low] L1[Scrubbing Liquid
Fresh Solvent Tank] --> P1[Pump] P1 --> F1 D1 --> R1[Rich Solvent Outlet
X1 high] R1 --> S1[Stripper or
Regeneration Unit] S1 --> L1 style A1 fill:#ffd6d6 style H1 fill:#d6ffd6 style R1 fill:#fff4cc ``` ### 4.2 Schematic — Fixed-Bed Adsorber with Breakthrough Curve ```mermaid flowchart TB In1[Polluted Gas In
C0 high] --> Fan1[Fan Blower] Fan1 --> Bed1[Fixed Adsorbent Bed
Activated Carbon] Bed1 --> Out1[Treated Gas Outlet
C low] Bed1 -.-> Brk[Breakthrough Curve
C vs t] Brk --> Brk1[Phase 1 Saturation Zone
MTZ Moves Down] Brk1 --> Brk2[Phase 2 Mass Transfer Zone
MTZ at Bed Centre] Brk2 --> Brk3[Phase 3 Breakthrough
Regenerate or Replace] Out1 --> Mon1[Continuous Emission
Monitor] style Brk fill:#cce5ff style Brk3 fill:#ffcccc ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Thermal Incinerator (Combustion System) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Process Vent Gas
VOC laden] --> P2[Pre-Heater
Heat Exchanger] P2 --> P3[Combustion Chamber
760 to 1200 C] B1[Natural Gas or
Auxiliary Fuel] --> Burn[Burner] Burn --> P3 P3 --> Q1[Quench Section
Rapid Cooling] Q1 --> P2 P2 --> S1[Stack
CO2 and H2O Exit] P3 -.-> TT[3Ts Triangle
Temperature Time Turbulence] style P3 fill:#ffcccc style S1 fill:#d6ffd6 style TT fill:#fff4cc ``` ### 4.4 Decision Flow — Method Selection for a Given Pollutant ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Identify Pollutant]) --> Q1{Is it water soluble?} Q1 -- Yes --> Abs[Choose ABSORPTION
Scrubbing Tower] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is it valuable or toxic at low concentration?} Q2 -- Yes --> Ad[Choose ADSORPTION
Activated Carbon Bed] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is it combustible organic vapour?} Q3 -- Yes --> Com[Choose COMBUSTION
Thermal or Catalytic Oxidizer] Q3 -- No --> Hybrid[Consider Hybrid System
e.g. Absorption plus Adsorption] style Abs fill:#cce5ff style Ad fill:#d6ffd6 style Com fill:#ffcccc style Hybrid fill:#fff4cc ``` ### 4.5 Functional Architecture of an Integrated Air-Pollution Control Train ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Industrial Source
Stack Emission] --> S2[Quench / Saturation
Pre-conditioning] S2 --> S3[ESP or Cyclone
Particulate Removal] S3 --> S4[Absorber
SO2 HCl HF] S4 --> S5[Reheater
Prevent Plume] S5 --> S6[Adsorber
Residual VOCs Hg] S6 --> S7[Thermal Oxidizer
Final Polishing] S7 --> S8[ID Fan and Stack
Atmospheric Discharge] style S1 fill:#ffcccc style S8 fill:#d6ffd6 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this architecture?** Industrial flue gas (e.g., from a coal-fired boiler or waste incinerator) typically contains **multiple pollutants** — particulates, acid gases, and VOCs. A *single control device* cannot handle all; hence a **train** of unit operations is deployed, often called a **"polishing chain"** in air-pollution control engineering. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Distinguish between Absorption and Adsorption with suitable examples.** **Model Answer:** - **Absorption** is a bulk phenomenon in which the solute molecules are taken up by the *entire volume* of the absorbent (liquid). Example: removal of SO₂ from flue gas using water/lime slurry. - **Adsorption** is a *surface phenomenon* in which solute molecules accumulate on the surface of a solid adsorbent. Example: removal of benzene vapour using activated carbon. - **Mnemonic:** Absorption → bulk "**b**"; Adsorption → surface "**s**". - Absorption is governed by **Henry's Law**; adsorption is governed by **isotherms** (Freundlich/Langmuir). - In absorption, the absorbent is consumed and may need regeneration; in adsorption, the adsorbent is regenerated by desorption (steam, heat, vacuum). **(3 marks)** --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` > **State and explain the "3-T Rule" of combustion as applied to gaseous pollutant control.** **Model Answer:** The 3-T Rule describes the three critical parameters for efficient thermal destruction of gaseous pollutants in an incinerator/oxidizer: 1. **Temperature:** Sufficiently high to ignite and oxidize the pollutant. Typical design value = **760 °C to 980 °C** for hydrocarbons; up to **1200 °C** for chlorinated VOCs (to prevent dioxin formation). 2. **Time (Residence Time):** The pollutant must remain at this temperature for an adequate duration — usually **0.5 to 2 seconds** (τ = V_chamber / Q_gas). 3. **Turbulence:** Adequate mixing of pollutant, oxygen and flame to ensure **complete combustion** without cold pockets; achieved using baffles, swirl burners, and high gas velocities. > All three must be satisfied simultaneously; increasing one can compensate for decreasing another (cost trade-off). **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Q3A.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **(a)** Explain the working principle of a packed absorption tower with a neat sketch. Derive the operating line equation for a counter-current absorber. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A gas mixture containing **10 mol % SO₂** in air is to be scrubbed with water in a counter-current packed tower to reduce SO₂ to **0.5 mol %**. The water inlet is pure ($X_2 = 0$). Calculate the **minimum L/G ratio** if equilibrium is given by $Y^* = 2.0 \, X$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a): **Working Principle:** A vertical cylindrical column is filled with inert packing material (Raschig rings, Pall rings, Berl saddles). The gas enters at the bottom and rises; the liquid absorbent enters at the top and flows down by gravity over the packing, creating a **large gas-liquid interfacial area**. Mass transfer occurs as SO₂ (or any soluble pollutant) diffuses from gas into liquid. **Operating Line Derivation (refer Section 3.1):** For steady-state counter-current operation, the component material balance yields: $$ Y_{n+1} = \frac{L}{G}(X_{n+1} - X_n) + Y_n $$ Integrated over the full column: $$ Y_1 - Y_2 = \frac{L}{G}(X_1 - X_2) $$ This is the **operating line** with slope $L/G$, used to determine the number of transfer stages graphically using the McCabe–Thiele method. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (b): **Given:** - $Y_1 = 0.10$ mol SO₂ / mol air (inlet) - $Y_2 = 0.005$ mol SO₂ / mol air (outlet) - $X_2 = 0$ (pure water) - Equilibrium: $Y^* = 2.0 \, X$ **Step 1 — Find $X_1^*$ (liquid concentration in equilibrium with $Y_1$):** $$ X_1^* = \frac{Y_1}{m} = \frac{0.10}{2.0} = 0.050 $$ **Step 2 — Apply the minimum L/G formula:** $$ \left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^{*} - X_2} $$ $$ \left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} = \frac{0.10 - 0.005}{0.050 - 0} = \frac{0.095}{0.050} $$ $$ \boxed{\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} = 1.90} $$ **Step 3 — Practical Design:** Multiply by 1.2 to 1.5 safety factor for actual operation. So design $L/G \approx 2.3$ to $2.85$. **(7 marks)** --- > **Q3B.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(Alternative to Q3A)** > **(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the principle of a **fixed-bed adsorber** and discuss the concept of the **breakthrough curve**. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A 50 g sample of activated carbon adsorbs **120 mg of benzene vapour** from an air stream at equilibrium partial pressure of **0.05 atm**. The Freundlich constants are $K = 0.80$ and $1/n = 0.42$. Verify the data using the Freundlich isotherm and determine whether the carbon is operating within its **monolayer regime**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a): **Fixed-Bed Adsorber:** A vertical cylindrical vessel is packed with granular adsorbent (typically activated carbon). The polluted gas flows through the bed; the pollutant is adsorbed progressively from top to bottom. Eventually, the top of the bed becomes saturated, and the **mass-transfer zone (MTZ)** moves downward as a wave. **Breakthrough Curve:** A plot of **exit gas concentration (C) vs. time (t)** has three characteristic regions: - **Phase 1 — Initial region:** Clean exit gas ($C \approx 0$). - **Phase 2 — Breakthrough:** MTZ reaches the bed exit; C rises steeply (S-shaped curve). - **Phase 3 — Saturation:** Exit concentration ≈ inlet concentration ($C = C_0$). Operation is stopped at the **breakthrough point** (typically $C/C_0 = 0.05$); the bed is then regenerated by steam or hot gas desorption. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (b): **Step 1 — Compute $x/m$ from given data:** $$ \frac{x}{m} = \frac{120 \text{ mg}}{50 \text{ g}} = 2.4 \text{ mg/g} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Freundlich equation:** $$ \left(\frac{x}{m}\right)_{model} = K \cdot P^{1/n} = 0.80 \times (0.05)^{0.42} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate $(0.05)^{0.42}$:** $$ \ln(0.05) = -2.9957 \quad ; \quad 0.42 \times (-2.9957) = -1.2582 $$ $$ e^{-1.2582} = 0.2843 $$ **Step 4 — Substitute:** $$ \left(\frac{x}{m}\right)_{model} = 0.80 \times 0.2843 = 0.2274 $$ **Step 5 — Compare and Conclude:** Experimental value = 2.40 mg/g; Freundlich predicted = 0.23 mg/g. The experimental value is **~10× higher** than the Freundlich prediction, indicating either: - (i) Langmuir (monolayer) regime is **not** applicable — Freundlich suggests multi-layer coverage at low concentration. - (ii) The data likely follows the **Langmuir isotherm** in the **Henry's Law (linear) region**, indicating **monolayer coverage has not been reached yet**. The carbon is therefore operating in the **linear region of the Langmuir isotherm**, well below saturation — useful in design, but capacity can be improved by increasing partial pressure. **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "absorption" with "adsorption"** in short answers → **1 mark lost**. Always state the bulk vs surface distinction explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting units** of $L/G$ or quoting $m$ as a dimensional number → **½ mark deduction**. > 3. **Skipping the equilibrium line in McCabe–Thiele** → Operating line alone gives 0 marks. > 4. **Wrong "residence time" formula**: Students often use $V/Q$ for liquid instead of gas → 1 mark lost. > 5. **Ignoring excess air** in combustion numericals → Use 10–25% EA in design problems. > 6. **Langmuir vs Freundlich selection**: Choose **Freundlich** for heterogeneous surfaces and **Langmuir** for monolayer; write the assumption clearly. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision):** - ⭐ **Absorption** → gas dissolves into liquid → governed by **Henry's Law** ($y = mx$). - ⭐ **Adsorption** → gas sticks to solid → governed by **Langmuir / Freundlich / BET isotherms**. - ⭐ **Combustion** → burn to CO₂ + H₂O → governed by **3-T Rule** (Temperature, Time, Turbulence). - ⭐ **Operating line slope** = $L/G$; **equilibrium line slope** = $m$ (Henry's constant). - ⭐ **Minimum L/G** formula: $(L/G)_{min} = (Y_1 - Y_2) / (X_1^* - X_2)$. - ⭐ **Langmuir equation:** $q = (q_m b P) / (1 + bP)$ → monolayer assumption. - ⭐ **Freundlich equation:** $(x/m) = K_F \cdot C^{1/n}$ → empirical, multi-layer. - ⭐ **Excess air** in combustion: 10–25% in industrial practice. - ⭐ **Residence time** $\tau = V_{chamber} / Q_{gas}$ — usually 0.5–2 s. - ⭐ **Selection criterion:** soluble → absorption; dilute-toxic → adsorption; combustible-VOC → combustion. - ⭐ **HTU–NTU method:** Total packing height $Z = H_{OG} \times N_{OG}$. - ⭐ **Adsorbents to remember:** Activated Carbon, Silica Gel, Activated Alumina, Zeolite. - ⭐ **Scrubbing liquids:** Water, NaOH, Ca(OH)₂ (lime slurry), amines (for H₂S, CO₂). - ⭐ **Kremser equation** is used for dilute multi-component absorption. > **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE examinations!** 📚

topics.title: Biological Methods for VOC and Odour Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT602 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND TOXIC RISKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Biological Methods for VOC and Odour Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biological methods for VOC (Volatile Organic Compounds) and odour control** constitute a class of air pollution control technologies that utilize the metabolic activity of microorganisms (bacteria and fungi) immobilized on a support medium to biologically oxidize, reduce, or otherwise transform airborne gaseous contaminants into innocuous end-products such as carbon dioxide, water, mineral salts, and biomass. The principal reactor configurations recognized under the KTU 2024 scheme are **biofilters**, **biotrickling filters**, and **bioscrubbers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PCSFT602):** Biological treatment is classified as a "secondary treatment" in the hierarchy of VOC abatement techniques. It is favoured for treating **low-concentration, high-flow-rate, readily biodegradable** waste gas streams (typically inlet loading below **5 g/(m³·h)**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Principle:** The three-step simultaneous process operating in every biological air treatment system is:
> 1. **Mass transfer** of the VOC from the gas phase to the liquid/biofilm phase.
> 2. **Diffusion** of the dissolved VOC through the liquid film to the microbial cell.
> 3. **Biodegradation / Enzymatic Oxidation** of the substrate by the immobilized microflora.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a giant, moist sponge used as a "living air-purifier" for a chemical factory. The sponge is filled with compost, wood chips, or plastic rings that act like an apartment complex for billions of microscopic bacteria and fungi. As the smelly, polluted air is forced upward through this sponge (the **biofilter bed**):

- The harmful molecules dissolve into the thin moisture layer surrounding the microorganisms (just like sugar dissolves in wet tea leaves).
- The microorganisms, which feed on these chemicals as their "food", eat them up, breaking them down into harmless carbon dioxide and water (just like a bio-compost pit turns kitchen waste into manure).
- The cleaned air exits from the top, almost odour-free.

The system is **self-sustaining**, **low-cost**, and **environmentally friendly**, because nature is doing the cleanup work using living organisms rather than expensive chemicals or high-temperature combustion.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Performance Metrics used in KTU 2024 evaluations:**
> - **Empty Bed Residence Time (EBRT):** typically **15 to 60 seconds** for biofilters.
> - **Bed Moisture Content:** maintained between **40% to 60%** (wet basis) for optimal microbial activity.
> - **Operating pH Range:** **6.0 to 8.0** (bacterial systems); **4.0 to 7.0** (fungal systems).
> - **Operating Temperature:** **15°C to 40°C** (mesophilic range).
> - **Superficial Gas Velocity:** **0.005 to 0.5 m/s**.

## 1.4 Classification of Bio-Techniques

| S. No. | Technique | Support Medium | Liquid Phase | Typical Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Biofilter (BF)** | Natural organic (compost, peat, soil) | Static, minimal recirculation | Hydrophobic VOCs, odours, low concentrations |
| 2 | **Biotrickling Filter (BTF)** | Inert synthetic (plastic, ceramic, lava rock) | Continuous recirculating liquid | Acidic intermediates, H₂S, NH₃, soluble VOCs |
| 3 | **Bioscrubber (BS)** | Suspended activated sludge | Continuously flowing liquid | High inlet loads, two-stage pollutant removal |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The biological abatement of VOCs and odourous compounds follows a structured, multi-stage operational sequence. The KTU 2024 examiner expects you to articulate each step clearly:

**Step 1 — Pretreatment of the Waste Gas Stream**
The inlet airstream must be preconditioned before entering the biological reactor. This involves:
- **Particulate Removal:** A pre-filter (typically a **polypropylene mesh or HEPA filter**) is used to remove dust which can clog the biofilter bed.
- **Humidification:** The air is passed through a humidification chamber to raise the relative humidity to **> 95%**, ensuring the moisture content of the support medium remains within the optimal **40% to 60%** range.
- **Temperature Conditioning:** Inlet temperatures above **45°C** are cooled because they are lethal to mesophilic microorganisms.

**Step 2 — Mass Transfer from Gas to Liquid/Biofilm**
The driving force for this transfer is the concentration gradient between the bulk gas phase and the liquid/biofilm interface. This step is governed by the **two-film theory** of Lewis and Whitman (1924). The rate of mass transfer is given by:
$$J_A = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$
where $J_A$ is the mass flux, $k_L$ is the liquid-side mass transfer coefficient, $a$ is the interfacial area per unit volume, $C^*$ is the saturation concentration, and $C_L$ is the bulk liquid concentration.

**Step 3 — Diffusion Through the Biofilm**
Once the VOC is dissolved in the moisture film, it diffuses into the biofilm according to **Fick's First Law**:
$$N_A = -D_{AB} \cdot \frac{dC}{dz}$$
The diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$ in the biofilm is typically **50% to 80%** of its value in pure water because the biofilm presents a tortuous path to the substrate.

**Step 4 — Microbial Metabolism (Biodegradation)**
The substrate is taken up by the microbial cells and enzymatically converted. For a typical VOC such as toluene, the overall aerobic reaction is:
$$C_7H_8 + 9\,O_2 \rightarrow 7\,CO_2 + 4\,H_2O + \text{biomass} + \text{heat}$$
The reaction is exothermic; hence, the bed must be designed to dissipate the heat of reaction (typically **2000 to 5000 kJ per kg of VOC** oxidized).

**Step 5 — End-Product Discharge**
The cleaned air, now containing CO₂, water vapour, and excess O₂, is vented to the atmosphere through a stack. The leachate (if any) from the biotrickling filter is treated separately as a wastewater stream.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarizes all KTU 2024 essential equations. Memorize these for Part A, Part B, and numerical problems.**

| S. No. | Parameter | Formula | Description of Variables | Standard Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Empty Bed Residence Time (EBRT)** | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{bed}}{Q_g}$ | $V_{bed}$ = bed volume, $Q_g$ = gas volumetric flow rate | seconds (s) |
| 2 | **Removal Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$ = inlet / outlet gas-phase concentration | dimensionless (\%) |
| 3 | **Volumetric Loading Rate (VLR)** | $VLR = \dfrac{Q_g \cdot C_{in}}{V_{bed}}$ | Mass of pollutant fed per unit bed volume per unit time | $\text{g}/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h})$ |
| 4 | **Elimination Capacity (EC)** | $EC = \dfrac{Q_g \cdot (C_{in} - C_{out})}{V_{bed}}$ | Mass of pollutant removed per unit bed volume per unit time | $\text{g}/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h})$ |
| 5 | **First-Order Plug-Flow Decay** | $C_{out} = C_{in} \cdot e^{-k \cdot \tau}$ | $k$ = first-order biodegradation rate constant | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 6 | **Michaelis–Menten Kinetics** | $v = \dfrac{v_{max} \cdot C_s}{K_m + C_s}$ | $v_{max}$ = max reaction rate, $K_m$ = half-saturation constant, $C_s$ = substrate conc. at biofilm | $\text{g}/(\text{L} \cdot \text{s})$ |
| 7 | **Darcy's Pressure Drop (Ergun Equation)** | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{H} = \dfrac{150 \mu (1-\epsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \epsilon^3} v_s + \dfrac{1.75 \rho (1-\epsilon)}{d_p \epsilon^3} v_s^2$ | $\mu$ = gas viscosity, $\epsilon$ = bed porosity, $d_p$ = particle diameter, $v_s$ = superficial velocity, $H$ = bed height | $\text{Pa/m}$ |
| 8 | **Biofilm Steady-State Thickness** | $\delta_{ss} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot D_f \cdot C_s}{r_{max}}}$ | $D_f$ = diffusion coefficient in biofilm, $r_{max}$ = maximum specific uptake rate | m |
| 9 | **Specific Surface Area (Packed Bed)** | $a = \dfrac{6(1-\epsilon)}{d_p}$ | $a$ = specific interfacial area | $\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$ |
| 10 | **Power for Fan Sizing** | $P_{fan} = \dfrac{Q_g \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_{fan}}$ | $\eta_{fan}$ = fan mechanical efficiency (typically 0.6 to 0.8) | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> - These formulas are used in the design of **municipal sewage-treatment odour-control units**, **foundry core-making VOC abatement**, **pharmaceutical industry solvent recovery**, and **livestock (poultry/piggery) ammonia removal systems**.
> - In KTU's 2024 OBE framework, mastery of these equations directly maps to **CO1: Apply principles of environmental engineering to identify and quantify pollutant emissions**.

## 2.3 Critical "Why and How" of the Process

- **Why is moisture so critical?** Microbial cells require an aqueous environment for metabolism. Below 40% moisture, microbes become dormant; above 65%, the bed pores fill with water, creating anaerobic zones and excessive pressure drop.
- **Why fungi instead of bacteria for hydrophobic VOCs?** Fungi possess **hydrophobic cell walls** rich in mycelial networks that can directly assimilate compounds such as styrene, hexane, and BTEX, which are poorly soluble in water. Bacterial systems struggle with these.
- **How is acid accumulation controlled in biotrickling filters?** When pollutants like H₂S are oxidized, sulfuric acid is produced, dropping the pH. A continuous recirculating nutrient solution with **automated NaOH dosing** maintains pH in the **6.0 to 8.0** range.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Plug-Flow Biofilter Outlet Concentration

**Starting Point:** Consider a differential slice of biofilter bed of thickness $dH$ and cross-sectional area $A$.

**Step 1 — Steady-State Mass Balance (no accumulation):**
Mass in - Mass out - Mass consumed by reaction = 0

$$Q \cdot C - Q \cdot (C + dC) - r \cdot A \cdot dH = 0$$

**Step 2 — Simplification:**
$$-Q \cdot dC = r \cdot A \cdot dH$$

$$\frac{dC}{dH} = -\frac{r \cdot A}{Q} = -\frac{r}{v_s}$$

where $v_s = Q/A$ is the superficial gas velocity.

**Step 3 — Apply First-Order Biodegradation Kinetics:**
For dilute VOCs at low concentrations, the biodegradation is typically first-order with respect to substrate concentration:
$$r = k \cdot C$$
where $k$ is the apparent first-order rate constant (units: $\text{s}^{-1}$).

**Step 4 — Substitute and Separate Variables:**
$$\frac{dC}{C} = -\frac{k}{v_s} \, dH$$

**Step 5 — Integrate Across the Bed Height $H$:**
$$\int_{C_{in}}^{C_{out}} \frac{dC}{C} = -\frac{k}{v_s} \int_0^H dH$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the Integrals:**
$$\ln C \Big|_{C_{in}}^{C_{out}} = -\frac{k \cdot H}{v_s}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) = -\frac{k \cdot H}{v_s}$$

**Step 7 — Express in Exponential Form:**
$$\boxed{\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}} = \exp\left(-\frac{k \cdot H}{v_s}\right) = e^{-k \cdot \tau}}$$

where $\tau = H/v_s = V_{bed}/Q$ is the **Empty Bed Residence Time (EBRT)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Note for KTU Valuation:** Examiners allocate 2 to 3 marks specifically for the steady-state assumption, 2 marks for the first-order kinetics assumption, and 1 to 2 marks for the final exponential form. Do not skip the intermediate differentials.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Steady-State Biofilm Thickness

**Starting Point:** A biofilm of thickness $\delta$ is attached to a solid surface, and the substrate diffuses from the bulk liquid through a stagnant boundary layer of thickness $\delta_L$ into the biofilm.

**Step 1 — Fick's First Law in the Biofilm:**
$$J = D_f \cdot \frac{dC}{dz}$$

**Step 2 — Substrate Mass Balance in Biofilm (at steady state):**
The rate of substrate diffusion = rate of substrate consumption by microbes
$$D_f \cdot \frac{d^2 C}{dz^2} - \frac{r_{max} \cdot C}{K_m + C} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Approximation for Monod Kinetics (Deep Biofilm):**
In a deep biofilm where substrate concentration at the attachment surface approaches zero, we use a zero-order approximation:
$$r \approx r_{max}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Simplified ODE:**
$$D_f \cdot \frac{d^2 C}{dz^2} = r_{max}$$

Integrating twice with boundary conditions $C = C_s$ at $z = 0$ and $C = 0$ at $z = \delta$:

$$C(z) = C_s - \frac{r_{max}}{2 D_f} z^2$$

**Step 5 — Apply $C(\delta) = 0$:**
$$0 = C_s - \frac{r_{max}}{2 D_f} \delta_{ss}^2$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\delta_{ss}$:**
$$\boxed{\delta_{ss} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D_f C_s}{r_{max}}}}$$

This derivation is essential for understanding **why biofilms do not grow indefinitely**: an equilibrium thickness is established when diffusion limits match consumption.

## 3.3 Numerical Problem — KTU 2024 Standard Format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Module 1, KTU Pattern):**
> A biofilter is installed to treat a contaminated airstream from a paint-manufacturing unit. The operating data are as follows:
> - Inlet VOC (toluene) concentration, $C_{in} = 250$ mg/m³
> - Outlet concentration, $C_{out} = 25$ mg/m³
> - Volumetric flow rate, $Q_g = 2000$ m³/h
> - Bed volume, $V_{bed} = 10$ m³
> - Bed height, $H = 1.5$ m
> - Bed porosity, $\epsilon = 0.45$
>
> **Required:** (a) Empty Bed Residence Time, (b) Removal Efficiency, (c) Elimination Capacity, (d) Volumetric Loading Rate, (e) Specific Surface Area (assuming spherical particles of $d_p = 5$ mm).

### Solution — Part (a): Empty Bed Residence Time (EBRT)

**Step 1 — Convert Flow Rate to m³/s:**
$$Q_g = \frac{2000 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}{3600 \text{ s/h}} = 0.5556 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply EBRT Formula:**
$$\tau = \frac{V_{bed}}{Q_g} = \frac{10 \text{ m}^3}{0.5556 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}} = 18 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 18 \text{ s}}$$

This lies in the **typical range of 15–60 s** for biofilter operation, confirming that the bed is appropriately sized.

### Solution — Part (b): Removal Efficiency ($\eta$)

**Step 1 — Apply the Definition:**
$$\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{250 - 25}{250} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Compute:**
$$\boxed{\eta = 90\%}$$

### Solution — Part (c): Elimination Capacity (EC)

**Step 1 — Apply the Formula in Consistent Units:**
Use $Q_g = 2000$ m³/h and $C$ in g/m³ for a result in $\text{g}/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h})$.
$C_{in} = 250$ mg/m³ $= 0.250$ g/m³
$C_{out} = 25$ mg/m³ $= 0.025$ g/m³

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$EC = \frac{Q_g \cdot (C_{in} - C_{out})}{V_{bed}} = \frac{2000 \times (0.250 - 0.025)}{10}$$

$$EC = \frac{2000 \times 0.225}{10} = \frac{450}{10}$$

$$\boxed{EC = 45 \text{ g}/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h})}$$

### Solution — Part (d): Volumetric Loading Rate (VLR)

$$VLR = \frac{Q_g \cdot C_{in}}{V_{bed}} = \frac{2000 \times 0.250}{10} = \frac{500}{10}$$

$$\boxed{VLR = 50 \text{ g}/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Check:** $\eta = EC / VLR = 45/50 = 0.90 = 90\%$. This is a useful sanity-check formula frequently tested by KTU examiners.

### Solution — Part (e): Specific Surface Area ($a$)

For a packed bed of spherical particles:
$$a = \frac{6(1-\epsilon)}{d_p} = \frac{6(1-0.45)}{0.005} = \frac{6 \times 0.55}{0.005} = \frac{3.30}{0.005}$$

$$\boxed{a = 660 \text{ m}^2/\text{m}^3}$$

This high specific surface area is precisely why packed-bed biofilters achieve such high mass-transfer efficiency.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Complete Biofiltration System

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture flow** of an industrial biofilter system, showing the interactions between the major unit operations from contaminated air intake to clean air discharge.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Contaminated Air Inlet] --> B2[Dust Pre-Filter]
    B2 --> C3[Humidification Chamber]
    C3 --> D4[Temperature Conditioning Unit]
    D4 --> E5[Fan / Blower]
    E5 --> F6[Biofilter Packed Bed]
    F6 --> G6a[Microbial Oxidation Zone]
    F6 --> G6b[Moisture Retention Zone]
    F6 --> G6c[Nutrient Buffering Zone]
    G6a --> H7[Demister / Knock-Out Pot]
    G6b --> H7
    G6c --> H7
    H7 --> I8[Clean Air Stack Discharge]
    F6 --> J9[Leachate Collection Trough]
    J9 --> K10[Nutrient Make-up Tank]
    K10 --> F6
    F6 --> L11[Excess Sludge Discharge]
    L11 --> M12[Wastewater Treatment Plant]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Node A1 → B2 → C3 → D4 → E5:** The contaminant-laden airstream is sequentially cleaned of particulates, humidified to > 95% RH, and temperature-conditioned before being forced by the fan into the biofilter.
- **Node F6 (the Biofilter Packed Bed)** is the heart of the system. It is internally subdivided into three functional zones: microbial oxidation, moisture retention, and nutrient buffering.
- **Node H7 → I8:** Clean air exits through a demister (to remove entrained moisture droplets) and is vented.
- **Node J9 → K10 → F6 (the feedback loop):** Leachate is collected, nutrients are replenished, and the liquid is recycled back to the bed.
- **Node L11 → M12:** Spent biomass and excess sludge are sent for wastewater treatment.

## 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram: Biofilter vs. Biotrickling Filter vs. Bioscrubber

The following block diagram contrasts the three biological reactor configurations in terms of their support media, liquid flow, and dominant removal mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BIOF
        BF1[Inlet Air] --> BF2[Organic Support: Compost / Peat]
        BF2 --> BF3[Static Moisture Film]
        BF3 --> BF4[Immobilized Microbes]
        BF4 --> BF5[Clean Air]
    end

    subgraph BTF
        BTF1[Inlet Air] --> BTF2[Inert Support: Plastic Rings]
        BTF2 --> BTF3[Recirculating Liquid Film]
        BTF3 --> BTF4[Biofilm on Inert Surface]
        BTF4 --> BTF5[Clean Air]
        BTF6[Nutrient Tank] --> BTF3
        BTF3 --> BTF7[pH Control Loop]
    end

    subgraph BS
        BS1[Inlet Air] --> BS2[Absorption Column]
        BS2 --> BS3[Liquid Scrubbing]
        BS3 --> BS4[Activated Sludge Reactor]
        BS4 --> BS5[Clean Air and Treated Liquid]
    end
```

**Interpretation of Subgraphs:**
- **Subgraph BIOF (Biofilter):** Uses a *natural organic* medium that is consumed slowly over time. The moisture is essentially static; the microbes are embedded in the support.
- **Subgraph BTF (Biotrickling Filter):** Uses *inert synthetic* packing. A *continuously recirculating* aqueous film supplies nutrients and buffers pH — this is the key advantage for acidic by-products.
- **Subgraph BS (Bioscrubber):** Operates as a *two-stage system*: first, the gas is absorbed into a scrubbing liquid; then, the pollutant-laden liquid is biologically oxidized in a separate activated sludge reactor.

## 4.3 Mass Transfer and Biodegradation Mechanism Flow

This diagram illustrates the **three-step simultaneous mechanism** of VOC removal in a biofilter, which is a high-priority topic in KTU 2024 examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Bulk Gas Phase: C_g] -->|Step 1: Gas-Liquid Mass Transfer| M2[Liquid/Biofilm Interface: C_s]
    M2 -->|Step 2: Fickian Diffusion in Biofilm| M3[Biofilm Interior: C_bulk]
    M3 -->|Step 3: Monod / Michaelis-Menten Kinetics| M4[Microbial Cell]
    M4 --> M5[Enzymatic Oxidation]
    M5 --> M6[End Products: CO2 + H2O + Biomass]
    M6 -->|Heat of Reaction: 2000 to 5000 kJ/kg VOC| M7[Bed Temperature Rise]
    M7 -.->|Temperature Feedback| M3
```

**Key Reading Points:**
- The **three sequential arrows** (Step 1, 2, 3) represent the rate-limiting cascade. The slowest of the three dictates the overall removal rate.
- The **dashed feedback arrow (M7 → M3)** indicates that the exothermic nature of oxidation raises the biofilm temperature, which in turn affects microbial activity (typically following an Arrhenius-type dependency up to about 40°C).
- **End products** (CO₂ + H₂O) are environmentally benign, which is the central eco-friendly selling point of biofiltration.
