## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Define BOD and explain its significance as a water quality parameter.**

**Model Answer:**

> Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) is the amount of dissolved oxygen (in mg/L) required by aerobic microorganisms to biologically decompose the organic matter present in a water sample over a standard period of **5 days at 20°C**.
>
> **Significance:**
> - Indicates the strength of organic pollution in water.
> - Helps in designing wastewater treatment plants.
> - Determines the assimilative capacity of receiving water bodies.
> - Regulatory compliance metric (IS 10500 standard for drinking water: BOD < 2 mg/L).
>
> Higher BOD → Greater pollution → Lower DO → Greater risk to aquatic life.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**Distinguish between Bioaccumulation and Biomagnification with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Bioaccumulation:** The gradual accumulation of a substance (e.g., mercury) in an individual organism's body over time, when the rate of intake exceeds the rate of excretion.
>
> *Example:* Mercury accumulation in tuna fish from seawater.
>
> **Biomagnification:** The progressive increase in concentration of a substance as it moves up the food chain through trophic transfers.
>
> *Example:* DDT concentration amplification from water (0.003 ppm) → fish (0.5 ppm) → birds (25 ppm).
>
> Bioaccumulation occurs within a single organism; Biomagnification occurs across multiple trophic levels.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Streeter-Phelps equation** for dissolved oxygen sag in a river. Derive the expression for the critical time at which the DO reaches its minimum value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wastewater discharge has an ultimate first-stage BOD of 50 mg/L and initial DO deficit of 2 mg/L. If the deoxygenation rate constant $k = 0.25$ day⁻¹ and reaeration constant $k_2 = 0.6$ day⁻¹, determine:
   (i) The critical time $t_c$
   (ii) The critical dissolved oxygen deficit $D_c$
   (iii) The critical DO concentration (Assume DO saturation = 9.2 mg/L) **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Streeter-Phelps Equation — Sketch and Derivation:**

The **Streeter-Phelps Oxygen Sag Curve** plots dissolved oxygen (DO) versus distance (or time) downstream from a pollution discharge point. The curve shows DO dropping to a minimum (critical point) and then gradually recovering.

```
       DO (mg/L)
        |
   9.2  |---------\
        |          \  Zone of
        |           \ Degradation
        |            \
   DOc  |_____________\__ Critical Point (Minimum DO)
        |              \___________
        |                            \  Zone of Recovery
        |                             \
        |                              \____________
        |
        +------------------------------------------> Distance Downstream
                    tc
```

**Derivation of Critical Time:**

Let $D_t$ be the oxygen deficit at time $t$ downstream.

Two competing processes operate:
- **Deoxygenation** due to BOD exertion at rate $k$: $\frac{dL}{dt} = -kL$
- **Reaeration** from atmosphere at rate $k_2$: $\frac{dD}{dt} = -k_2 D$ (deficit decreases as reaeration increases DO)

Combined differential equation:

$$\frac{dD_t}{dt} = k \cdot L_0 e^{-kt} - k_2 D_t$$

Solving with initial condition $D(0) = D_0$:

$$D_t = \frac{k L_0}{k_2 - k}\left(e^{-kt} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$$

At critical point: $\frac{dD_t}{dt} = 0$

Setting derivative to zero and simplifying:

$$t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k}\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k}\left[1 - \frac{D_0(k_2 - k)}{k L_0}\right]\right)$$

When $D_0$ is small (typical assumption), this reduces to:

$$\boxed{t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k}\ln\left(\frac{k L_0}{k_2 D_0}\right)}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L_0 = 50$ mg/L
- $D_0 = 2$ mg/L
- $k = 0.25$ day⁻¹
- $k_2 = 0.6$ day⁻¹
- $DO_{sat} = 9.2$ mg/L

**(i) Critical Time:**

$$t_c = \frac{1}{0.6 - 0.25}\ln\left(\frac{0.25 \times 50}{0.6 \times 2}\right)$$

$$t_c = \frac{1}{0.35}\ln\left(\frac{12.5}{1.2}\right) = \frac{1}{0.35}\ln(10.417)$$

$$t_c = \frac{2.344}{0.35} = 6.70 \text{ days}$$

**(ii) Critical Deficit:**

$$D_c = \frac{k L_0}{k_2 - k}\left(e^{-k t_c} - e^{-k_2 t_c}\right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t_c}$$

Computing exponential terms:
- $e^{-0.25 \times 6.70} = e^{-1.675} = 0.1873$
- $e^{-0.6 \times 6.70} = e^{-4.020} = 0.01796$

$$D_c = \frac{0.25 \times 50}{0.35}\left(0.1873 - 0.01796\right) + 2 \times 0.01796$$

$$D_c = 35.71 \times 0.1693 + 0.0359$$

$$D_c = 6.046 + 0.0359 = 6.082 \text{ mg/L}$$

**(iii) Critical DO:**

$$DO_c = DO_{sat} - D_c = 9.2 - 6.082 = 3.118 \text{ mg/L}$$

> ⚠️ **Critical Insight:** Since $DO_c = 3.118$ mg/L **< 4 mg/L**, the river exhibits **oxygen stress** and is **unsuitable for sensitive aquatic species**. The discharge load must be reduced.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain **eutrophication** in detail. Discuss its causes, consequences, and control measures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A water sample has BOD₅ = 180 mg/L. The deoxygenation constant $k$ at 20°C is 0.23/day. Calculate:
   (i) The ultimate first-stage BOD $L_0$
   (ii) The BOD remaining after 10 days
   (iii) The BOD exerted after 7 days
   (iv) If the same sample is at 30°C, find the rate constant (assume $\theta = 1.047$) **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Eutrophication — Comprehensive Explanation:**

**Definition:** Eutrophication is the excessive enrichment of water bodies with nutrients (mainly nitrogen and phosphorus), leading to accelerated algal growth, oxygen depletion, and ecosystem degradation.

**Causes:**
- **Point sources:** Sewage treatment plant discharge, industrial effluents.
- **Non-point sources:** Agricultural fertilizer runoff, livestock waste, atmospheric deposition.
- **Natural causes:** Slow release of nutrients from sediments.

**Consequences:**
1. **Algal blooms** — Surface scum, color change, foul odor.
2. **Light blockage** — Submerged macrophytes die.
3. **Oxygen depletion** — Decomposition consumes DO.
4. **Fish kills** — Hypoxic conditions cause mortality.
5. **Toxin production** — Cyanobacteria release microcystins and hepatotoxins.
6. **Dead zones** — Permanent anoxic zones (e.g., Gulf of Mexico hypoxic zone).
7. **Loss of biodiversity** — Native species replaced by tolerant species.

**Control Measures:**
- **Source control:** Limit N and P in detergents and fertilizers.
- **Tertiary treatment:** Remove N and P in wastewater plants.
- **Buffer zones:** Constructed wetlands to absorb runoff.
- **Algaecides:** Copper sulfate application (controlled use).
- **Biomanipulation:** Restocking with planktivorous fish.
- **Phytoremediation:** Aquatic plants (water hyacinth, duckweed) absorb nutrients.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $BOD_5 = 180$ mg/L
- $k_{20} = 0.23$ /day

**(i) Ultimate BOD $L_0$:**

Using $BOD_5 = L_0(1 - e^{-k \times 5})$:

$$180 = L_0(1 - e^{-0.23 \times 5}) = L_0(1 - e^{-1.15})$$

$$e^{-1.15} = 0.3166$$

$$180 = L_0(1 - 0.3166) = 0.6834 \cdot L_0$$

$$\boxed{L_0 = \frac{180}{0.6834} = 263.39 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**(ii) BOD Remaining After 10 Days:**

Remaining organic matter: $L_{10} = L_0 \cdot e^{-k \times 10}$

$$L_{10} = 263.39 \times e^{-0.23 \times 10} = 263.39 \times e^{-2.3}$$

$$L_{10} = 263.39 \times 0.1003 = 26.42 \text{ mg/L}$$

**(iii) BOD Exerted After 7 Days:**

$$BOD_7 = L_0(1 - e^{-k \times 7}) = 263.39(1 - e^{-1.61})$$

$$e^{-1.61} = 0.1999$$

$$BOD_7 = 263.39 \times 0.8001 = 210.74 \text{ mg/L}$$

**(iv) Rate Constant at 30°C:**

$$k_{30} = k_{20} \times \theta^{(30-20)} = 0.23 \times 1.047^{10}$$

Computing $1.047^{10}$:

$$1.047^{10} = e^{10 \ln(1.047)} = e^{10 \times 0.04593} = e^{0.4593} = 1.583$$

$$\boxed{k_{30} = 0.23 \times 1.583 = 0.364 \text{ /day}}$$

> ✅ **Insight:** At higher temperatures, biological activity accelerates, increasing BOD exertion rate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Students often forget to convert BOD₅ into $L_0$ before applying Streeter-Phelps. KTU deducts **1–2 marks** for unit inconsistencies.
> 2. **Sign Error in $k_2 - k$:** When $k_2 > k$ (typical), the difference is positive. Reversing this gives negative $t_c$ — guaranteed 0.
> 3. **Saturation DO Confusion:** Always use $DO_{sat}$ from the standard table or compute via the empirical equation. Hard-coding 9.2 mg/L for all temperatures is incorrect.
> 4. **BOD/COD Ratio Not Interpreted:** Merely computing is not enough; you must state *biodegradability implications*.
> 5. **Biomagnification Factor Misstated:** The amplification factor (e.g., 8000×) is a frequently-asked 1-mark short note. Memorize a canonical example (DDT).
> 6. **No Sketch in Streeter-Phelps:** A 7-mark question *without* a labelled sag curve will lose **at least 2 marks** in KTU 2024 scheme.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Effects of Pollutants on Water Quality and Aquatic Life**
>
> ✅ **Core Definitions to Memorize:**
> - BOD, COD, DO, TDS, TSS, pH, Eutrophication, Bioaccumulation, Biomagnification, Streeter-Phelps model.
>
> ✅ **Critical Constants:**
> - DO saturation at 20°C = **9.2 mg/L** (standard)
> - BOD₅ ≈ **0.68 × L₀** (first-order approximation)
> - Temperature coefficient θ = **1.047**
> - Drinking water BOD limit (IS 10500) = **< 2 mg/L**
> - Fish survival threshold = **> 4 mg/L DO**
>
> ✅ **Must-Know Formulas:**
> - $BOD_t = L_0(1 - e^{-kt})$
> - $L_0 = \frac{BOD_t}{1 - e^{-kt}}$
> - $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$
> - Streeter-Phelps $D_t$ equation
> - Critical time $t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k}\ln\left(\frac{kL_0}{k_2 D_0}\right)$
>
> ✅ **Pollutant Categories:**
> - Organic → BOD↑, DO↓
> - Nutrients (N, P) → Eutrophication
> - Heavy Metals → Bioaccumulation
> - Persistent Organics (DDT) → Biomagnification
> - Thermal → DO solubility↓
> - Acidic/Alkaline → pH stress
>
> ✅ **Key Numerical Patterns (Always Asked):**
> - BOD₅ to L₀ conversion problems
> - Streeter-Phelps critical point problems
> - Temperature-corrected rate constant
> - Biomagnification factor computation
>
> ✅ **Diagrams to Practice:**
> - Oxygen sag curve (with zones labelled)
> - Eutrophication cascade flowchart
> - Biomagnification pyramid
>
> ✅ **Real-World Case Studies to Mention in Answers:**
> - Minamata disease (Mercury, Japan)
> - Itai-itai disease (Cadmium, Japan)
> - Gulf of Mexico Dead Zone
> - Yamuna and Ganga pollution (Indian context)
> - Lake Erie algal blooms (USA)
>
> ✅ **Common 1-Mark Definitions (High Frequency):**
> - Define BOD/COD/DO
> - Define Eutrophication
> - State DO saturation value at 20°C
> - Name two heavy metal pollutants
> - Give one example of biomagnification

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Unit Operations in Waste Water Treatment - Flow Equalization, Sedimentation and Flotation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT602 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND TOXIC RISKS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Physical Unit Operations in Waste Water Treatment - Flow Equalization, Sedimentation and Flotation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Physical Unit Operations — Formal Definition **Physical Unit Operations** in wastewater engineering are the engineered processes that exploit *physical phenomena* (gravity, buoyancy, diffusion, momentum transfer) to remove suspended, colloidal, or floating matter from wastewater **without altering their chemical identity**. They form the **first barrier** (after preliminary screening and grit removal) in any municipal or industrial Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) and constitute the **primary treatment** stage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The three physical unit operations specifically mandated under the 2024 scheme are: > 1. **Flow Equalization** (hydraulic and mass-load buffering) > 2. **Sedimentation** (gravity settling — primary clarifier) > 3. **Flotation** (buoyancy-based separation — predominantly DAF) > > These are the operations that decide whether the downstream **biological/chemical** units (Module 3 & 4) function within their design envelope. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Sink Metaphor" Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen at lunch rush: - **Flow Equalization** = A large holding vessel (the *dump sink*) where all the chaotic washing water from 12 noon to 3 PM is pooled, so a *steady trickle* can be sent to the grease trap. - **Sedimentation** = The **grease trap** itself — heavy food particles *sink* to the bottom under gravity while the clarified water overflows. - **Flotation** = The **skimming spoon** — tiny oil droplets that *refuse to settle* (because they are lighter than water) are *lifted* to the surface using fine air bubbles, just like a spoon scooping froth from a boiling dal. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Intuition:** Sedimentation and Flotation are *mirror-image* processes. Sedimentation uses **gravity (g = 9.81 m/s²)** to pull solids *down*; Flotation uses **buoyancy of gas bubbles** to push low-density matter *up*. The decision of which to use is governed by the **specific gravity difference** between the suspended particle and water (ρs − ρw). ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reference Constants | Symbol | Parameter | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | **g** | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** | | **ρw** | Density of water @ 20°C | **998.2 kg/m³** | | **μw** | Dynamic viscosity of water @ 20°C | **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** | | **ν** | Kinematic viscosity of water @ 20°C | **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** | | **SOR (v₀)** | Surface Overflow Rate | **24 – 48 m³/m²·d** (primary) | | **DAF A/S** | Air-to-Solids ratio | **0.005 – 0.06** (mass basis) | | **HLR** | Hydraulic Loading Rate (DAF) | **5 – 25 m/h** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Flow Equalization ### 2.1.1 Operating Principle Wastewater flow and pollutant concentration fluctuate strongly across a diurnal cycle (peak factors of **2.5–4.0** are common in municipal sewers). An **equalization basin** acts as a *hydraulic capacitor* — storing influent during peaks and releasing it during troughs — so that the downstream plant sees a **near-constant** flow and load. ### 2.1.2 Types of Equalization **a) In-Line (Series) Equalization** - Basin placed **directly in the main flow path**. - Provides both flow and concentration damping. - Requires larger volume (~**20–30%** of average daily flow). - Mixing is **mandatory** (mechanical/aeration) to prevent septicity. **b) Side-Line (Off-Line) Equalization** - Basin placed on a **bypass line** off the main channel. - Only captures *excess flow above the average*. - Volume requirement is smaller (~**10–15%** of average daily flow). - Used for **peak shaving** only; concentration damping is poor. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse *flow equalization* with *wastewater storage*. Storage is for emergency containment; equalization is for **continuous process smoothing**. ### 2.1.3 Design Logic — Step-by-Step 1. Obtain **24-hour flow hydrograph** and **influent concentration profile**. 2. Compute the **average flow** $Q_{avg}$ and **average concentration** $C_{avg}$. 3. Plot a **cumulative inflow curve** and **cumulative outflow line** (slope = $Q_{avg}$). 4. The **maximum vertical deviation** between the two curves = required equalization **volume (Veq)**. 5. Provide **mixing energy** of **0.005–0.020 kW/m³** to keep solids in suspension. 6. If BOD is high, provide **aeration** (1.0–1.5 m³ air/m³ tank·h) to prevent anaerobic odor. ### 2.1.4 Analytical Mass-Balance Equation For a continuously-mixed in-line equalizer with influent concentration $C_i(t)$ and flow $Q_i(t)$, the **effluent concentration** $C_e(t)$ follows: $$ C_e(t) = \frac{C_{i,0} \cdot V_{eq} \cdot e^{-Q_{avg} t / V_{eq}}}{V_{eq}} + \frac{1}{V_{eq}} \int_{0}^{t} C_i(\tau) \cdot Q_i(\tau) \cdot e^{-Q_{avg}(t-\tau)/V_{eq}} \, d\tau $$ For a **step change** $C_i$ → $C_{i,new}$, the response is a **first-order exponential decay**: $$ C_e(t) = C_{i,new} + (C_{i,0} - C_{i,new}) \cdot e^{-t \cdot Q_{avg}/V_{eq}} $$ The **time constant** is $\tau = V_{eq}/Q_{avg}$, and to achieve **95% smoothing** we need $t = 3\tau$. --- ## 2.2 Sedimentation ### 2.2.1 Operating Principle When a discrete particle settles under gravity in a quiescent fluid, it accelerates until **buoyancy + drag = weight**. Beyond this point, it descends at a **constant terminal settling velocity (vs)** — the foundation of every clarifier design. ### 2.2.2 The Four Regimes of Settling (Kynch's Classification) | Regime | Particle Conc. | Behaviour | Relevance in KTU | |---|---|---|---| | **Type I — Discrete** | < 100 mg/L | Particles settle *individually*, no floc interaction | Grit chambers, sand removal | | **Type II — Flocculent** | 100–1000 mg/L | Particles *coalesce* as they fall, vs increases | Primary clarifiers | | **Type III — Hindered/Zone** | 1000–10000 mg/L | Mass settles as a *blanket*; clear liquid above | Secondary clarifiers, thickeners | | **Type IV — Compression** | > 10000 mg/L | Compressed sludge layer thickens slowly | Sludge thickeners | ### 2.2.3 Core Engineering Equations **Stoke's Law** (valid for laminar, Reynolds < 1, particle diameter d < 0.1 mm): $$ v_s = \frac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \cdot \mu} $$ **Transitional/Newton's Law** (Re = 1–1000): $$ v_s = \left[\frac{4 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d}{3 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_w}\right]^{1/2} $$ **Turbulent Regime** (Re > 1000, typical of grit): $$ v_s = 1.83 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{\rho_w} \cdot d} $$ ### 2.2.4 Clarifier Design Parameters - **Surface Overflow Rate (SOR or v₀):** $v_0 = Q/A$ — the **single most important** design parameter. - **Detention Time (td):** $t_d = V/Q$ — typically **2 to 3 hours** for primary, **2 to 2.5 hours** for secondary. - **Weir Loading Rate (WLR):** $WLR = Q/L_{weir}$ — must be < **300 m³/m·d** to prevent solids carryover. - **Removal Efficiency (η):** $$ \eta = \frac{v_0}{v_s} \quad \text{(for discrete settling, ideal case)} $$ For **flocculent settling**, the modified Camp-Hazen model with a settling-velocity distribution $F(v)$ gives: $$ \eta = 1 - \frac{1}{v_0} \int_{0}^{v_0} [1 - F(v)] \, dv $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Key:** A particle is **removed** if its terminal settling velocity $v_s \geq v_0$. The **over-designing for longer detention** is futile beyond a point because removal is controlled by $v_0$, not $t_d$ — an extremely common conceptual trap in viva voce. --- ## 2.3 Flotation (DAF) ### 2.3.1 Operating Principle When water saturated with air at high pressure (300–600 kPa) is suddenly depressurized to atmospheric, **microbubbles** (10–100 μm diameter) nucleate and rise. These bubbles **attach to suspended particles** (especially low-density oils, greases, algae, fibers) and float them to the surface, where a **skimmer blade** removes the float layer. ### 2.3.2 Henry's Law Foundation The mass of air dissolved in water at saturation follows: $$ C_{air} = K_H \cdot P $$ Where $K_H$ is Henry's constant (~ **29.3 mg/L·atm** for air in water at 20°C). The volume of air released on depressurization: $$ A = S \cdot \frac{(P_{sat} - P_{atm})}{P_{atm}} $$ Where $S$ is the saturation solubility at $P_{sat}$. ### 2.3.3 Air-to-Solids Ratio (A/S) The **master design parameter** for DAF: $$ \frac{A}{S} = \frac{Air \, mass \, released}{Influent \, solids \, mass} = \frac{R \cdot C_{rec} \cdot (P_{sat} - P_{atm})}{P_{atm} \cdot Q \cdot C_i} $$ Where $R$ is the **recycle ratio** (typically **5–30%** of influent flow) and $C_{rec}$ is saturation concentration at $P_{sat}$. ### 2.3.4 Design Logic — Step-by-Step 1. Determine **influent SS concentration** ($C_i$, mg/L) and **flow** ($Q$, m³/h). 2. Select **recycle ratio** $R$ (start at 20%). 3. Choose **saturation pressure** $P_{sat}$ (typically 400–500 kPa, i.e., 4–5 atm gauge). 4. Calculate **air release** $A$ per unit volume of recycled water. 5. Compute **A/S ratio** — aim for **0.005 to 0.060** (mass basis). 6. Size the **flotation tank** for **Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR) = 5–25 m/h**. 7. Provide **retention time of 20–60 min** in the contact zone. ### 2.3.5 Sedimentation vs Flotation — Decision Matrix | Parameter | Choose Sedimentation | Choose Flotation | |---|---|---| | Specific gravity of particle | $\rho_s > 1.05$ | $\rho_s \leq 1.0$ (oils, fibers) | | Particle size | > 50 μm | 1–50 μm | | Influent nature | Municipal sewage | Oily wastes, algae-laden water | | Capital cost | Lower | Higher (needs saturator + recycle pump) | | Sludge moisture | High (sludge blanket) | Low (skimmed float) | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Application | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{eq} = Q_{avg} \cdot t_{smoothing}$ | Equalization basin volume | m³ | | 2 | $t_d = V/Q$ | Detention time | h | | 3 | $v_0 = Q/A$ | Surface Overflow Rate | m/d or m/h | | 4 | $v_s = \dfrac{g \cdot \Delta\rho \cdot d^2}{18 \mu}$ | Stoke's settling velocity | m/s | | 5 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_w \cdot v_s \cdot d}{\mu}$ | Reynolds check for regime | dimensionless | | 6 | $\eta = 1 - v_0/v_s$ | Removal efficiency (discrete) | fraction | | 7 | $C_{air} = K_H \cdot P$ | Henry's law saturation | mg/L | | 8 | $A/S = \dfrac{R \cdot C_{rec}(P_{sat}-P_{atm})}{P_{atm} \cdot C_i}$ | DAF air-to-solids ratio | dimensionless | | 9 | $HLR = Q/A_{flot}$ | Flotation hydraulic loading | m/h | | 10 | $WLR = Q/L_{weir}$ | Weir loading rate | m³/m·d | > [!NOTE] > **Use $\vert$ carefully:** In every formula above, the absolute value has been written as $\vert \Delta\rho \vert$ or $C_{air} = K_H \cdot P$ with positive magnitudes. The KTU valuation team will deduct marks for confused $\vert$ notation. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Flow Equalization** is the **silent hero** of every municipal STPs in Kerala (e.g., Cochin, Trivandrum STP upgradations under AMRUT) — without it, the Activated Sludge Process (ASP) downstream would fail under peak shock loads. - **Sedimentation** dominates as the **primary clarifier** in conventional ASP and the **secondary clarifier** that determines effluent SS quality (a key IS 3025 compliance metric). - **Flotation (DAF)** is critical in the **Kerala fish-processing industrial belt** (Cochin, Aroor) where dissolved/colloidal proteins and oils would otherwise overwhelm gravity settlers. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Stoke's Law (From First Principles) **Problem:** Derive the terminal settling velocity of a discrete spherical particle in a viscous fluid under gravity. **Step 1 — Forces acting on the particle in steady descent:** - **Weight** (downward): $F_g = \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot d^3 \cdot \rho_s \cdot g$ - **Buoyancy** (upward): $F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot d^3 \cdot \rho_w \cdot g$ - **Drag force** (Stokes regime, Re < 1): $F_d = 3 \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d \cdot v_s$ **Step 2 — Equilibrium at terminal velocity** (Net force = 0): $$ F_g = F_b + F_d $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the expressions:** $$ \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot d^3 \cdot \rho_s \cdot g = \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot d^3 \cdot \rho_w \cdot g + 3 \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d \cdot v_s $$ **Step 4 — Factor out common terms and rearrange:** $$ \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot d^3 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) = 3 \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d \cdot v_s $$ $$ v_s = \frac{\pi \cdot d^3 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w)}{6 \cdot 3 \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d} = \frac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \mu} $$ **Step 5 — Final compact form (Stoke's Law):** $$ \boxed{\, v_s = \frac{g \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot d^2}{18 \mu} \,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Stoke's validity check:** Must satisfy $Re = \rho_w v_s d / \mu < 1$. For a 60 μm quartz particle in water, $v_s \approx 2.4 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s and $Re \approx 0.15$ — *valid*. For 2 mm grit, Stoke's *fails* and Newton's law must be used. --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Removal Efficiency in an Ideal Sedimentation Tank **Step 1 — Consider a rectangular basin** of length L, depth H, width B, with flow Q entering horizontally at velocity $v_h = Q/(B \cdot H)$. **Step 2 — Define the surface overflow rate:** $$ v_0 = \frac{Q}{A_{surface}} = \frac{Q}{L \cdot B} = \frac{v_h \cdot H}{L} $$ **Step 3 — Particle trajectory** — A particle entering at the water surface travels a *parabolic path*: horizontal motion at $v_h$, vertical motion at $v_s$ (settling). **Step 4 — The critical particle** that *just* reaches the bottom at the outlet has $v_s = v_0$. All particles with $v_s > v_0$ are removed; those with $v_s < v_0$ escape. **Step 5 — Mass balance for uniform particle size distribution with $v_s$ constant:** $$ \eta = \frac{\text{Mass removed}}{\text{Mass in}} = 1 - \frac{v_0}{v_s} \quad \text{(for } v_s > v_0 \text{)} $$ $$ \eta = 0 \quad \text{(for } v_s \leq v_0 \text{)} $$ **Step 6 — Combined expression:** $$ \boxed{\, \eta = 1 - \min\!\left(1, \frac{v_0}{v_s}\right) \,} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation III — DAF Air Release and A/S Ratio **Step 1 — Air solubility in water** (Henry's law) at saturation pressure $P_{sat}$: $$ C_{rec} = K_H \cdot P_{sat} \quad [\text{mg/L}] $$ **Step 2 — Air solubility at atmospheric pressure** (residual in DAF tank, $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa): $$ C_{atm} = K_H \cdot P_{atm} $$ **Step 3 — Mass of air released per m³ of recycled water:** $$ A = S_{rel} = K_H \cdot (P_{sat} - P_{atm}) \quad [\text{mg/L}] $$ **Step 4 — Air released per unit time** from the recycle stream of flow $Q_r$: $$ A_{total} = K_H \cdot (P_{sat} - P_{atm}) \cdot Q_r $$ **Step 5 — Mass of influent solids arriving per unit time:** $$ S_{total} = Q \cdot C_i $$ **Step 6 — Air-to-solids ratio:** $$ \boxed{\, \frac{A}{S} = \frac{K_H \cdot (P_{sat} - P_{atm}) \cdot Q_r}{Q \cdot C_i} = \frac{K_H \cdot (P_{sat} - P_{atm})}{C_i} \cdot \frac{Q_r}{Q} \,} $$ With $Q_r = R \cdot Q$: $$ \frac{A}{S} = \frac{K_H \cdot (P_{sat} - P_{atm})}{C_i} \cdot R $$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Question:** A municipal STP handles $Q = 10,000$ m³/d of sewage. Design a **primary sedimentation tank** with **two units** (so that one can be bypassed for maintenance). Given: - Peak flow factor = **2.5** - SOR = **30 m³/m²·d** (at average flow) - Detention time = **2.0 hours** - Tank depth = **3.5 m** (standard) - Weir length = **5 m** per unit **Solution:** **Step A — Average and peak flows:** $$ Q_{avg} = 10{,}000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}, \quad Q_{peak} = 2.5 \times 10{,}000 = 25{,}000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d} $$ **Step B — Surface area per tank (use peak flow for safety):** $$ A_{per\,unit} = \frac{Q_{peak}/2}{SOR_{peak}} $$ Assuming SOR scales with peak factor: $$ A_{per\,unit} = \frac{12{,}500}{30 \times 2.5} = \frac{12{,}500}{75} = 166.67 \, \text{m}^2 $$ **Step C — Tank dimensions (rectangular, L:B = 4:1):** $$ L \cdot B = 166.67, \quad L = 4B $$ $$ 4B^2 = 166.67 \Rightarrow B^2 = 41.67 \Rightarrow B = 6.45 \, \text{m} $$ $$ L = 4 \times 6.45 = 25.8 \, \text{m} $$ **Step D — Actual volume and detention check:** $$ V = 25.8 \times 6.45 \times 3.5 = 582.4 \, \text{m}^3 \text{ per tank} $$ $$ t_d = \frac{V}{Q_{avg}/2} = \frac{582.4}{5{,}000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{d}} = 0.1165 \, \text{d} = 2.80 \, \text{h} > 2.0 \, \text{h} \quad \checkmark $$ **Step E — Weir loading check:** $$ WLR = \frac{Q_{peak}/2}{L_{weir}} = \frac{12{,}500}{5} = 2{,}500 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m}\cdot\text{d} $$ This **violates** the recommended WLR < 300 m³/m·d. **Remediation:** Install a *launder* (double-sided weir) along both long sides: $$ L_{weir} = 2L = 51.6 \, \text{m} $$ $$ WLR_{corrected} = \frac{12{,}500}{51.6} = 242 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m}\cdot\text{d} \quad \checkmark $$ **Step F — Final Design Summary:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Number of tanks | **2 (operational), 1 standby** | | Length × Breadth × Depth | **25.8 m × 6.45 m × 3.5 m** | | Total volume | **1164.8 m³** | | Detention time @ avg | **2.8 h** | | SOR @ peak | **75 m³/m²·d** | | Weir loading | **242 m³/m·d** | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ##
