> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Common mistakes — (1) Forgetting to convert mg/L to kg using $\times 10^{-3}$ factor, (2) Confusing HRT (θ) with MCRT (θc), (3) Using 30% as recycle ratio instead of wasting fraction. Each error typically costs 1.5–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2 (Industrial Wastewater)**

- **Fertilizer wastewater** = high NH₃-N and F⁻; treat by **stripping + nitrification-denitrification + lime precipitation**.
- **Refinery wastewater** = oil & grease dominant; treat by **API → DAF → biological → AC**.
- **Pulp and paper wastewater** = high colour, moderate BOD; treat by **black liquor recovery → coagulation → biological**.
- **Distillery wastewater** = highest BOD among all four; treat by **UASB → aerobic → RO → ZLD**.
- Always compute **BOD/COD ratio** to decide biological vs advanced oxidation.
- Standard deoxygenation constant **k₁ = 0.23 day⁻¹ at 20 °C**.
- F/M ratio for conventional activated sludge = **0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹**.
- MCRT for conventional ASP = **5 – 15 days**.
- Stoichiometry of lime defluoridation: **1 mole Ca(OH)₂ : 2 moles F⁻**.
- UASB biogas yield = **0.30 – 0.40 m³ CH₄ per kg COD removed**.
- CPCB effluent discharge limit: **BOD ≤ 30 mg/L (inland surface water)**; **O&G ≤ 10 mg/L** for refineries.
- All four industries must comply with **ZLD (Zero Liquid Discharge)** norms in notified critical zones.
- Anaerobic treatment always **precedes** aerobic for high-strength effluents.
- AOX (Adsorbable Organic Halides) is a regulated parameter specific to pulp and paper industry.

> **End of Module 2 — Industrial Wastewater Characteristics & Treatment Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid Wastes - Environmental, Aesthetic and Health Risks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT602 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND TOXIC RISKS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Solid Wastes - Environmental, Aesthetic and Health Risks ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Solid Waste** is defined under **Section 2(35) of the Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016 (India)** and the **Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA), USA (1976)** as: > *"Any garbage, refuse, sludge from a wastewater treatment plant, water supply treatment plant, or air pollution control facility, and other discarded material, including solid, liquid, semi-solid, or contained gaseous material resulting from industrial, commercial, mining, and agricultural operations, and from community activities."* In KTU 2024 terminology, solid wastes are broadly classified into: - **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** – domestic, commercial, institutional - **Hazardous Solid Waste** – toxic, reactive, ignitable, corrosive - **Biomedical Solid Waste (BMW)** – pathological, pharmaceutical, sharps - **Industrial Solid Waste** – process residues, slag, fly ash - **Electronic Waste (E-Waste)** – end-of-life electrical equipment - **Construction & Demolition (C&D) Waste** – debris, concrete, steel > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction (KTU Board Favourite):** Liquid wastes are governed by the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**, while solid wastes are governed by the **Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016** (revised from Municipal Solid Waste Rules, 2000). Do not confuse the two in answers. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine your home as a small **"city ecosystem"**. Every day, you generate vegetable peels, packaging, old newspapers, broken plastic toys, and expired medicines. If you just throw them in a corner, three things happen: 1. **The kitchen (environment) starts rotting** – just like soil, water, and air get polluted. 2. **Your house looks ugly and smells bad** – this is the **aesthetic impact**. 3. **Flies, rats, and mosquitoes show up** – they bring **diseases (health risk)** to your family. Now scale this up to a city of **10 million people** generating **0.5 kg of waste per person per day** (= **5,000 tonnes/day**). The same three problems explode into a full-blown environmental, aesthetic, and public health crisis. > [!TIP] > **Real-world analogy:** Think of unmanaged solid waste as a "**time bomb with three triggers**" — the **Environmental Trigger** (soil/water/air pollution), the **Aesthetic Trigger** (visual blight + odor), and the **Health Trigger** (pathogens, toxins, vectors). All three get activated simultaneously when waste is not scientifically managed. --- ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Units - **Per capita waste generation rate (q):** **0.2 – 0.85 kg/capita/day** (India average ≈ **0.45 kg/capita/day**; developed nations ≈ **1.0 – 2.0 kg/capita/day**). - **Density of MSW (loose):** **150 – 250 kg/m³**; (compacted): **400 – 600 kg/m³**. - **Moisture Content:** **40 – 60 %** (Indian MSW is high-moisture, low-calorific). - **Calorific Value:** **800 – 1500 kcal/kg** (Indian MSW); **2000 – 3000 kcal/kg** (developed countries). - **C/N Ratio (Composting):** **25:1 – 35:1** is ideal. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Domains of Solid Waste Impact Solid waste, when mismanaged, creates cascading impacts across **three interconnected domains**. The KTU 2024 module structure is built exactly on this triad. --- #### 2.1.1 Environmental Risks (The "Hidden, Long-Term" Impact) **Why it happens:** Uncontrolled decomposition and leaching of waste release pollutants into soil, water, and air. **How it manifests:** - **Soil Pollution** - Heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Hg, Cr) from e-waste and batteries accumulate in topsoil. - **Leachate** (toxic liquid) from landfills percolates down to the **groundwater table**. - Reduces soil fertility and renders agricultural land unproductive. - **BOD₅ of leachate** can reach **10,000 – 30,000 mg/L** (compare: domestic sewage ≈ 200 mg/L). - **Water Pollution** - Surface water contamination via runoff during monsoon (Indian context: **June–September**). - **Eutrophication** of lakes/rivers due to high nitrogen and phosphorus from decomposing organic waste. - Leachate plumes from unsanitary landfills can travel **hundreds of metres** through aquifers. - **Air Pollution** - **Methane (CH₄)** generation from anaerobic decomposition → **21× more potent than CO₂** as a greenhouse gas (IPCC AR5, 100-year GWP). - Open burning of MSW releases **Dioxins, Furans, PAHs, PM₂.₅, VOCs**. - **Biomass + plastic burning** is one of the top sources of urban air pollution in Indian cities. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Insight:** Methane from solid waste landfills is the **third-largest anthropogenic source** of methane globally, after rice paddies and livestock. --- #### 2.1.2 Aesthetic Risks (The "Visible, Immediate" Impact) **Why it matters:** Aesthetic degradation is often the **first observable signal** of solid waste mismanagement. While environmental damage is invisible, aesthetic blight is impossible to ignore and directly affects **tourism, property value, and mental well-being**. **How it manifests:** - **Visual Pollution** - Heaps of garbage at street corners, open drains, abandoned lots. - Plastic bags stuck on trees and fences ("**white pollution**" in Chinese/Korean literature; equally relevant in India). - Reduced aesthetic value of public spaces → decline in tourism and civic pride. - **Odor Nuisance** - Anaerobic decomposition releases **H₂S, NH₃, mercaptans, VOCs**. - Detection threshold of H₂S: **0.0005 ppm** (extremely low — complaints start very early). - Reduces quality of life within a **500 m radius** of dumpsites. - **Land-use Degradation** - **Loss of green cover** as waste encroaches onto parks, wetlands, riverbanks. - Encroachment on **floodplains** (common in Kerala: encroachment of paddy fields and river banks for dumping). - Reduced real-estate values near dump sites (studies show **15–30 %** drop in property prices within 1 km). --- #### 2.1.3 Health Risks (The "Direct, Life-Threatening" Impact) This is the **most critical domain** from a public health perspective and carries the highest weightage in KTU examinations. **How it manifests:** - **Direct Pathogen Exposure** - **Faecal-oral route:** Pathogens in waste → flies → food → humans. - **Common pathogens:** *E. coli, Salmonella typhi, Vibrio cholerae, Rotavirus, Hepatitis A/E*. - **WHO estimate:** Poor sanitation and waste management cause **~430,000 deaths/year** from diarrheal diseases globally (mostly in low-income countries). - **Vector-Borne Diseases** - **Vectors:** Houseflies (*Musca domestica*), rats (*Rattus rattus*), mosquitoes (*Aedes*, *Culex*), cockroaches. - **Disease-Vector Mapping (KTU High-Yield):** - Flies → Diarrhea, Typhoid, Cholera, Trachoma - Rats → Leptospirosis, Plague, Hantavirus, Rat-bite fever - Mosquitoes → Dengue, Malaria, Chikungunya, Filariasis - Cockroaches → Asthma (allergens), Food poisoning - **Chemical Toxicity** - **Heavy metal poisoning** (Pb → neurological damage in children; Cd → Itai-Itai disease; Hg → Minamata disease). - **Dioxins and furans** (from PVC/medical waste burning) → carcinogenic, endocrine disruption. - **Brine (leachate in groundwater)** → kidney disease, blue-baby syndrome (methemoglobinemia from nitrates). - **Fire and Explosion Hazards** - Landfill gas (LFG) accumulation → **methane explosions** (e.g., the **2005 Bangalore landfill fire**). - Open burning of waste → **burn injuries, respiratory illness**. - **Occupational Health** - **Waste pickers** (ragpickers in India) face: skin infections, respiratory illness, injuries from sharps, exposure to biomedical waste. - No protective equipment → **average life expectancy of waste pickers ≈ 39 years** (informal estimates from NGOs). --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes the **key quantitative formulae** associated with solid waste impact assessment. The qualitative impacts (aesthetic, health) are covered in the theoretical sections above. | # | Formula / Metric | Symbol | Equation | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Total MSW Generated** | $W$ | $W = P \times q \times 10^{-3}$ | tonnes/day | $P$ = population, $q$ = per capita generation (kg/cap/day) | | 2 | **Per Capita Generation** | $q$ | $q = \dfrac{W_{total}}{P \times n}$ | kg/cap/day | $n$ = number of days | | 3 | **Volume of Waste** | $V$ | $V = \dfrac{W}{\rho}$ | m³/day | $\rho$ = waste density (kg/m³) | | 4 | **Container Volume (Haudor)** | $V_c$ | $V_c = \dfrac{W \times N_c \times d}{1000 \times \rho}$ | m³ | $N_c$ = trips, $d$ = storage days | | 5 | **Leachate BOD₅ Approx.** | $BOD_L$ | $BOD_L \approx 0.5 \times BOD_{ref}$ | mg/L | Reference for sanitary landfill | | 6 | **Landfill Gas Generation** | $Q_{CH_4}$ | $Q_{CH_4} = k \times M \times L_0 \times e^{-kt}$ | m³/year | $k$ = rate constant, $M$ = mass, $L_0$ = methane yield | | 7 | **Population Equivalence of Leachate** | $PE$ | $PE = \dfrac{Q_L \times C_L}{G_{per\,capita}}$ | persons | $Q_L$ = leachate flow, $C_L$ = conc., $G$ = per capita BOD | | 8 | **Methane GWP (vs CO₂)** | $GWP$ | $GWP_{CH_4} = 21$ (AR5, 100-yr) | – | 1 kg CH₄ ≡ 21 kg CO₂-equivalent | | 9 | **Vector Density Index** | $VDI$ | $VDI = \dfrac{N_{vectors}}{A_{site}}$ | vectors/m² | Used in health-risk screening | | 10 | **Distance-Radius of Odor Impact** | $r$ | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{Q_{odor}}{C_{threshold} \times u}}$ | m | $u$ = wind speed (m/s) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In the 14-mark Part B questions, you will rarely get a pure numerical problem on "health/aesthetic risks." These domains are typically assessed via **short notes (3 marks)** or **descriptive sub-parts (7 marks)**. The formulae above are tested via **numericals on waste generation (Formula 1–3)** as a stand-alone question, and qualitative risk-mapping as the descriptive part. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering | Engineering Domain | Application of Solid Waste Risk Knowledge | |---|---| | **Urban Planning** | Site selection for landfills, buffer zones (typically **500 m** from habitation per SWM Rules 2016) | | **Public Health Engineering** | Design of vector control programs, sanitation worker safety protocols | | **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** | Mandatory for landfills > 5 TPD (Tons Per Day) under EIA Notification 2006 | | **Disaster Management** | Post-flood solid waste management (Kerala 2018 floods generated **> 5 lakh tonnes** of waste) | | **Industrial Hygiene** | Occupational health standards for waste-picker communities | | **Climate Engineering** | Landfill gas capture for **flaring or power generation (0.5–1.0 MWh/tonne of MSW)** | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivative 1: Total MSW Generation (Worked Numerical) **Problem (KTU University Exam - May 2023):** A town has a population of **2,50,000**. The per capita waste generation rate is **0.45 kg/cap/day**. Calculate: (a) Total MSW generated per day in tonnes, (b) Volume per day assuming waste density of **300 kg/m³**, (c) Number of compactor trips required if each trip carries **5 tonnes** and collection is **daily**. --- #### Part (a): Total MSW per day $$ \begin{aligned} W &= P \times q \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{[tonnes/day]} \\ &= 250000 \times 0.45 \times 10^{-3} \\ &= 250000 \times 0.00045 \\ &= 112.5 \;\; \text{tonnes/day} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Multiply population by per-capita rate to get kg/day, then convert kg → tonnes by dividing by 1000 (i.e., multiplying by $10^{-3}$). --- #### Part (b): Volume per day $$ \begin{aligned} V &= \dfrac{W}{\rho} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/day]} \\ &= \dfrac{112.5 \times 1000}{300} \quad \text{[tonnes} \rightarrow \text{kg: multiply by 1000]} \\ &= \dfrac{112500}{300} \\ &= 375 \;\; \text{m}^3\text{/day} \end{aligned} $$ --- #### Part (c): Number of compactor trips $$ \begin{aligned} N_{trips} &= \dfrac{W}{W_{per\,trip}} \\ &= \dfrac{112.5}{5} \\ &= 22.5 \;\; \text{trips} \rightarrow \text{round up to} \;\; 23 \;\; \text{trips/day} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Always round UP (ceiling function) the number of trips — you cannot run a "half trip." This is a common KTU valuation trap (1/2 mark deducted if you write 22 trips). --- ### 3.2 Derivative 2: Population Equivalence of Landfill Leachate (Analytical Insight) **Conceptual Question (KTU Pattern):** A landfill generates **500 m³/day** of leachate with an **average BOD₅ of 15,000 mg/L**. If the per-capita BOD contribution from domestic sewage is **54 g/cap/day (0.054 kg/cap/day)**, calculate the population equivalence of the leachate pollution load. --- $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Leachate BOD load per day} \; (B_L) &= Q_L \times C_L \quad \text{[kg/day]} \\ &= 500 \times 15,000 \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{[convert mg/L to kg/m}^3\text{: 1 mg/L = 1 g/m}^3 = 10^{-3}\text{ kg/m}^3\text{]} \\ &= 500 \times 15 \\ &= 7500 \;\; \text{kg BOD/day} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Population Equivalence} \; (PE) &= \dfrac{B_L}{G_{per\,capita}} \\ &= \dfrac{7500 \;\; \text{kg/day}}{0.054 \;\; \text{kg/cap/day}} \\ &= 138,888.89 \\ &\approx 1,38,889 \;\; \text{persons} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** A single medium-sized landfill is producing leachate pollution equivalent to a city of **~1.4 lakh people** — and this pollution is **concentrated and toxic**, not just organic. This is why leachate management is a critical KTU topic under environmental risks of solid waste. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Solid Waste → Health Risk Pathway > [!IMPORTANT] > The matrix below is a **high-yield KTU revision tool**. Exam questions often ask: *"List the health hazards of municipal solid waste"* or *"Explain the vector-borne disease transmission pathway from solid waste."* Use this matrix as a model answer skeleton. | **Stage** | **Pathway** | **Mechanism** | **Disease / Impact** | **Affected Population** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Waste Generation** | Organic fraction decomposes anaerobically | Odor, leachate formation | Residents within 1 km | | 2 | **Storage at Source** | Mixed waste in open bins | Vector breeding (flies, cockroaches) | Household members | | 3 | **Collection (Delayed)** | Garbage overflows onto streets | Visual pollution, stray animal access | Street users, children | | 4 | **Transportation** | Spillage, leachate drip | Soil contamination, bad odor | Commuters | | 5 | **Open Dumping** | Leachate percolation, gas generation | Groundwater contamination, methane explosion | Downstream aquifer users, 500 m radius | | 6 | **Open Burning** | Dioxin, furan, PM₂.₅ release | Respiratory illness, cancer | Downwind residents | | 7 | **Informal Recycling** | Direct contact without PPE | Cuts, infections, heavy-metal poisoning | Ragpickers, waste pickers | | 8 | **Landfill Closure** | Long-term gas + leachate | Chronic groundwater pollution | Future generations | --- ### 3.4 Derivative 3: Buffer Zone Calculation (Conceptual Geometry) **KTU Short-Note Style (5 marks):** Why is a **500 m buffer zone** prescribed around a sanitary landfill site? > **Derivation Logic:** > 1. **Odor propagation radius:** Using the Gaussian plume dispersion model, a strong odor source (e.g., H₂S at 1 ppm) at a wind speed of 2 m/s would be diluted below the **0.0005 ppm detection threshold** at approximately **r ≈ 400–600 m**. > 2. **Vector flight range:** Houseflies travel up to **1–2 km**, but **95 %** of breeding activity is within **200 m** of the source. > 3. **Groundwater protection radius:** A 500 m buffer prevents residential wells from tapping the leachate plume (typical groundwater flow velocity ≈ 0.5 m/day → 500 m = 1000 days ≈ 3 years of safe residence time). > 4. **Explosion safety:** Methane dispersion at 5 % LEL (Lower Explosive Limit) is negligible beyond 500 m under typical wind conditions. > **Conclusion:** The **500 m** distance is a **compromise** between (a) engineering feasibility of land acquisition, (b) odor and vector control, and (c) groundwater safety. This is why the **SWM Rules 2016** prescribe this buffer for legacy dumpsites. --- ### 3.5 Risk Classification Matrix (Health Severity) | **Hazard Category** | **Acute Effect** | **Chronic Effect** | **Population at Risk** | **KTU Priority** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Biological (Pathogens)** | Diarrhea, cholera | Hepatitis, parasitic load | All | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Vector-Borne** | Malaria, dengue | Elephantiasis, post-dengue syndrome | Residents near dumps | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Chemical (Heavy Metals)** | Acute poisoning (rare) | Neurotoxicity, cancer | E-waste workers, children | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Air (Dioxins, PM)** | Asthma attack | Lung cancer, COPD | Downwind 5 km | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Physical (Sharps, Fires)** | Injury, burns | Chronic respiratory | Ragpickers | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Psychological** | Stress, anxiety | Depression, reduced property value | Nearby residents | ⭐⭐ | ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't write vague answers** like *"Solid waste causes pollution."* Examiners expect **specific sub-points** — Soil, Water, Air, Climate for environmental; Visual, Odor, Land-use, Socio-economic for aesthetic; and Pathogens, Vectors, Chemicals, Physical, Psychological for health. > 2. **Don't confuse Solid Waste Rules (2016)** with **Hazardous Waste Rules (2016)**. They are two different sets of rules under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**. > 3. **Always cite the specific disease** when talking about vector-borne risk. "Rats cause disease" is **0 marks**; "Rats cause leptospirosis, plague, and rat-bite fever" gets **full marks**. > 4. **Never write "MSW"** without expanding it to **Municipal Solid Waste** in the first occurrence. KTU deducts ½ mark for unexplained acronyms. > 5. In numericals, **always convert units explicitly** (e.g., mg/L → g/m³ → kg/m³). Showing the conversion is worth **1 mark** by itself. --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1 (3 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: List any THREE health hazards associated with improper management of municipal solid waste.** **Model Answer:** Improper management of Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) leads to the following health hazards: 1. **Pathogen-related diseases:** Open dumping of organic waste allows pathogens like *Vibrio cholerae*, *Salmonella typhi*, and Rotavirus to multiply. These spread via the faecal-oral route through flies and contaminated water, causing **cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A, and gastroenteritis**. 2. **Vector-borne diseases:** Accumulated solid waste serves as breeding grounds for **houseflies (diarrhea, trachoma)**, **rats (leptospirosis, plague)**, and **mosquitoes (dengue, malaria, chikungunya)**. 3. **Chemical toxicity:** E-waste and biomedical waste contain **lead, cadmium, mercury, and dioxins**. Chronic exposure causes **neurological damage, kidney disease, cancer, and endocrine disruption**, especially in children and waste-picker communities. > *(3 marks — 1 mark per well-explained hazard)* --- #### **Question 2 (3 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: Explain the term "leachate" and state any THREE of its environmental impacts.** **Model Answer:** **Leachate** is the **toxic liquid** that forms when water (rainfall or moisture from the waste itself) percolates through decomposing solid waste in a landfill, dissolving soluble organic and inorganic contaminants along the way. **Three Environmental Impacts of Leachate:** 1. **Groundwater contamination:** Leachate percolates down to aquifers, introducing **heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Hg)** and **high BOD₅ (10,000–30,000 mg/L)** into drinking-water sources, causing **methemoglobinemia (blue-baby syndrome)** and chronic kidney disease. 2. **Surface water pollution:** Leachate runoff into rivers and lakes causes **eutrophication** due to high nitrogen and phosphorus, leading to **algal blooms and fish kills**. 3. **Soil degradation:** Leachate lowers soil pH (acidic leachate, pH 4–6), destroys soil microbiota, and accumulates **non-biodegradable heavy metals** in the topsoil, rendering agricultural land barren. > *(3 marks — 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for impacts)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) > [!NOTE] > Following the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, every Part B question carries **14 marks** with **internal choice**. Both alternatives are presented below for full preparation. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]` **Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the **environmental risks** of solid waste mismanagement, with special reference to **soil, water, and air pollution**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **aesthetic and socio-economic impacts** of uncollected solid waste on urban areas. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Answer for Q. A (a) — Environmental Risks (7 marks)** The improper management of solid waste creates a **triad of environmental degradation** — affecting soil, water, and air — with cascading effects on climate. **1. Soil Pollution (2 marks):** - Heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Cr, Hg) from e-waste, batteries, and industrial waste accumulate in the topsoil. - Leachate percolation reduces soil pH and kills beneficial soil microbes. - **Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs)** and **dioxins** from open burning persist in soil for decades. - Reduction in agricultural yield and **bioaccumulation** in food crops (e.g., heavy metals in rice and leafy vegetables). **2. Water Pollution (2 marks):** - Leachate from unsanitary landfills contaminates **groundwater aquifers** — a major concern in Kerala's laterite terrain. - Surface runoff during monsoons carries waste into rivers and backwaters (e.g., Cochin backwater pollution). - Leachate from **1 hectare of landfill ≈ pollution load of a 1 lakh-population city** (population equivalence). - Eutrophication of freshwater bodies due to high N and P from decomposing organic waste. **3. Air Pollution (2 marks):** - **Open burning of MSW** releases PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, VOCs, dioxins, and furans. - **Methane (CH₄)** generation from anaerobic decomposition in landfills — **21× more potent than CO₂** as a greenhouse gas (IPCC AR5). - **Hazardous air pollutants** from biomedical and e-waste incineration cause respiratory and cardiovascular disease. **4. Climate Change (1 mark):** - Solid waste contributes **~5 % of global anthropogenic GHG emissions** (UNEP estimate). - Landfills are the **3rd-largest source of CH₄ emissions** globally. --- ### **Model Answer for Q. A (b) — Aesthetic and Socio-Economic Impacts (7 marks)** **1. Visual Pollution (2 marks):** - Heaps of garbage at street corners, drains, and vacant plots create a "**visual blight**." - Plastic bags stuck on trees and fences — sometimes called "**white pollution**" — reduce the scenic value of cities and tourist destinations. - Aesthetic decline correlates with reduced **civic engagement** and **public pride** in urban spaces. **2. Odor Nuisance (2 marks):** - Anaerobic decomposition releases **hydrogen sulphide (H₂S), ammonia (NH₃), and mercaptans**. - **H₂S** is detectable at **0.0005 ppm** — complaints start well below health-threat levels. - Odor plumes travel **500 m to 2 km** depending on wind speed, affecting quality of life in surrounding neighbourhoods. **3. Land-use Degradation (1.5 marks):** - Encroachment on **wetlands, riverbanks, and paddy fields** for waste dumping (highly relevant in Kerala post-2018 floods). - Reduced **green cover** and ecological balance. - Property values within **1 km of a dumpsite drop by 15–30 %**. **4. Socio-Economic Impact (1.5 marks):** - **Tourism decline** in heritage and coastal cities due to aesthetic degradation. - **Public health expenditure** rises for surrounding communities. - Loss of **livelihoods in fisheries and tourism** sectors. - **Injustice to waste-picker communities** who work without protective equipment and are often socially marginalized. > *(14 marks — model answer complete)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` **Q: (a)** List the **major health risks** of solid waste to humans and explain the **vector-borne disease transmission mechanism** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A city has a population of **8,00,000**, generating solid waste at **0.50 kg/cap/day**. If the waste density is **400 kg/m³**, calculate: (i) Total waste generated per day in **tonnes**, (ii) Volume of waste per day in **m³**, (iii) Number of **compactor trucks of 6-tonne capacity** required if **2 trips/day** per truck is planned. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Answer for Q. B (a) — Health Risks and Vector-Borne Transmission (7 marks)** **Major Health Risks of Solid Waste (4 marks):** | Category | Specific Risk | Vulnerable Group | |---|---|---| | **Biological** | Typhoid, Cholera, Hepatitis A/E, Diarrhea | All residents | | **Vector-Borne** | Dengue, Malaria, Plague, Leptospirosis | Residents near dumps | | **Chemical** | Lead poisoning, Cancer, Endocrine disruption | Children, waste pickers | | **Physical** | Injuries from sharps, burns, explosions | Waste pickers | | **Psychological** | Stress, anxiety, depression | Nearby residents | **Vector-Borne Transmission Mechanism (3 marks):** Solid waste provides **breeding substrate and shelter** for disease vectors. The transmission mechanism follows this pathway: 1. **Substrate availability:** Organic waste + stagnant water (in discarded tires, cans, plastic containers) = ideal breeding sites for **flies and mosquitoes**. 2. **Vector proliferation:** Houseflies breed in 8–12 hours; Aedes mosquito eggs hatch in 7 days. 3. **Pathogen pickup:** Vectors feed on decomposing waste containing faecal pathogens. 4. **Mechanical/biological transfer:** Flies carry pathogens on their legs and body to **kitchen surfaces and food** (mechanical transmission). Mosquitoes biologically transmit viruses like **Dengue (Aedes aegypti)**, **Malaria (Anopheles)**, and **Chikungunya (Aedes albopictus)**. 5. **Human infection:** When an infected vector bites a human or contaminates food/water, disease transmission is complete. 6. **Examples:** Rats from waste piles transmit **Leptospirosis** via urine in water; **Plague** via flea bites; houseflies transmit **trachoma and typhoid** mechanically. > **Conclusion:** Breaking the transmission chain at any point (e.g., eliminating breeding sites) prevents disease — this is the principle behind **Integrated Vector Management (IVM)** in modern SWM. --- ### **Model Answer for Q. B (b) — Numerical (7 marks)** **Given:** - Population, $P = 8,00,000$ - Per capita generation, $q = 0.50$ kg/cap/day - Waste density, $\rho = 400$ kg/m³ - Truck capacity, $W_{truck} = 6$ tonnes - Trips per truck per day, $N = 2$ **Step 1: Total waste per day (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} W &= P \times q \times 10^{-3} \\ &= 8,00,000 \times 0.50 \times 10^{-3} \\ &= 8,00,000 \times 0.0005 \\ &= 400 \;\; \text{tonnes/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Volume per day (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} V &= \dfrac{W}{\rho} = \dfrac{400 \times 1000}{400} \\ &= 1000 \;\; \text{m}^3\text{/day} \end{aligned} $$ > *(Conversion note: 400 tonnes = 400 × 1000 = 4,00,000 kg)* **Step 3: Number of trucks required (3 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total trips required} &= \dfrac{W}{W_{truck}} = \dfrac{400}{6} = 66.67 \;\; \text{trips/day} \\ \text{Trucks required} &= \dfrac{\text{Total trips}}{\text{Trips per truck}} = \dfrac{66.67}{2} = 33.33 \\ &\rightarrow \text{Round up to} \;\; 34 \;\; \text{trucks} \end{aligned} $$ > *(Always round UP — 33 trucks would leave ~ 6.67 trips unhandled)* **Final Answers:** - (i) **400 tonnes/day** - (ii) **1000 m³/day** - (iii) **34 trucks** > *(14 marks — model answer complete)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save This for the Last-Night Read)** - ✅ **Solid waste** is governed by **SWM Rules, 2016** (revised from MSW Rules, 2000); hazardous waste is a **separate** rule set. - ✅ Three impact domains: **Environmental, Aesthetic, Health** — never merge them in an answer. - ✅ **Per capita waste generation** in India ≈ **0.45 kg/cap/day**; developed nations ≈ **1.0–2.0 kg/cap/day**. - ✅ **Leachate BOD₅** can reach **10,000–30,000 mg/L** — 50–150× stronger than domestic sewage. - ✅ **Methane** from landfills has a **GWP of 21** (CO₂-equivalent, 100-yr horizon) — **landfills are the 3rd-largest CH₄ source globally**. - ✅ **Buffer zone** for sanitary landfills = **500 m** from habitation (per SWM Rules 2016). - ✅ **Vector-disease mapping (Must-Memorize):** - **Flies** → Diarrhea, Typhoid, Cholera, Trachoma - **Rats** → Leptospirosis, Plague, Hantavirus - **Aedes mosquito** → Dengue, Chikungunya, Zika - **Anopheles mosquito** → Malaria - **Culex mosquito** → Filariasis - ✅ **Open burning of MSW** releases **Dioxins, Furans, PAHs, PM₂.₅** — avoid saying "CO₂" alone. - ✅ **Aesthetic impact** = **Visual pollution + Odor + Land-use loss + Socio-economic** (always 4 sub-points). - ✅ **Health impact** = **Biological + Vector-borne + Chemical + Physical + Psychological** (always 5 sub-points). - ✅ **Key formulae:** $W = P \times q \times 10^{-3}$ (tonnes/day); $V = W/\rho$ (m³/day). - ✅ **Always convert units explicitly** in numericals (kg ↔ tonnes ↔ mg/L ↔ g/m³). - ✅ **Always round UP** the number of trucks/trips (ceiling function). - ✅ **500 m** is the canonical buffer zone — derived from odor dispersion, vector flight, groundwater travel time, and explosion safety. - ✅ **EIA Notification 2006** mandates Environmental Impact Assessment for **landfills > 5 TPD** (Tonnes Per Day). - ✅ **Kerala-specific context:** 2018 floods generated **> 5 lakh tonnes of disaster waste** — a classic local-relevance point examiners love. - ✅ **Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 11.6** specifically targets "**reduce adverse environmental impact of cities, including air quality and waste management**" — quote this for higher-order marks. - ✅ **Ragpickers' average life expectancy** is estimated at **~39 years** — a powerful socio-economic data point.

topics.title: Sources, Quantities and Composition of Solid Wastes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT602 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND TOXIC RISKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sources, Quantities and Composition of Solid Wastes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid Waste** is defined under the **Solid Waste Management Rules (SWM), 2016** of India and the **Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA)** of the US EPA as: *Any garbage, refuse, sludge from a wastewater treatment plant, water supply treatment plant, or air pollution control facility, and other discarded material, including solid, liquid, semi-solid, or contained gaseous material resulting from industrial, commercial, mining, agricultural operations, and from community activities.*

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** is specifically referred to as the everyday items and materials that are discarded by the public from residential, commercial, institutional, and industrial sources. It does **not** include hazardous waste, biomedical waste, or e-waste unless mixed with general MSW streams.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a city as a giant human body, and solid waste as its metabolic waste.** Just as our body produces urine, sweat, and feces as by-products of consuming food, water, and air, a city "consumes" packaged food, water bottles, old clothes, broken appliances, and construction material — and "excretes" the leftovers as solid waste. The **Sources** tell us *where this waste originates* (kitchens, offices, factories), the **Quantities** tell us *how much is produced* (kg/capita/day), and the **Composition** tells us *what it is made of* (organic vs. plastic vs. metal).

### 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics

The quantification of solid waste relies on three primary standard metrics:

- **Per Capita Waste Generation Rate (PCGR):** Measured in **kg/capita/day** (typically **0.3 to 0.6 kg/capita/day** in Indian cities; **1.2 to 2.0 kg/capita/day** in developed nations like the USA).
- **Waste Density (Bulk Density):** Measured in **kg/m³** (loose density ranges from **100 to 200 kg/m³**; compacted **400 to 700 kg/m³**).
- **Moisture Content:** Expressed as a percentage of wet weight (**20% to 60%** for typical Indian MSW).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always cite the **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board)** values when answering numerical questions on Indian waste generation. The most cited CPCB figure for Indian metro cities is **0.50 kg/capita/day**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Solid Waste — A Structured Breakdown

Solid waste sources are broadly categorized into **six primary sectors** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus and standard text by **Tchobanoglous & Kreith**:

1. **Residential / Domestic Sources**
   - Kitchen waste (food scraps, vegetable peels)
   - Garden waste (leaves, grass)
   - Paper, plastics, glass, metals, textiles
   - Bulky items (furniture, appliances)

2. **Commercial Sources**
   - Offices, shops, hotels, restaurants
   - Markets — high organic, packaging waste
   - Printing press waste (paper-heavy)

3. **Institutional Sources**
   - Schools, colleges, hospitals (excludes biomedical waste)
   - Government offices
   - Religious/charity centers

4. **Construction & Demolition (C&D) Sources**
   - Concrete, bricks, wood, steel, drywall
   - Excavated soil, rubble

5. **Industrial Sources (Non-hazardous fraction)**
   - Fly ash, slag, scrap metals, packaging
   - Food processing wastes

6. **Municipal Services / Street Sweeping**
   - Road dust, leaves, animal carcasses
   - Drainage silt, park waste

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept — Source vs. Type:**
> The **source** answers *WHO* generates the waste, while the **type** answers *WHAT* it is composed of. KTU questions frequently confuse these two, so remember: *Kitchen = Source; Vegetable peel = Type.*

### 2.2 Classification (Types) of Solid Waste

- **Organic / Biodegradable** — Food waste, yard waste, paper
- **Recyclable / Inorganic** — Plastics, glass, metals, paper
- **Hazardous / Toxic** (household level) — Batteries, paints, pesticides, e-waste
- **Inert** — Construction debris, soil, rubble
- **Composite** — Tetra packs, multilayer plastics

### 2.3 Composition of Solid Waste — Physical & Chemical

The composition is typically expressed as a **percentage by weight** of:

- **Physical Components:** Food waste, paper, plastics, glass, metals, textiles, yard waste, wood, leather, rubber, inert material.
- **Chemical Components:** Moisture, volatile matter (combustible), fixed carbon, ash (non-combustible), calorific value (kJ/kg).
- **Biological Components:** Pathogens, biodegradable fraction.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Parameter** | **Expression** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Waste Generation | $Q = P \times q \times 365$ | kg/year | Annual waste from a city |
| Per Capita Rate | $q = Q_{total} / (P \times N)$ | kg/capita/day | Average waste by one person |
| Density | $\rho = M / V$ | kg/m³ | Container/vehicle capacity design |
| Moisture Content | $MC = (W_w - W_d) / W_w \times 100$ | % | Composting/biodegradability assessment |
| Volatile Solids | $VS = (W_d - W_a) / W_d \times 100$ | % | Calorific value estimation |
| Calorific Value | $CV = \Sigma (f_i \times CV_i)$ | kJ/kg | Energy recovery (incineration) |
| Waste Composition (Component %) | $\% C_i = W_i / W_{total} \times 100$ | % | Sorting/recycling design |
| Collection Efficiency | $\eta_c = W_{collected} / W_{generated} \times 100$ | % | Municipal performance metric |
| BOD/COD Ratio | $Ratio = BOD_5 / COD$ | dimensionless | Leachate biodegradability |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Rule for Tables:** When writing modulus bars in tables (e.g., $\vert x \vert$), always use the LaTeX `\vert` command to prevent markdown table breakage.

### 2.5 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

- **Landfill Design:** Composition determines leachate strength (high organics → high BOD/COD leachate).
- **Incinerator Design:** Calorific value determines whether waste can sustain combustion without auxiliary fuel.
- **Composting Plant:** Organic fraction determines the plant's capacity and economics.
- **Recycling Economics:** Plastic/metal composition dictates revenue streams for MRFs (Material Recovery Facilities).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations of Core Quantification Equations

#### **Derivation 1: Total Annual Waste Generation**

The total waste generated by a municipality in one year is the product of population, per capita generation rate, and the number of days in a year.

$$
Q_{annual} = P \times q \times 365
$$

Where:
- $Q_{annual}$ = Total annual waste (kg/year)
- $P$ = Population (number of persons)
- $q$ = Per capita waste generation rate (kg/capita/day)
- $365$ = Number of days in a year (constant)

**Conversion logic:** Waste generated per person per day is multiplied by population to give total per day, then multiplied by 365 to annualize.

#### **Derivation 2: Bulk Density of Waste in a Collection Vehicle**

$$
\rho_{bulk} = \frac{M_{waste}}{V_{container}}
$$

Where:
- $\rho_{bulk}$ = Bulk density in **kg/m³**
- $M_{waste}$ = Total mass of waste in kg
- $V_{container}$ = Volume of the container in m³

**Conversion logic:** Density is fundamentally mass per unit volume. For MSW, this value is critical for designing compaction trucks, transfer stations, and landfill cells.

#### **Derivation 3: Moisture Content on Wet Basis**

$$
MC_{wb} = \frac{W_{w} - W_{d}}{W_{w}} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $MC_{wb}$ = Moisture content (%)
- $W_{w}$ = Wet weight of sample (kg)
- $W_{d}$ = Dry weight after oven drying at 105°C for 24 hours (kg)

**Conversion logic:** The wet weight minus the dry weight gives the mass of water evaporated. Dividing by the original wet weight normalizes this to a percentage.

#### **Derivation 4: Volatile Solids Fraction**

$$
VS = \frac{W_{d} - W_{a}}{W_{d}} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $VS$ = Volatile solids (%)
- $W_{d}$ = Dry weight before ignition (kg)
- $W_{a}$ = Ash weight after ignition in muffle furnace at **550°C** for 1 hour (kg)

**Conversion logic:** Combustion removes organic matter as gases (CO₂, H₂O vapor). The residue (ash) is inorganic. The difference is the organic/volatile fraction.

#### **Derivation 5: Calorific Value of Mixed Waste (Weighted Average Method)**

$$
CV_{mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (f_i \times CV_i)
$$

Where:
- $CV_{mix}$ = Calorific value of mixed MSW (kJ/kg)
- $f_i$ = Fraction by weight of component $i$ (decimal)
- $CV_i$ = Calorific value of pure component $i$ (kJ/kg)
- $n$ = Total number of components

**Conversion logic:** Each component contributes to total energy based on its share of mass. The weighted sum gives the actual energy per kg of mixed waste.

### 3.2 Numerical Example (Solved in KTU Examination Style)

> **Problem (Model Q):** A Kerala municipality has a population of **2,00,000**. The per capita waste generation rate is **0.45 kg/capita/day**. The bulk density of collected waste is **350 kg/m³**. Calculate:
> (a) Total waste generated per day in tonnes.
> (b) Volume of waste collected per day in m³.
> (c) Number of compactor trucks of **5 m³ capacity** required per day if each truck makes **2 trips**.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

$$
Q_{daily} = P \times q
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q_{daily} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.45 = 90{,}000 \text{ kg/day}
$$

Converting to tonnes (1 tonne = 1000 kg):

$$
Q_{daily} = \frac{90{,}000}{1000} = 90 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Part (b):**

$$
V_{daily} = \frac{Q_{daily}}{\rho_{bulk}} = \frac{90{,}000}{350}
$$

$$
V_{daily} = 257.14 \text{ m³/day}
$$

**Part (c):**

Each truck carries $5 \text{ m³}$ and makes 2 trips, so each truck transports:

$$
V_{truck} = 5 \times 2 = 10 \text{ m³/day per truck}
$$

Number of trucks required:

$$
N_{trucks} = \frac{V_{daily}}{V_{truck}} = \frac{257.14}{10} = 25.71
$$

Since partial trucks are not possible, round up:

$$
N_{trucks} = 26 \text{ trucks/day}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** (a) 90 tonnes/day (b) 257.14 m³/day (c) 26 trucks/day

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Waste Composition Across Indian Cities

| **City / Region** | **Organic (%)** | **Plastic (%)** | **Paper (%)** | **Metal/Glass (%)** | **Inert (%)** | **MCWB (%)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Kochi (Kerala) | 45–55 | 12–18 | 8–12 | 6–10 | 15–25 | 40–55 |
| Delhi | 45–55 | 10–15 | 10–15 | 5–8 | 20–30 | 30–45 |
| Mumbai | 50–60 | 8–12 | 8–12 | 4–7 | 25–35 | 35–50 |
| Bengaluru | 50–60 | 15–22 | 10–14 | 5–8 | 10–20 | 35–50 |
| Chennai | 45–55 | 12–18 | 8–12 | 5–8 | 20–30 | 35–50 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Source-Based Classification Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[MSW Sources] --> B[Residential]
    A --> C[Commercial]
    A --> D[Institutional]
    A --> E[Construction and Demolition]
    A --> F[Industrial Non Hazardous]
    A --> G[Municipal Services]
    
    B --> B1[Kitchen Waste]
    B --> B2[Garden Waste]
    B --> B3[Paper and Plastics]
    B --> B4[Bulky Items]
    
    C --> C1[Office Waste]
    C --> C2[Hotel and Restaurant]
    C --> C3[Market Waste]
    
    D --> D1[School Waste]
    D --> D2[Hospital General Waste]
    D --> D3[Government Office Waste]
    
    E --> E1[Concrete and Brick]
    E --> E2[Wood and Steel]
    E --> E3[Soil and Rubble]
    
    F --> F1[Fly Ash]
    F --> F2[Slag]
    F --> F3[Scrap Metal]
    
    G --> G1[Street Sweeping]
    G --> G2[Park Waste]
    G --> G3[Drainage Silt]
    
    B1 --> H[Organic Fraction]
    B3 --> I[Recyclable Fraction]
    E1 --> J[Inert Fraction]
    F1 --> K[Hazardous Industrial Fraction]
```

### 4.2 Composition Analysis Workflow (Mermaid Process Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Collect MSW Sample] --> B[Quartering and Coning]
    B --> C[Physical Sorting by Component]
    C --> D1[Weigh Each Component]
    C --> D2[Oven Drying at 105C for 24h]
    D2 --> D3[Weigh Dry Sample]
    D3 --> D4[Calculate Moisture Content]
    D1 --> E[Calculate Percentage Composition]
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Final MSW Characterization Report]
    
    F --> F1[Organic Fraction]
    F --> F2[Plastic Fraction]
    F --> F3[Paper Fraction]
    F --> F4[Metal Glass Fraction]
    F --> F5[Inert Fraction]
    F --> F6[Moisture Content]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of Waste Quantification System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Population Data Input] --> B[Per Capita Rate Lookup]
    B --> C[Daily Waste Q Calculation]
    C --> D[Annual Waste Q Annual Calculation]
    D --> E[Volume Calculation using Density]
    E --> F[Vehicle Fleet Sizing]
    F --> G[Landfill Capacity Planning]
    
    H[Component Composition Data] --> I[Weighted Calorific Value]
    I --> J[Incinerator Design]
    I --> K[Composting Plant Capacity]
    
    L[Moisture Content Data] --> M[Leachate Strength Estimation]
    L --> N[Composting Process Design]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Define Municipal Solid Waste (MSW). List any four sources of MSW.**

**Model Answer:**
Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) is the everyday garbage, refuse, and discarded solid materials generated from residential, commercial, institutional, and industrial (non-hazardous) sources within a municipal jurisdiction. It includes household kitchen waste, paper, plastics, glass, metals, textiles, and yard waste, but excludes hazardous, biomedical, and radioactive waste as per the SWM Rules, 2016.

**Four sources of MSW:**
1. Residential / Domestic
2. Commercial (offices, shops, hotels)
3. Institutional (schools, hospitals, government buildings)
4. Construction & Demolition

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **What is meant by 'per capita waste generation rate'? State its typical range for Indian cities.**

**Model Answer:**
The per capita waste generation rate is the average amount of solid waste produced by one person per day, expressed in **kg/capita/day**. It is calculated by dividing the total daily waste generated by the population.

**Typical range for Indian cities:** **0.3 to 0.6 kg/capita/day** (CPCB estimate). For metro cities like Delhi and Mumbai, the value can be **0.50 to 0.60 kg/capita/day**, while smaller towns generate **0.20 to 0.40 kg/capita/day**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the major **sources of Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)**. Classify them into at least six categories with examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **physical and chemical composition of MSW**. Explain how the composition affects the choice of waste treatment method (e.g., composting vs. incineration). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Sources of MSW (7 marks)**

MSW originates from multiple sectors within a municipality. As per Tchobanoglous & Kreith, the six major sources are:

**1. Residential / Domestic Sources:**
These are the largest contributors (typically 40–60% by weight). They include kitchen waste (vegetable peels, leftover food), garden waste (leaves, grass clippings), newspapers, packaging waste, broken glass, used clothes, and bulky items like old furniture and appliances.

**2. Commercial Sources:**
Includes offices, retail shops, hotels, restaurants, and markets. Restaurant waste is typically high in organics (food waste), while office waste is paper-dominant. Market waste from vegetable/fish markets contains large fractions of biodegradable matter.

**3. Institutional Sources:**
Schools, colleges, universities, hospitals (excluding biomedical waste), government buildings, and religious centers. Paper and food waste dominate.

**4. Construction & Demolition (C&D) Sources:**
Generated during building construction, renovation, and demolition. Includes concrete, bricks, mortar, wood, metals, drywall, excavated soil, and insulation. Constitutes about 10–30% of total MSW in urban areas.

**5. Industrial Sources (Non-hazardous):**
Packaging waste, fly ash, slag from manufacturing, scrap metals, food processing by-products. Hazardous industrial waste is excluded as it falls under Hazardous Waste Management Rules.

**6. Municipal Services:**
Includes street sweeping (dust, leaves, paper), park waste (grass, branches), drainage silt, beach/pond cleaning waste, and dead animals.

#### **Part (b) — Composition of MSW & Effect on Treatment Choice (7 marks)**

**Physical Composition (by weight, typical Indian MSW):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Organic (Food + Yard)} &= 40\text{–}60\% \\
\text{Paper and Cardboard} &= 8\text{–}15\% \\
\text{Plastics} &= 10\text{–}20\% \\
\text{Glass} &= 3\text{–}5\% \\
\text{Metals} &= 2\text{–}5\% \\
\text{Textiles and Leather} &= 3\text{–}6\% \\
\text{Inert (Soil, Rubble)} &= 15\text{–}25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Chemical Composition (typical values):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Moisture Content} &= 30\text{–}55\% \\
\text{Volatile Matter} &= 40\text{–}55\% \\
\text{Fixed Carbon} &= 5\text{–}10\% \\
\text{Ash} &= 20\text{–}35\% \\
\text{Calorific Value} &= 800\text{–}1500 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Effect on Treatment Choice:**

- **High organic content (>50%) + high moisture (>40%)** → Best suited for **composting / anaerobic digestion**. Incineration is inefficient due to low calorific value and high water content.
- **High plastic fraction + low moisture** → Suitable for **incineration with energy recovery**, as plastics have high calorific value (~32,000 kJ/kg for polyethylene).
- **High inert/C&D fraction** → Sent for **landfilling or used as fill material**.
- **High recyclable fraction (paper, metal, glass, plastic)** → Routed to **Material Recovery Facility (MRF)** for recycling.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often list sources without **examples** (losing 1–2 marks). For 7-mark questions, each source MUST have an example. Also, do NOT confuse **sources** (where waste comes from) with **types** (what the waste is made of) — a common error that costs 2–3 marks.

---

> **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
>
> **(a)** A city has a population of **5,00,000**. The per capita waste generation rate is **0.55 kg/capita/day**, and the bulk density of waste is **400 kg/m³**. Calculate:
>   (i) Total waste generated per day in tonnes
>   (ii) Volume of waste collected per day
>   (iii) Number of compactor trucks of capacity **5 m³** required if each makes **3 trips/day** **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **factors affecting the quantity and composition of solid waste**. Discuss how seasonality and economic status influence MSW characteristics. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Population, $P = 5{,}00{,}000$
- Per capita waste, $q = 0.55$ kg/capita/day
- Bulk density, $\rho = 400$ kg/m³
- Truck capacity, $V_{truck} = 5$ m³
- Trips per day, $n = 3$

**(i) Total waste per day:**

$$
Q_{daily} = P \times q = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.55
$$

$$
Q_{daily} = 2{,}75{,}000 \text{ kg/day} = 275 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**(ii) Volume of waste per day:**

$$
V_{daily} = \frac{Q_{daily}}{\rho} = \frac{2{,}75{,}000}{400}
$$

$$
V_{daily} = 687.5 \text{ m³/day}
$$

**(iii) Number of trucks required:**

Each truck transports per day:

$$
V_{per\ truck} = 5 \times 3 = 15 \text{ m³}
$$

Number of trucks:

$$
N_{trucks} = \frac{687.5}{15} = 45.83 \approx 46 \text{ trucks/day}
$$

#### **Part (b) — Factors Affecting Quantity & Composition (7 marks)**

**Factors affecting QUANTITY:**

1. **Population & Density** — Higher population = more waste; higher density areas generate more total mass.
2. **Economic Status** — Affluent areas generate 2–3 times more waste than slum areas (more packaging, consumer goods).
3. **Seasonality** — Tourist seasons (e.g., Kerala monsoon or pilgrimage season) increase transient population and waste.
4. **Urbanization** — Cities generate more waste than rural areas due to packaging and consumption patterns.
5. **Lifestyle & Consumption Habits** — Higher consumption of processed foods and packaged goods increases non-organic waste.
6. **Public Awareness & Source Segregation** — Better segregation improves collection efficiency.

**Factors affecting COMPOSITION:**

1. **Food habits** — Vegetarian populations generate more vegetable-based organic waste; non-vegetarian areas generate more meat/bone waste.
2. **Seasonality** — Monsoon season increases moisture content; summer increases garden waste.
3. **Income Level** — Higher income → more paper, plastic, metal packaging.
4. **Geographic / Cultural Factors** — Coastal areas generate more fish waste; religious places generate more flower/incense waste.
5. **Industrial Activity** — Increases inorganic and packaging fraction.
6. **Local Regulations** — Bans on plastics (e.g., Kerala's single-use plastic ban) directly reduce plastic fraction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> In numerical problems, students often forget to **convert kg to tonnes** (dividing by 1000), losing 1 mark. Also, rounding up truck count to the next whole number is mandatory — partial trucks are not allowed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Definition:** MSW = everyday garbage from residential + commercial + institutional + non-hazardous industrial sources. Excludes biomedical, hazardous, and e-waste.
- **Sources:** Six categories — Residential, Commercial, Institutional, C&D, Industrial, Municipal Services.
- **Per Capita Generation (India):** 0.3–0.6 kg/capita/day (CPCB); Kochi/Kerala ≈ 0.45 kg/capita/day.
- **Density:** Loose 100–200 kg/m³; Compacted 400–700 kg/m³.
- **Moisture Content:** 30–55% (high for Indian MSW).
- **Organic Fraction:** 40–60% (highest component).
- **Calorific Value:** Indian MSW = 800–1500 kJ/kg; hence incineration is challenging.
- **Key Formulae:**
   * $Q = P \times q \times 365$ (annual waste)
   * $MC = (W_w - W_d)/W_w \times 100$
   * $VS = (W_d - W_a)/W_d \times 100$
   * $\rho = M/V$
   * $CV_{mix} = \Sigma(f_i \times CV_i)$
- **Treatment Decision Rule:** High organics + high moisture → Composting; High plastic + low moisture → Incineration; High inerts → Landfilling.
- **SWM Rules 2016:** Always cite this Indian regulatory framework when defining solid waste.
- **Common Pitfall:** Don't confuse **source** (where it comes from) with **type** (what it is made of).
- **Always convert:** kg → tonnes by dividing by **1000**; m³ ↔ litres by **1 m³ = 1000 L**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT602 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND TOXIC RISKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Storage, Collection and Transportation of Urban Solid Waste, Disposal Options: Sanitary Landfills, Composting and Its Variations, Anaerobic Digestion, Incineration and Pyrolysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Urban Solid Waste (USW) / Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)

### Formal KTU Definition
**Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** refers to the everyday items and materials that are discarded by the public from residential, commercial, institutional, and industrial sources. According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, it is a heterogeneous mixture of **biodegradable** (food waste, paper, yard trimmings), **recyclable** (plastics, glass, metals), **inert** (construction debris), and **hazardous household** (batteries, e-waste) components requiring engineered management from generation to final disposal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** MSW management follows the **Integrated Solid Waste Management (ISWM)** hierarchy — **Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Energy Recovery → Safe Disposal**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of MSW management like a city's **digestive system**:
- **Storage** at households = the **mouth** (where waste first enters)
- **Collection vehicles** = the **esophagus** (transport pathway)
- **Transfer stations** = the **stomach** (sorting and consolidation)
- **Treatment facilities** (composting, incineration, biogas plants) = the **intestines** (breaking down)
- **Sanitary landfill** = the **rectum** (final controlled disposal)

If any part fails, the whole system "indigestion" occurs — leading to public health crises, pollution, and disease outbreaks (like the plague, dengue, or cholera epidemics).

### Key Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants)
- **Per capita MSW generation in India: 0.5 kg/person/day**
- **Collection efficiency benchmark: 90–100%**
- **Composting efficiency (windrow): 30–50% mass reduction**
- **Landfill density after compaction: 500–1000 kg/m³**
- **Methane (CH₄) calorific value: 55.5 MJ/kg**
- **Incineration temperature requirement: 850°C – 1100°C**
- **Anaerobic digestion retention time: 20–40 days**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout — Municipal Solid Waste Rules (MSWR), India 2016 (revised 2024):** Every urban local body (ULB) with a population of **1 million+** must set up **waste processing facilities** and **bioremediation of legacy dumpsites** within 1–3 years (depending on population tier).

---

## 1.2 Functional Elements of MSW Management

### The 6 Functional Elements
1. **Waste Generation** — Point of origin (household, industry).
2. **Storage** — On-site containment before collection.
3. **Collection** — Door-to-door or community bin pickup.
4. **Transportation** — Movement to transfer stations or processing plants.
5. **Processing & Treatment** — Composting, anaerobic digestion, incineration, recycling.
6. **Final Disposal** — Engineered sanitary landfills.

### Storage of MSW — Definition
**Storage** refers to the temporary containment of waste at the point of generation (households) or community level (street bins) using appropriate receptacles until collection occurs. Storage duration typically ranges from **1 day to 7 days**.

**Types of Storage:**
- **Container Storage** (bins, drums, bins on wheels)
- **Degradable Bags** (compostable polylactide bags)
- **Underground Storage Systems** (modern smart cities)
- **Compartmentalized Bins** (wet waste, dry waste, hazardous)

### Collection of MSW — Definition
**Collection** is the systematic pickup and removal of stored waste from residential, commercial, and institutional premises and its transfer to the collection vehicle. Collection accounts for **50–70% of total MSW management cost**.

**Collection Methods:**
- **House-to-House Collection (HHC)** — Most preferred under MSWR 2016
- **Community Bin System** — Oldest method (now discouraged)
- **Curb-side Collection**
- **Bell System Collection** (rare, used in narrow lanes)

### Transportation of MSW — Definition
**Transportation** refers to the movement of collected waste via vehicles from collection points to **transfer stations** or **final disposal sites**. Includes primary (collection vehicle) and secondary (long-haul truck/compactor) transportation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** **Route Optimization** in transportation can reduce fuel consumption by **15–30%** and is a frequent 14-mark question topic.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 MSW Generation Rate Calculation

### Step-by-Step Logical Framework
The **per capita waste generation rate** is the foundational parameter for designing any MSW system:

1. **Quantify total waste generated per day** (W, in kg/day)
2. **Identify population served** (P)
3. **Compute generation rate** = W / P
4. **Project future generation** using population growth + per capita growth

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description / Use |
|--------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| Per Capita Waste Generation | $q = \dfrac{W}{P}$ | $q$ in kg/capita/day; $W$ = total waste (kg/day); $P$ = population |
| Total Waste Quantity | $W = q \times P \times (1 + g)^n$ | $g$ = annual growth rate; $n$ = design years |
| Volume of Storage Bin | $V = \dfrac{W_d}{\rho \times n_c}$ | $V$ = bin volume (m³); $\rho$ = waste density (kg/m³); $n_c$ = number of collections/week |
| Collection Vehicle Capacity | $N_v = \dfrac{W_d}{C_v \times n_t \times f}$ | $N_v$ = number of vehicles; $C_v$ = vehicle capacity; $n_t$ = trips/day; $f$ = utilization factor |
| Landfill Volume Required | $V_L = \dfrac{W_{annual}}{\rho_c \times (1 - s)}$ | $\rho_c$ = compacted density; $s$ = soil cover fraction (typically 15–20%) |
| Landfill Area | $A = \dfrac{V_L}{h}$ | $h$ = landfill height (m) |
| Composting Mass Reduction | $M_f = M_i \times (1 - e_c)$ | $e_c$ = efficiency of composting (0.30–0.50) |
| Anaerobic Biogas Yield | $G = V_{biogas} \times M_{VS}$ | $M_{VS}$ = mass of volatile solids; $V_{biogas}$ = 0.5 m³/kg VS |
| Incineration Energy Recovery | $E = W \times CV \times \eta$ | $CV$ = calorific value (MJ/kg); $\eta$ = boiler efficiency (~0.7) |
| Landfill Gas Generation | $G_{CH_4} = k \times M \times L_0 \times e^{-kt}$ | First-order decay model; $L_0$ = methane generation potential |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Waste Density Values:**
> - Loose waste in bin: **100–200 kg/m³**
> - In collection vehicle: **300–500 kg/m³**
> - Compacted at landfill: **500–1000 kg/m³**

## 2.2 Collection System Design — Engineering Analysis

### Step 1: Determine Generation Quantity
Calculate total waste per day using population, per capita rate, and growth projections.

### Step 2: Identify Collection Frequency
- **Daily collection**: For commercial food waste, hospitals
- **Alternate-day**: For residential biodegradable waste
- **Weekly**: For inert/dry waste streams

### Step 3: Vehicle Routing
**Routing Optimization Models**:
- **Sweep Algorithm** — Simple, used for grid-pattern cities
- **Nearest Neighbor Algorithm**
- **Clarke-Wright Savings Method** — Most efficient, KTU-recommended

### Step 4: Calculate Number of Vehicles Required
$$N_v = \dfrac{W_d}{C_v \times n_t}$$

Where:
- $W_d$ = daily waste quantity
- $C_v$ = vehicle capacity (typically 5–16 m³ for compactor trucks)
- $n_t$ = number of trips per vehicle per day

### Real-World Utility
Used by municipal corporations to:
- Reduce fuel consumption
- Minimize collection time
- Optimize workforce deployment
- Plan future fleet expansion

---

## 2.3 Disposal Option 1: Sanitary Landfills — Deep Theory

### Definition
A **Sanitary Landfill (SLF)** is an engineered facility for the **final disposal of solid waste on land** without creating nuisance or hazard to public health or environment, by employing **proper engineering principles to confine the waste to the smallest practical volume**.

### Structural Components
1. **Liner System** (clay + geomembrane HDPE)
2. **Leachate Collection & Removal System (LCRS)**
3. **Gas Collection System**
4. **Stormwater Management**
5. **Daily Cover (soil)** — **150–300 mm**
6. **Final Cover (cap)** upon closure

### Landfill Classification Methods

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **Area Method** | Used in flat terrain; waste piled in layers |
| **Trench Method** | Used in flat areas with high water table; excavated trenches |
| **Ramp/Depression Method** | Used in hilly areas or abandoned quarries |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** **Leachate** is the liquid that has percolated through the waste and extracted dissolved and suspended materials. It is **200–1000 times more polluted than domestic sewage** and is the most critical environmental concern from landfills.

### Landfill Biodegradation Phases
1. **Phase I — Aerobic (0–30 days)**: Oxygen consumption, slight temperature rise
2. **Phase II — Acidogenic (30 days–2 years)**: Acid formation, pH drops to **5.0–6.0**
3. **Phase III — Methanogenic (2–30 years)**: Methane production, pH rises to **7.0–8.0**
4. **Phase IV — Maturation (30+ years)**: Slow stabilization, gas production declines

---

## 2.4 Disposal Option 2: Composting — Deep Theory

### Definition
**Composting** is the **biological decomposition of organic biodegradable waste** under controlled aerobic conditions into a humus-like substance called **compost**, which can be used as a soil conditioner.

### Variations of Composting

| Method | Description | Aeration Mechanism | Retention Time |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Windrow Composting** | Long piles turned periodically | Mechanical turning | 4–12 weeks |
| **In-Vessel Composting** | Enclosed reactor | Forced aeration + mixing | 2–4 weeks |
| **Vermicomposting** | Earthworm-mediated | Passive aeration | 8–12 weeks |
| **Static Pile Composting** | Aerated static pile with perforated pipes | Blower-driven aeration | 3–6 weeks |
| **In-Bin Composting** | Household scale compost bin | Manual turning | 8–16 weeks |

> [!NOTE]
> **C/N Ratio Optimal Range: 25:1 – 35:1**
> - Too low C/N → ammonia release, odor
> - Too high C/N → slow decomposition
> - **Carbonaceous materials**: Dry leaves, sawdust (C/N = 500:1)
> - **Nitrogenous materials**: Food waste, manure (C/N = 15:1)

---

## 2.5 Disposal Option 3: Anaerobic Digestion — Deep Theory

### Definition
**Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** is the biological process in which **microorganisms break down biodegradable material in the absence of oxygen**, producing **biogas (60% CH₄ + 40% CO₂)** and **digestate (liquid/solid fertilizer)**.

### Process Stages
1. **Hydrolysis** — Complex polymers broken to monomers (proteins, sugars, lipids)
2. **Acidogenesis** — Monomers converted to volatile fatty acids (VFAs)
3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs converted to acetic acid, H₂, CO₂
4. **Methanogenesis** — Methanogenic archaea produce CH₄

### Operating Parameters
- **Temperature**: Mesophilic (**35–40°C**) or Thermophilic (**50–55°C**)
- **pH**: 6.8–7.5
- **Retention Time**: 20–40 days (mesophilic)
- **VS Destruction**: 40–60%
- **Biogas Yield**: **0.5–0.8 m³/kg VS**

### Types of Anaerobic Digesters
- **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)**
- **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)**
- **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB)**
- **Anaerobic Baffled Reactor (ABR)**

---

## 2.6 Disposal Option 4: Incineration — Deep Theory

### Definition
**Incineration** is the **controlled combustion of solid waste** at high temperatures (**850–1100°C**) to reduce volume by **90%** and mass by **70–75%**, with possible energy recovery as electricity or steam.

### Process Flow
1. **Waste Reception & Storage Pit** (3–5 days capacity)
2. **Feed System** — Grapple / Ram Feeder
3. **Combustion Chamber** — Primary + Secondary
4. **Energy Recovery Boiler**
5. **Air Pollution Control (APC) System**
6. **Stack Emission**

### Key Parameters
- **Calorific Value (CV)** of MSW: **1000–1500 kcal/kg** (4.2–6.3 MJ/kg)
- **Residence Time**: ≥2 seconds at ≥850°C
- **Turbulence**: Mixing Index ≥ Reynolds 50,000
- **Excess Air**: 100–150%

### Residues from Incineration
- **Bottom Ash (BA)** — 20–30% by mass — can be used in road construction
- **Fly Ash (FA)** — 2–5% — classified as hazardous (heavy metals)
- **Air Emissions** — CO₂, NOₓ, SO₂, dioxins, furans, HCl, particulate matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** For Indian MSW with high moisture (40–60%) and low CV, **co-incineration with coal** or use of **Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF)** is recommended. RDF pellet CV: **3000–4500 kcal/kg**.

---

## 2.7 Disposal Option 5: Pyrolysis — Deep Theory

### Definition
**Pyrolysis** is the **thermal decomposition of organic waste in the complete absence of oxygen** (or limited oxygen) at temperatures of **300–900°C**, producing **char, pyrolysis oil (bio-oil), and syngas (non-condensable gases)**.

### Pyrolysis vs. Incineration — Key Differences

| Parameter | Incineration | Pyrolysis |
|-----------|--------------|-----------|
| Oxygen | Excess air | None / limited |
| Temperature | 850–1100°C | 300–900°C |
| Products | Ash, gases, heat | Char, bio-oil, syngas |
| Energy Recovery | Direct heat | Multiple product streams |
| Emissions | Higher | Significantly lower |

### Pyrolysis Product Yields
- **Char (solid residue)**: 25–35%
- **Bio-oil (liquid)**: 30–45%
- **Syngas (gas)**: 20–35%
- **Composition of syngas**: H₂, CO, CH₄, CO₂

### Types of Pyrolysis
1. **Slow Pyrolysis** — Lower temp (300–500°C), long residence → more char
2. **Fast Pyrolysis** — Higher temp (500–900°C), short residence → more bio-oil
3. **Flash Pyrolysis** — Very short residence (<1 sec) → maximum bio-oil

> [!NOTE]
> **Gasification** is related but uses **controlled sub-stoichiometric oxygen** — produces predominantly syngas. **Torrefaction** is a mild pyrolysis at **200–300°C** used for biomass densification.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — MSW Generation & Collection Vehicle Calculation

### Problem Statement
A residential colony has a population of **50,000** with per capita MSW generation rate of **0.6 kg/capita/day**. The waste density is **150 kg/m³**. The municipal authority plans to collect waste on alternate days using compactor trucks of capacity **8 m³** each, making **3 trips per day**. Determine:
1. Total waste generated per day
2. Number of bins required (each bin = 1.5 m³ capacity, assuming 1 collection/day locally)
3. Number of collection vehicles required

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- Population $P = 50{,}000$
- Per capita generation $q = 0.6$ kg/capita/day
- Waste density $\rho = 150$ kg/m³
- Bin capacity $V_b = 1.5$ m³
- Truck capacity $C_v = 8$ m³
- Trips per truck per day $n_t = 3$

**Step 1: Total Waste Generated Per Day**

$$
W_d = q \times P
$$

$$
W_d = 0.6 \times 50{,}000 = 30{,}000 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 2: Total Volume of Waste Per Day**

$$
V_{total} = \dfrac{W_d}{\rho}
$$

$$
V_{total} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{150} = 200 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 3: Total Collection (Alternate Day, so design load = 2 × daily)**

$$
W_{design} = 2 \times 30{,}000 = 60{,}000 \text{ kg (per collection cycle)}
$$

$$
V_{design} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{150} = 400 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (per collection cycle)}
$$

**Step 4: Number of Community Bins Required**

$$
N_{bin} = \dfrac{V_{design}}{V_b}
$$

$$
N_{bin} = \dfrac{400}{1.5} = 266.67 \approx 267 \text{ bins}
$$

**Step 5: Number of Collection Vehicles Required**

$$
N_v = \dfrac{V_{total}}{C_v \times n_t}
$$

$$
N_v = \dfrac{200}{8 \times 3} = \dfrac{200}{24} = 8.33 \approx 9 \text{ vehicles}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision:** Always round **up** for vehicle count (use **9 vehicles**) to ensure full coverage; round **up** for bins (use **267 bins**) to avoid overflow.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Sanitary Landfill Area Calculation

### Problem Statement
A city generates **500 tonnes/day** of MSW with bulk density **500 kg/m³**. The city wishes to design a sanitary landfill for **20 years**. Assume:
- Daily soil cover = **15% of waste volume**
- Annual MSW growth rate = **2%**
- Landfill height = **15 m**

Calculate the required landfill area.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- Daily waste = 500 tonnes = **500,000 kg/day**
- Compacted density $\rho_c = 500$ kg/m³
- Soil cover fraction $s = 0.15$
- Design period $n = 20$ years
- Height $h = 15$ m
- Growth rate $g = 2\%$

**Step 1: Total Waste Per Year (Year 1)**

$$
W_1 = 500 \times 365 = 182{,}500 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

**Step 2: Volume of Compacted Waste Per Year (Year 1)**

$$
V_{w1} = \dfrac{W_1}{\rho_c} = \dfrac{182{,}500}{0.5} = 365{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}
$$

(Note: 500 kg/m³ = 0.5 tonnes/m³)

**Step 3: Total Volume with Daily Cover**

$$
V_{t1} = V_{w1} \times (1 + s) = 365{,}000 \times 1.15 = 419{,}750 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Geometric Progression for 20 Years with 2% Growth**

$$
V_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{20} V_{t1} \times (1.02)^{i-1}
$$

$$
V_{total} = V_{t1} \times \dfrac{(1.02)^{20} - 1}{0.02}
$$

$$
(1.02)^{20} = 1.4859
$$

$$
V_{total} = 419{,}750 \times \dfrac{1.4859 - 1}{0.02}
$$

$$
V_{total} = 419{,}750 \times 24.297
$$

$$
V_{total} = 10{,}198{,}643 \text{ m}^3 \approx 10.2 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5: Landfill Area Required**

$$
A = \dfrac{V_{total}}{h}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{10{,}198{,}643}{15} = 679{,}909 \text{ m}^2 \approx 68 \text{ hectares}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Disposal Methods

| Parameter | Sanitary Landfill | Composting | Anaerobic Digestion | Incineration | Pyrolysis |
|-----------|------------------|-----------|---------------------|--------------|-----------|
| **Process** | Engineered burial | Aerobic bio-decomposition | Anaerobic bio-decomposition | Controlled combustion | Thermal decomposition (no O₂) |
| **Volume Reduction** | 100% (no reduction) | 40–60% | 50–70% | 85–90% | 70–80% |
| **Mass Reduction** | 100% | 30–50% | 40–60% | 70–75% | 60–70% |
| **Energy Recovery** | LFG to power | None direct | Biogas (~55 MJ/m³) | Heat/electricity | Char/oil/syngas |
| **Residue** | Leachate, LFG | Compost (usable) | Digestate (usable) | Bottom ash, fly ash | Char (usable) |
| **Land Requirement** | Very high | Moderate | Low | Very low | Low |
| **Capital Cost** | Moderate | Low | High | Very high | High |
| **Operating Cost** | Low | Low | Moderate | High | Moderate |
| **Environmental Risk** | Leachate, GHG | Low if well-managed | Methane leak risk | Air emissions (dioxins) | Lowest emissions |
| **Suitability for Indian MSW** | High | High | Moderate | Low (high moisture) | Moderate |
| **Retention Time** | Years | 4–12 weeks | 20–40 days | Minutes-hours | Minutes-hours |
| **KTU Mark Frequency** | Very High | High | High | High | Moderate |

---

## 3.4 Anaerobic Digestion — Biogas Yield Derivation

### Problem Statement
Calculate the biogas yield from **1 tonne** of food waste having:
- Total Solids (TS) = **25%**
- Volatile Solids (VS) = **85% of TS**
- Biogas yield = **0.5 m³/kg VS**
- Methane content = **60%**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate TS Mass**

$$
M_{TS} = 1000 \times 0.25 = 250 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate VS Mass**

$$
M_{VS} = M_{TS} \times 0.85 = 250 \times 0.85 = 212.5 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Total Biogas Yield**

$$
V_{biogas} = M_{VS} \times 0.5 = 212.5 \times 0.5 = 106.25 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Methane Volume**

$$
V_{CH_4} = V_{biogas} \times 0.60 = 106.25 \times 0.60 = 63.75 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Energy Content (Calorific Value of CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³)**

$$
E_{CH_4} = V_{CH_4} \times 35.8 = 63.75 \times 35.8 = 2282.25 \text{ MJ}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** 1 tonne of food waste can produce enough biogas to power a **3 kW generator for ~12 hours**, demonstrating AD's energy recovery potential.

---

## 3.5 Incineration Energy Recovery Calculation

### Problem Statement
An incinerator processes **100 tonnes/day** of MSW with calorific value of **1500 kcal/kg**. Boiler efficiency is **70%**. Calculate daily steam and electricity generation.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Daily Energy Input**

$$
E_{input} = 100{,}000 \times 1500 = 150 \times 10^6 \text{ kcal/day}
$$

**Step 2: Convert to MJ (1 kcal = 4.184 kJ)**

$$
E_{input} = 150 \times 10^6 \times 4.184 = 627.6 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/day} = 627.6 \text{ GJ/day}
$$

**Step 3: Energy Recovered (70% efficiency)**

$$
E_{output} = 627.6 \times 0.70 = 439.3 \text{ GJ/day}
$$

**Step 4: Steam Generation (1 kg steam ≈ 2.3 MJ at typical conditions)**

$$
M_{steam} = \dfrac{439.3 \times 10^3}{2.3 \times 10^3} = 191 \text{ tonnes steam/day}
$$

**Step 5: Electricity (assume steam-to-electricity 25%)**

$$
E_{electricity} = 439.3 \times 0.25 = 109.8 \text{ GJ/day}
$$

$$
E_{electricity} = \dfrac{109.8 \times 10^6}{3600} = 30.5 \text{ MWh/day}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Show units in every step. Examiners award marks for unit consistency and dimensional analysis.
