## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Moisture Content from a Typical MSW Sample

**Problem Statement:** A fresh municipal solid waste sample weighing **1,250 g** is dried in a hot air oven at **105 ± 5 °C** for 24 hours. The dried residue weighs **640 g**. Calculate the **moisture content (% by wet basis)**.

**Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Identify known values from the problem.

$$
W_w = 1250 \text{ g} \qquad \text{and} \qquad W_d = 640 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the moisture content formula.

$$
\text{MC} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator (mass of water evaporated).

$$
W_w - W_d = 1250 - 640 = 610 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 4:** Divide by wet weight.

$$
\frac{610}{1250} = 0.488
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to percentage by multiplying by 100.

$$
\text{MC} = 0.488 \times 100 = 48.8\%
$$

**Step 6:** Engineering Interpretation.

> [!NOTE]
> An MC of **48.8%** is typical of **freshly generated Indian MSW** (range 40–60%). Such waste is unsuitable for direct incineration — it must either be **mechanically dewatered** or **co-fired with dry RDF (Refuse-Derived Fuel)** to maintain the 45–55% optimum range. If MC > 60%, combustion becomes self-unsustaining and auxiliary fuel is needed.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Volatile Solids (Combustible Fraction)

**Problem Statement:** The dried sample from Derivation 1 (640 g) is then placed in a **muffle furnace at 550 ± 10 °C** for 2 hours. The ash residue weighs **215 g**. Calculate the **Volatile Solids (%)** and **Ash Content (%)**.

**Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Known values.

$$
W_d = 640 \text{ g}, \qquad W_a = 215 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into VS formula.

$$
\text{VS} = \frac{W_d - W_a}{W_d} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator (mass of organic matter burned off).

$$
W_d - W_a = 640 - 215 = 425 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute VS.

$$
\text{VS} = \frac{425}{640} \times 100 = 66.41\%
$$

**Step 5:** Compute Ash Content.

$$
\text{Ash} = \frac{W_a}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{215}{640} \times 100 = 33.59\%
$$

**Step 6:** Validation — VS + Ash should equal 100% (within rounding).

$$
66.41 + 33.59 = 100.00\% \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 7:** Engineering Interpretation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A VS of **66.41%** indicates a **biodegradable-rich waste stream**, suitable for **composting or anaerobic digestion** rather than landfilling. Indian MSW typically has VS between **40–60%**; the slightly higher value here suggests food and yard waste dominance. High VS wastes generate more landfill gas (methane), which is a **climate liability but also an energy asset** if captured.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Per Capita Waste Generation

**Problem Statement:** A medium-sized Indian town has a population of **2,50,000** (2.5 lakh). The municipal authority records an average daily waste collection of **175 metric tonnes (MT)** over a 30-day month. Some days had uncollected waste; field surveys indicate a **collection efficiency of 70%**. Calculate the **actual per capita waste generation rate (kg/person/day)**.

**Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Account for collection inefficiency. The **collected** mass is only 70% of the **generated** mass.

$$
W_{\text{generated}} = \frac{W_{\text{collected}}}{\text{Collection Efficiency}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values (using daily collected mass).

$$
\text{Daily collected} = \frac{175{,}000 \text{ kg}}{30 \text{ days}} = 5833.33 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute actual daily generated waste.

$$
W_{\text{generated}} = \frac{5833.33}{0.70} = 8333.33 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute per capita rate.

$$
q = \frac{W_{\text{generated}}}{P} = \frac{8333.33}{2{,}50{,}000}
$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation.

$$
q = 0.03333 \text{ kg/person/day} \approx 0.33 \text{ kg/person/day}
$$

**Step 6:** Engineering Interpretation.

> [!NOTE]
> A per capita rate of **0.33 kg/person/day** is consistent with **Tier-2 Indian cities** (typical range 0.2–0.5 kg). Compare this with **metro cities** like Mumbai (~0.55 kg) or **developed nations** (~1.5–2.0 kg). The value is a **design input** for sizing collection vehicles, transfer stations, and processing facilities.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Landfill Gas (LFG) Generation by IPCC First-Order Decay Model

**Problem Statement:** A sanitary landfill receives **1,00,000 tonnes** of MSW in Year 1. The waste has a **DOC (Degradable Organic Carbon) of 0.15** and **DOC$_f$ (fraction that actually decomposes) of 0.77**. The fraction of methane in LFG ($F$) is **0.5**. Calculate the **mass of methane generated in Year 1** (in tonnes).

**Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** State the IPCC methane generation equation.

$$
Q_{\text{CH}_4} = M \times \text{DOC} \times \text{DOC}_f \times F \times \frac{16}{12}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute all values.

$$
Q_{\text{CH}_4} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15 \times 0.77 \times 0.5 \times \frac{16}{12}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply step by step.

$$
1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15 = 15{,}000
$$

$$
15{,}000 \times 0.77 = 11{,}550
$$

$$
11{,}550 \times 0.5 = 5775
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the molecular weight conversion factor.

$$
\frac{16}{12} = 1.3333
$$

**Step 5:** Final methane mass.

$$
Q_{\text{CH}_4} = 5775 \times 1.3333 = 7700 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to volume at STP (since 1 tonne CH₄ ≈ 1496 m³ at NTP, but for engineering use 1 t CH₄ ≈ 1393 m³).

$$
V_{\text{CH}_4} = 7700 \times 1393 = 1.0726 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 7:** Energy equivalent (1 m³ CH₄ ≈ 35.17 MJ).

$$
E_{\text{CH}_4} = 1.0726 \times 10^7 \times 35.17 = 3.77 \times 10^8 \text{ MJ} \approx 104{,}700 \text{ MWh}
$$

**Step 8:** Engineering Interpretation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single landfill, just from Year-1 waste, can theoretically generate enough methane to power ~10,000 Indian households annually. **Monitoring LFG flow and composition (CH₄, CO₂, H₂S) is thus not just an environmental compliance step — it is the foundation of a profitable landfill gas-to-energy (LFGTE) project.**

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sampling Plan for Different Waste Streams

| Waste Stream | Sampling Frequency | Minimum Sample Size | Key Parameters to Monitor | Regulatory Limit Reference |
|--------------|--------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Raw MSW (Transfer Station)** | Seasonal (4×/yr) | 100–200 kg | MC, VS, density, composition | CPCB 2016 Rules |
| **Landfill Leachate** | Quarterly + post-rain event | 1 L grab + 24-h composite | pH, BOD, COD, NH₃-N, Cl⁻, Pb, Cd, Hg | CPCB leachate standards (Schedule II) |
| **Landfill Gas** | Continuous (online GC) | Continuous | CH₄, CO₂, O₂, H₂S, flow rate | CPCB emission norms |
| **Compost (Output)** | Batch-wise | 1–2 kg | C:N ratio, stability, fecal coliforms, heavy metals | FCO 1985 (amended 2009) |
| **Incinerator Ash** | Daily composite | 500 g | TCLP metals (Pb, Cd, Cr, Hg) | Hazardous Waste M&H Rules, 2016 |
| **Biomedical Waste** | Daily | As per BMW Rules | Autoclave efficiency, color-coding compliance | BMW Rules 2016 (amended 2018) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1.** *Define Moisture Content of MSW. State the standard test method and the formula used for its determination.*

**Model Answer:**

Moisture Content (MC) of municipal solid waste is the **mass of water present per unit mass of wet waste**, expressed as a percentage on a wet basis. It is a critical physical parameter that affects the **combustion behaviour, biodegradability, and leachate generation potential** of the waste.

$$
\text{MC} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100
$$

where $W_w$ is the wet weight and $W_d$ is the weight after oven drying.

**Standard Test Method:** **ASTM D2216 / IS 9234** — the sample is dried in a **hot air oven at 105 ± 5 °C for 24 hours** until constant mass is achieved. Typical MC for Indian MSW ranges between **40–60%**, while European MSW is much drier (~20–30%) due to source segregation and lower food waste fraction.

---

**Q2.** *What is the significance of the C:N ratio in solid waste monitoring? State the optimum value for composting.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Carbon-to-Nitrogen (C:N) ratio** is the mass ratio of total carbon to total Kjeldahl nitrogen in the waste, both expressed on a **dry weight basis**. It governs the **microbial activity** during composting and anaerobic digestion.

$$
\text{C:N} = \frac{\%\text{Carbon}}{\%\text{Nitrogen}}
$$

**Optimum Range for Composting:** **25:1 to 30:1** (CPCB/FCO standards).

- **C:N < 20** → excess nitrogen, leads to **ammonia volatilization, odour, and nitrogen loss**.
- **C:N > 35** → carbon excess, microbes consume nitrogen rapidly, causing **slow composting and nitrogen immobilization**.

The ratio is monitored at **start, mid, and end of composting** to ensure process stability and a mature product. Municipal sewage sludge (low C:N ~10:1) is often **co-composted with sawdust or straw** (high C:N ~500:1) to balance the mix.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU End-Semester Examination — Model Question Paper Pattern, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Monitoring Methodology & Physical-Chemical Analysis*

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 3 Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **sampling and characterization methodology** for municipal solid waste as per **ASTM D5231** and CPCB guidelines. Include the **quartering procedure** with a stepwise description.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 1,500 g fresh MSW sample is oven-dried at 105 °C for 24 hours; the dried mass is **675 g**. The dried sample is then ignited in a muffle furnace at 550 °C for 2 hours; the ash residue weighs **230 g**. Calculate **(i) Moisture Content**, **(ii) Volatile Solids**, **(iii) Ash Content**, and comment on the suitability of this waste for **composting versus incineration**.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The ASTM D5231-92 procedure is the **internationally accepted protocol** for sampling and characterization of unprocessed MSW, adapted by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** under the Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016.

**Step 1 — Site Selection & Sampling Plan**
- Identify representative sampling points: **collection vehicle**, **transfer station**, **processing facility**.
- Decide **minimum sample mass**: ≥ **100 kg** for seasonal studies; ≥ 200 kg for annual characterization.
- Document **sampling date, time, weather, source segregation status**.

**Step 2 — Sample Collection (Field)**
- Use **front-end loader** or **mechanical grab sampler** to draw **8–10 incremental samples** from different locations on the waste heap.
- Combine into one **composite sample** of ≥ 100 kg.
- Weigh composite sample immediately using a **calibrated platform balance**.

**Step 3 — Manual Sorting & Composition Analysis**
- Spread the composite on a **clean sorting table** (typically 3 m × 5 m).
- Manually segregate into categories: **organic (food + yard), paper, plastics, glass, metals, textiles, inert, hazardous**.
- Weigh each category separately. Record as **% by wet weight**.

**Step 4 — Coning and Quartering (Sample Reduction)**
- **Step 4a:** Mix the composite thoroughly by repeated shoveling.
- **Step 4b:** Pile into a **conical heap**.
- **Step 4c:** Flatten the cone into a circular disc of uniform thickness.
- **Step 4d:** Divide the disc into **4 equal quadrants** with cross-shaped dividers.
- **Step 4e:** Discard **two opposite quarters** (e.g., NW and SE); retain the other two (NE and SW).
- **Step 4f:** Combine the retained quarters and **repeat the coning and quartering** process until the sample is reduced to **1–2 kg**.
- **Step 4g:** This 1–2 kg sample is the **laboratory sample**, representative of the original 100+ kg.

**Step 5 — Laboratory Sub-Analysis**
- **Moisture Content** — Oven drying 105 °C, 24 h (ASTM D2216).
- **Volatile Solids** — Muffle furnace 550 °C, 2 h (ASTM D2974).
- **Calorific Value** — Bomb calorimeter (ASTM D5865).
- **Heavy Metals** — Acid digestion (HNO₃:HCl = 3:1, USEPA 3050B) followed by AAS/ICP-OES.
- **C:N ratio** — Walkley-Black for carbon; Kjeldahl for nitrogen.

**Step 6 — Reporting**
- Generate a **standardized monitoring report** in the format prescribed by CPCB.
- Submit to **SPCB online portal** as per **SWM Rules 2016** (Schedule III).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Wet sample weight $W_w = 1500$ g
- Oven-dried weight $W_d = 675$ g
- Ash weight $W_a = 230$ g

**(i) Moisture Content:**

$$
\text{MC} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 = \frac{1500 - 675}{1500} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{MC} = \frac{825}{1500} \times 100 = 55.0\%
$$

**(ii) Volatile Solids:**

$$
\text{VS} = \frac{W_d - W_a}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{675 - 230}{675} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{VS} = \frac{445}{675} \times 100 = 65.93\%
$$

**(iii) Ash Content:**

$$
\text{Ash} = \frac{W_a}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{230}{675} \times 100 = 34.07\%
$$

**Validation:** $65.93 + 34.07 = 100.00\%$ ✓

**Comment on End-Use Suitability:**

| Parameter | Value | Indication |
|-----------|-------|------------|
| Moisture Content | 55% | High — exceeds incineration optimum (45–55%) |
| Volatile Solids | 65.93% | High organic content — biodegradable |
| Ash Content | 34.07% | Moderate |

**Conclusion:**
- **Suitability for Composting:** **Highly Suitable** ✓ — High VS (>60%) and biodegradable organic content make this waste an excellent feedstock for aerobic composting or anaerobic digestion.
- **Suitability for Incineration:** **Marginal** ✗ — MC of 55% is at the upper limit; direct incineration will require **auxiliary fuel support** or **pre-drying via RDF (Refuse-Derived Fuel) processing**.
- **Recommendation:** Route this waste to a **composting facility** with energy recovery; if landfilling is unavoidable, ensure a **leachate collection and LFG capture system** is installed due to high biodegradability.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Leachate Monitoring & Landfill Gas Quantification*

`[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Module 3 Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **leachate monitoring protocol** for a municipal sanitary landfill as per CPCB. List the **key parameters**, their **regulatory limits**, and the **analytical methods** employed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sanitary landfill receives **50,000 tonnes of MSW per year**. The waste has **DOC = 0.18**, **DOC$_f$ = 0.77**, and the methane fraction ($F$) in landfill gas is **0.5**. Using the **IPCC first-order model**, calculate the **annual methane generation** in tonnes and convert it to **electrical energy in MWh** (assume 35% electrical conversion efficiency and CH₄ calorific value of 55.5 MJ/kg).

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Leachate Monitoring Protocol (CPCB Schedule II, SWM Rules 2016):**

Leachate is the **liquid that has percolated through the waste mass**, carrying dissolved and suspended contaminants. Monitoring is essential to prevent **groundwater pollution** — a major public health and legal liability.

**Step 1 — Sampling Point Identification**
- **Raw leachate sump** (before treatment) — captures landfill-generated liquid.
- **Treated effluent outlet** — after the Leachate Treatment Plant (LTP).
- **Downstream groundwater monitoring wells** (at least 3) — at distances 50 m, 100 m, and 200 m from landfill boundary.

**Step 2 — Sampling Frequency**
- **Quarterly** for routine compliance.
- **After every major rainfall event** (>25 mm in 24 hours).
- **Monthly** during the first 2 years of operation (active phase generates more leachate).

**Step 3 — Sample Type**
- **Grab sample** for pH, temperature, conductivity (instantaneous parameters).
- **24-hour composite sample** for BOD, COD, metals, nutrients.

**Step 4 — Key Parameters & Regulatory Limits (CPCB Schedule II):**

| Parameter | Unit | Regulatory Limit | Analytical Method |
|-----------|------|------------------|-------------------|
| pH | — | 5.5 – 9.0 | Electrometric (APHA 4500-H⁺) |
| BOD₅ (3 days, 27 °C) | mg/L | ≤ 30 | Winkler / DO probe (APHA 5210) |
| COD | mg/L | ≤ 250 | Open reflux dichromate (APHA 5220) |
| Total Suspended Solids (TSS) | mg/L | ≤ 100 | Gravimetric (APHA 2540) |
| Ammoniacal Nitrogen (NH₃-N) | mg/L | ≤ 50 | Nesslerization / Distillation |
| Chloride (Cl⁻) | mg/L | ≤ 1000 | Argentometric titration |
| Lead (Pb) | mg/L | ≤ 0.1 | AAS (APHA 3111) / ICP |
| Cadmium (Cd) | mg/L | ≤ 0.05 | AAS / ICP |
| Total Chromium (Cr) | mg/L | ≤ 0.05 | AAS |
| Mercury (Hg) | mg/L | ≤ 0.01 | Cold-vapor AAS |
| Arsenic (As) | mg/L | ≤ 0.05 | AAS / SDDC |

**Step 5 — Quality Assurance**
- **Duplicate samples** (10% frequency) for precision.
- **Spike recovery tests** (80–120% acceptable) for accuracy.
- **Chain-of-custody documentation** for legal defensibility.

**Step 6 — Reporting**
- Submit quarterly to **SPCB** in Form III.
- Maintain 5-year archival of all monitoring records.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Annual waste input $M = 50{,}000$ tonnes
- $\text{DOC} = 0.18$
- $\text{DOC}_f = 0.77$
- $F = 0.5$
- Electrical efficiency $\eta = 35\% = 0.35$
- CH₄ calorific value $\text{CV}_{\text{CH}_4} = 55.5$ MJ/kg

**Step 1:** Apply the IPCC methane generation equation.

$$
Q_{\text{CH}_4} = M \times \text{DOC} \times \text{DOC}_f \times F \times \frac{16}{12}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
Q_{\text{CH}_4} = 50{,}000 \times 0.18 \times 0.77 \times 0.5 \times \frac{16}{12}
$$

**Step 3:** Sequential multiplication.

$$
50{,}000 \times 0.18 = 9000
$$

$$
9000 \times 0.77 = 6930
$$

$$
6930 \times 0.5 = 3465
$$

$$
\frac{16}{12} = 1.3333
$$

**Step 4:** Final methane mass.

$$
Q_{\text{CH}_4} = 3465 \times 1.3333 = 4620 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert tonnes to kg.

$$
M_{\text{CH}_4} = 4620 \times 1000 = 4.62 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/year}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute total chemical energy.

$$
E_{\text{thermal}} = M_{\text{CH}_4} \times \text{CV}_{\text{CH}_4} = 4.62 \times 10^6 \times 55.5 = 2.564 \times 10^8 \text{ MJ/year}
$$

**Step 7:** Convert MJ to MWh (1 MWh = 3600 MJ).

$$
E_{\text{thermal in MWh}} = \frac{2.564 \times 10^8}{3600} = 71{,}228 \text{ MWh/year}
$$

**Step 8:** Apply 35% electrical conversion efficiency.

$$
E_{\text{electrical}} = 71{,}228 \times 0.35 = 24{,}930 \text{ MWh/year}
$$

**Step 9:** Final answer.

$$
\boxed{Q_{\text{CH}_4} = 4620 \text{ t/yr} \;\;\rightarrow\;\; E_{\text{electrical}} \approx 24{,}930 \text{ MWh/year}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
This landfill can generate enough electricity to power approximately **2,400 Indian households** (assuming 1 household ≈ 10,000 kWh/year). At a **feed-in tariff of ₹3/kWh**, the annual revenue is roughly **₹7.5 crore**, making LFG-to-energy monitoring not just a compliance step but a **commercial asset**. Under the **Carbon Credit (CDM)** mechanism, methane destruction also earns **1 tonne CO₂-equivalent credit per ~21 tonnes of CH₄ avoided**, adding further revenue.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Solid Waste Monitoring Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the molecular weight conversion factor 16/12 in the IPCC equation.** Many students calculate carbon, not methane — losing 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Mixing up "dry basis" vs "wet basis"** moisture content. Indian CPCB uses **wet basis**; some textbooks quote dry basis. Always state which basis you are using.
> 3. **Not naming the standard test method** (ASTM/IS/EPA) alongside the parameter. Examiners explicitly look for this — it differentiates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.
> 4. **Ignoring collection efficiency** in per capita waste generation problems — real-world data is always partial; if not accounted for, the answer is **systematically underestimated**.
> 5. **Coning and quartering description** is often vague. KTU expects you to mention: conical heap → flatten → divide into 4 → discard opposite quarters → repeat. Skipping any step loses marks.
> 6. **Confusing FCO standards for compost** (heavy metals, fecal coliform limits) with CPCB standards. They are different! FCO is for **end-product compost quality**, CPCB is for **environmental discharge**.
> 7. **Missing units** in final answers (especially in MWh vs MJ conversions) — silent killers of marks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Monitoring of Solid Wastes**

- **Moisture Content (MC)** = $\dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$; dry at **105 °C, 24 h** (ASTM D2216); typical Indian MSW: **40–60%**.
- **Volatile Solids (VS)** = $\dfrac{W_d - W_a}{W_d} \times 100$; ash at **550 °C, 2 h** (ASTM D2974); VS > 60% → biodegradable-rich.
- **Bulk Density** = mass/volume; affects truck capacity and landfill compaction design.
- **C:N Ratio** for composting = **25:1 to 30:1**; Kjeldahl N, Walkley-Black C.
- **Calorific Value** by bomb calorimeter (ASTM D5865); good for incineration if CV > **1500 kcal/kg**.
- **Per Capita Waste Generation** = Total waste / (Population × Time); Tier-2 Indian cities: **0.2–0.5 kg/person/day**.
- **Leachate Monitoring** parameters: pH, BOD, COD, TSS, NH₃-N, Cl⁻, heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Cr, Hg, As); per CPCB Schedule II.
- **Landfill Gas Composition**: ~50% CH₄, ~50% CO₂, traces of H₂S, NMOCs; monitoring via **online GC**.
- **IPCC Methane Equation**: $Q_{\text{CH}_4} = M \times \text{DOC} \times \text{DOC}_f \times F \times \dfrac{16}{12}$.
- **TCLP (Method 1311)** classifies a waste as **hazardous** based on extract toxicity.
- **Sampling Standards**: ASTM D5231 (MSW characterization), ASTM D2216 (moisture), ASTM D2974 (VS), IS 9234 (Indian equivalent), EPA SW-846.
- **Quartering Procedure**: Conical heap → flatten → 4 quarters → discard 2 opposite → repeat until 1–2 kg lab sample.
- **Heavy Metals** monitored in MSW and leachate: Pb, Cd, Cr, Hg, As, Ni, Cu, Zn; methods: AAS, ICP-OES, ICP-MS.
- **Statistical QA**: 95% confidence interval, Dixon Q-test for outliers, duplicate and spike recovery checks.
- **Regulatory Framework**: SWM Rules 2016 (revised), Hazardous Waste M&H Rules 2016, BMW Rules 2016, Plastic Waste Rules 2022, EIA Notification 2006.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazardous Wastes - Definition and Classification, Health and Environmental Effects, Treatment, Disposal and Management, Legal Framework for Hazardous Waste Management in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT602 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND TOXIC RISKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hazardous Wastes - Definition and Classification, Health and Environmental Effects, Treatment, Disposal and Management, Legal Framework for Hazardous Waste Management in India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Hazardous Waste

A **hazardous waste** is any waste material — solid, liquid, gas, or sludge — that possesses **inherent dangerous properties** (toxic, flammable, reactive, corrosive, explosive, infectious, or radioactive) and which, because of its quantity, concentration, or physical, chemical, or infectious characteristics, may pose a substantial present or potential hazard to human health or the environment when improperly treated, stored, transported, or disposed of.

**Per the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA), USA:**
> A solid waste is hazardous if it is *listed* specifically by the USEPA (e.g., F-list, K-list, P-list, U-list) **OR** if it exhibits one or more of four **characteristic properties**: Ignitability (D001), Corrosivity (D002), Reactivity (D003), or Toxicity (D004–D043).

**Per the Indian MoEF&CC (Hazardous and Other Wastes — Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016 (HOWM Rules 2016):**
> *"Hazardous waste means any waste which by reason of characteristics such as toxic, flammable, explosive, reactive, irritant, corrosive or radioactive, causes danger or is likely to cause danger to health or environment, whether alone or in combination with other waste or substances."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (must be memorised verbatim):** Hazardous wastes are discarded substances that, owing to their **quantity, concentration, or chemical/physical/biological nature**, pose a real or potential threat to human health and the ecology. This definition has **three components**: source, characteristic, and impact.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your **kitchen dustbin** on a normal day: banana peels, paper, vegetable trimmings — these are **municipal solid waste (MSW)**, largely biodegradable and harmless. Now imagine a **chemistry laboratory dustbin** that contains spent solvents, broken mercury thermometers, lead-contaminated glassware, and cyanide residues. This is a "ticking time-bomb" dustbin. If the sweeper dumps it in the same landfill as the kitchen waste, the cyanide can leach into groundwater, the solvents can evaporate and cause explosions, and the mercury can bioaccumulate in fish that a farmer downstream uses for food.

> **Hazardous waste is therefore "special-category garbage" that demands a completely separate, scientifically engineered lifecycle** — different bins, different trucks, different treatment plants, and a different legal ledger.

A useful rule-of-thumb cited in KTU answer-sheets is the **3-D Test**:
1. **Dangerous** to handle?
2. **Diffusable** into the environment?
3. **Disruptive** to ecological cycles?

If "yes" to any one, the waste stream is **hazardous**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Reference Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Numerical Thresholds (USEPA RCRA — to be remembered for KTU numerical questions):**
> - **Ignitability (D001):** Flash point **< 60 °C (140 °F)** for liquids; for solids, easily ignitable and burns so vigorously that it creates a hazard.
> - **Corrosivity (D002):** Aqueous pH **≤ 2.0** or **≥ 12.5**, OR it corrodes steel at a rate **> 6.35 mm/year** at 55 °C.
> - **Reactivity (D003):** Unstable, reacts violently with water, generates toxic gases, or is a strong oxidizer.
> - **Toxicity (D004–D043):** Fails the **Toxicity Characteristic Leaching Procedure (TCLP)** for any of **40 contaminants** at the regulatory threshold (e.g., Lead 5 mg/L, Mercury 0.2 mg/L, Cadmium 1 mg/L, Arsenic 5 mg/L).

### 1.4 Categories of Hazardous Waste Generators (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Generator Type | Quantity Generated per Month | Indian Classification |
|---|---|---|
| **Large / Major** | > 20 tonnes | Category A (CPCB registered) |
| **Medium** | 5 – 20 tonnes | Category B (State PCB registered) |
| **Small** | < 5 tonnes | Category C (Local body) |

> [!TIP]
> A KTU favourite question: *"Differentiate hazardous waste from biomedical waste and e-waste."* — answer must mention that **e-waste is governed by E-Waste (Management) Rules 2016** and **biomedical waste by BMWM Rules 2016**, both of which are **sub-categories of hazardous waste** under the umbrella HOWM Rules 2016.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Hazardous Waste (Multi-Tiered Approach)

A KTU question on classification **must** cover at least three orthogonal systems. The university expects layered answers.

#### 2.1.1 Classification by **Source / Origin**

| Class | Examples | Typical Industries |
|---|---|---|
| **Industrial** | Spent solvents, heavy-metal sludges, paint residues, fly-ash | Petrochemical, electroplating, dyeing, leather |
| **Biomedical / Healthcare** | Sharps, pathological waste, expired drugs, cultures | Hospitals, labs, blood banks |
| **Domestic / Household** | Batteries (lead-acid, Li-ion), paints, pesticides, CFLs (containing Hg) | Urban households |
| **Agricultural** | Expired pesticides, herbicide containers, BHC/HCH dumps | Farming sector |
| **Nuclear / Radioactive** | Spent fuel rods, contaminated PPE, radionuclide solutions | Nuclear power, research reactors |
| **E-Waste** | PCBs, CRTs, capacitors (PCB oil), mobile phones | IT, consumer electronics |
| **Construction & Demolition (C&D)** | Asbestos, lead-based paints, contaminated soil | Real estate, infrastructure |
| **Sludges** | Tannery sludge, refinery ETP sludge, paper-mill sludge | Common across industries |

#### 2.1.2 Classification by **USEPA Characteristic (4-Prong Test)**

1. **Ignitability (I)** – Fire-hazardous wastes (e.g., used solvents, petroleum sludges).
2. **Corrosivity (C)** – Acidic / alkaline wastes (e.g., spent pickle liquor from steel industry).
3. **Reactivity (R)** – Explosive or shock-sensitive wastes (e.g., discarded explosives, peroxides).
4. **Toxicity (T)** – Leachate-toxic wastes (e.g., heavy-metal contaminated soil).

> Mnemonic: **"I CaRe for Toxic materials"** → **I-C-R-T**.

#### 2.1.3 Classification by **Indian CPCB (Schedule-I to Schedule-III of HOWM Rules 2016)**

- **Schedule-I:** Part A — 36 categories of industrial hazardous waste streams; Part B — Concentrated Lead-acid Battery waste; Part C — Other batteries; Part D — Waste Oil; Part E — Waste tyre; Part F — Discarded Containers; Part G — Paint Sludge.
- **Schedule-II:** List of constituents/parameters to characterize hazardous waste (a 130-item list — heavy metals, organochlorines, pesticides, VOCs, etc.).
- **Schedule-III:** Management of waste oils, lead-acid batteries, used batteries, waste tyres.

#### 2.1.4 Classification by **UN / Basel Convention (Transboundary Movement)**

The Basel Convention (1989) classifies waste into **Annex-IX categories** (hazardous) and **Annex-II categories** (other wastes), and **Annex-VIII** lists wastes that are "hazardous" subject to transboundary movement controls.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Y-Code" Classification (Basel Convention)** — extremely high yield for KTU:
> - **Y1** – Clinical wastes from medical care
> - **Y2** – Wastes from pharmaceuticals manufacture
> - **Y3** – Waste oils/water-oil emulsions
> - **Y4** – Wastes of organic solvents
> - **Y5** – Wastes from heat-treatment of metals
> - **Y6** – Wastes from production and use of organic dyestuffs
> - **Y7** – Wastes from tanning/leather
> - **Y8** – Wastes from wood preservation
> - **Y9** – Waste oils from petroleum refining
> - **Y10–Y47** – Various organic/inorganic chemical wastes
> - **Y48** – Wastes with Annex-II constituents

### 2.2 Health and Environmental Effects (Detailed Mechanism)

#### 2.2.1 Health Effects on Humans

| Route of Exposure | Mechanism | Common Health Outcomes |
|---|---|---|
| **Inhalation** | Vapour/dust uptake via alveoli | Lung cancer (asbestos, benzene), asbestosis, silicosis, bronchitis |
| **Ingestion** | Contaminated food/water, hand-to-mouth | Lead encephalopathy, mercury Minamata disease, arsenic keratosis |
| **Dermal (skin)** | Absorption through intact/cut skin | Chemical burns, dermatitis, organophosphate poisoning |
| **Injection / Sharps** | Hospital staff, waste-pickers | Hepatitis B, HIV, septicaemia |

**Acute vs Chronic Toxicity:**
- **Acute toxicity:** Manifests within hours/days (e.g., cyanide poisoning, H₂S inhalation, strong acid burns).
- **Chronic toxicity:** Latency of months/years/decades (e.g., lead-induced cognitive decline in children, mesothelioma from asbestos after 20–40 years, endocrine disruption from PCBs/DDT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classic Case Studies (KTU 14-Mark favourites):**
> 1. **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984, India)** – MIC (methyl isocyanate) leak from Union Carbide plant; >3,800 immediate deaths; 500,000+ exposed.
> 2. **Minamata Disease (Japan, 1956)** – Mercury (Hg) bioaccumulation in fish; neurological disorders.
> 3. **Itai-Itai Disease (Japan)** – Cadmium poisoning; bone deformities, kidney failure.
> 4. **Love Canal (USA, 1978)** – Buried chemical waste contaminated residential groundwater; birth defects.
> 5. **Seveso Disaster (Italy, 1976)** – TCDD dioxin release; chloracne, cancer clusters.

#### 2.2.2 Environmental Effects

- **Soil Contamination:** Heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Cr, Hg) are **persistent** (do not degrade); they alter soil pH, kill soil microbiota, and enter food-chains. 
- **Groundwater Pollution:** Landfill leachate percolates to aquifers. **Chelation** of metals by fulvic acids makes them mobile.
- **Surface Water:** Oil films block oxygen transfer; acid mine drainage (AMD) lowers pH of rivers; bioaccumulation in aquatic life.
- **Air Pollution:** Volatilisation of VOCs (benzene, TCE, PCE), particulate matter, dioxin emission from improper incineration.
- **Bioaccumulation & Biomagnification:** Lipophilic pollutants (DDT, PCBs, dioxins) increase in concentration up the food chain. **BCF = C_organism / C_water**.
- **Ecological Disruption:** Loss of biodiversity, eutrophication, bio-magnification in top predators (e.g., raptors, polar bears).

### 2.3 Treatment, Disposal and Management of Hazardous Waste

#### 2.3.1 The **Hierarchical Management Pyramid** (KTU 2024 NEP-Aligned)

> **Most Preferred → Least Preferred:**
> 1. **Prevention / Source Reduction** – minimise waste generation at design stage.
> 2. **Minimisation** – process modification, material substitution.
> 3. **Reuse** – return to original use.
> 4. **Recycle / Recovery** – material recovery (solvent recovery, metal recovery).
> 5. **Treatment** – reduce hazard (volume, toxicity, mobility).
> 6. **Disposal** – secure landfill, deep-well injection, incineration without energy recovery.

#### 2.3.2 Treatment Methods — The **"PCBT"** Mnemonic

**(a) Physical Treatment:**
- Sedimentation, centrifugation, filtration, evaporation, distillation, soil washing, air-stripping (for VOCs), carbon adsorption.

**(b) Chemical Treatment:**
- **Neutralisation** (acid + base → salt + water)
- **Precipitation** (heavy metals as hydroxides/sulfides)
- **Oxidation-Reduction** (e.g., Cr⁶⁺ → Cr³⁺ using FeSO₄, Na₂S₂O₅)
- **Ion Exchange** (resin captures metal ions)
- **Cement-based Solidification / Stabilisation (S/S)** – encapsulate waste in cement matrix.

**(c) Biological Treatment (Bioremediation):**
- **Aerobic / Anaerobic biodegradation** for organic solvents, PAHs, phenols.
- **Phytoremediation** – plants like *Brassica juncea* (Indian mustard) hyperaccumulate As, Cd, Pb.
- **Mycoremediation** – fungi (white-rot) degrade lignins, PCBs, dioxins.
- **Bioaugmentation / Biostimulation** – adding microbes or nutrients.

**(d) Thermal Treatment (highest KTU weightage for treatment section):**

| Method | Operating Temp | Best For | Residue |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Incineration (with energy recovery)** | 850 – 1,200 °C | Organic solvents, PCBs, pathological waste | Ash (10–20% vol), flue gas |
| **Pyrolysis** | 400 – 800 °C (no O₂) | Tyres, plastics | Pyrolysis oil, char, gas |
| **Plasma Arc** | 1,500 – 5,000 °C | Highly toxic (chemical weapons, hospital waste) | Vitrified slag, syngas |
| **Autoclaving (wet heat)** | 121 °C, 15 psi, 30 min | Infectious biomedical waste | Sterilised solid |
| **Thermal Desorption** | 200 – 500 °C | Soil contaminated with petroleum hydrocarbons | Treated soil, off-gas |

**Key Incineration Performance Indicators:**
- **Destruction and Removal Efficiency (DRE):** *DRE = (W_in − W_out) / W_in × 100 %*
- **Combustion Efficiency (CE):** *CE = [CO₂] / ([CO₂] + [CO]) × 100 %* — should be > **99.99 %**.
- **Residence Time:** minimum **2 seconds** in combustion zone at > 1,000 °C for PCBs (USEPA).

#### 2.3.3 Final Disposal Methods

1. **Secure Hazardous Waste Landfill (SHWL):** Multi-liner, leachate collection, groundwater monitoring, run-on/run-off control, capping. India mandates **double-liner** (geomembrane + HDPE) as per CPCB guidelines.
2. **Deep Well Injection** (not legal in India) – inject liquid waste into deep porous strata.
3. **Ocean Dumping** (Banned by London Convention 1972 and the 1996 Protocol) – not legal.
4. **Surface Impoundments** – engineered ponds (e.g., red-mud ponds of alumina refineries).
5. **Underground / Mine Backfilling** – cement-stabilised waste in abandoned mines.
6. **Co-processing in Cement Kilns** – waste used as alternative fuel/raw material (AFR/ARM). High-temperature co-incineration at 1,400 °C, long residence time, alkaline environment destroys organics completely. **Permitted in India since 2003.**

### 2.4 Legal Framework for Hazardous Waste Management in India

#### 2.4.1 The Evolution of Indian Hazardous Waste Law

| Year | Rule / Act | Salient Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **1974** | Water (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act | First umbrella water-pollution control law |
| **1977** | Amended Water Act – included hazardous substances | |
| **1981** | Air (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act | |
| **1986** | Environment (Protection) Act (EPA) — **Umbrella Act** | Section 6 gave birth to hazardous-waste rules |
| **1989** | Hazardous Wastes (Management & Handling) Rules | First exclusive rules |
| **1989** | Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical (MSIHC) Rules | Process safety, MAH units |
| **2008** | Hazardous Waste (Management, Handling and Transboundary Movement) Rules | New thresholds, ESM policies |
| **2016** | **Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules — HOWM Rules** | **Currently in force** |
| **2022** | Amendment to HOWM Rules | Battery-waste extended producer responsibility (EPR) |

#### 2.4.2 Salient Features of **HOWM Rules, 2016** (Mandatory KTU Knowledge)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **16 Key Provisions of HOWM 2016 — must know for 14-mark questions:**

1. **Applicability:** All hazardous and other wastes listed in Schedule-I, II, III.
2. **Authorisation:** Occupier must obtain **authorisation from State Pollution Control Board (SPCB)** under Section 25 of Water Act 1974 and Section 21 of Air Act 1981 — *this is the twin authorisation system*.
3. **Treatment, Storage and Disposal Facility (TSDF):** Every state must have at least one integrated TSDF. **Common TSDF concept** is encouraged.
4. **Packaging, Labelling and Transport (PLT):** Containers must display Class label, UN code, hazard pictogram, sender/receiver details, emergency contact.
5. **Manifest System (Form 10 / 11 / 12):** Six-copy waste tracking from generator → transporter → operator → recycler → back to generator. *Without manifest, waste is illegal.*
6. **Record-keeping:** Generator, transporter, and operator must keep records for **5 years**.
7. **Annual Returns:** To be filed in **Form-4** by 30th June of every year.
8. **Liability of Generator, Transporter, Operator, and Importer (the "Liable Four").**
9. **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR):** Producer is responsible for end-of-life management of products (battery, tyre, oil, e-waste) — now enshrined in 2022 amendments.
10. **Transboundary Movement:** **Basel Convention**-compliant; prior informed consent (PIC) from destination country; **no import of hazardous waste from any country for final disposal**.
11. **Import Permissible for:** re-processing, recycling, recovery, co-processing, or as raw material.
12. **Export Permissible Only To:** countries where environmentally sound management (ESM) is possible and consent obtained.
13. **Used Oil Management (Schedule-V):** Specific thresholds for re-refining.
14. **Lead-Acid Battery Recycling:** Brand-owner must collect and channelise used batteries to registered recyclers.
15. **Accident Reporting:** Any accident involving hazardous waste must be reported to SPCB within **48 hours**.
16. **Penalty:** Imprisonment up to **5 years** and/or fine up to **₹1 lakh/day** (and continuing) under EPA, 1986.

#### 2.4.3 Institutional Framework

> **Hierarchy of Hazardous-Waste Governance in India:**
> - **MoEF&CC (Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change)** – apex policy body
> - **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board)** – technical standards, guidelines, national inventory
> - **SPCBs / PCCs (State Pollution Control Boards / Pollution Control Committees)** – authorisation, monitoring, enforcement
> - **District Level Authorities** – inspection
> - **TSDF Operators** – operational responsibility

#### 2.4.4 International Conventions India has ratified

- **Basel Convention (1989, ratified 1992)** – controls transboundary movement.
- **Stockholm Convention (2001, ratified 2006)** – 12 initial POPs (Persistent Organic Pollutants).
- **Rotterdam Convention (1998, ratified 2006)** – PIC for hazardous chemicals/pesticides.
- **Minamata Convention on Mercury (2013, ratified 2018)** – bans new mercury mines, controls emissions.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Application / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Destruction & Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \dfrac{W_{in} - W_{out}}{W_{in}} \times 100\,\%$ | Incinerator performance; > 99.99 % for PCBs |
| 2 | **Combustion Efficiency (CE)** | $CE = \dfrac{[CO_2]}{[CO_2]+[CO]} \times 100\,\%$ | > 99.99 % mandated |
| 3 | **Bioconcentration Factor (BCF)** | $BCF = \dfrac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}$ | Lipophilic pollutants; dimensionless |
| 4 | **Biomagnification Factor (BMF)** | $BMF = \dfrac{C_{predator}}{C_{prey}}$ | DDT, PCBs in food chain |
| 5 | **Leachate Generation Rate** | $L = P \times A \times (1 - E)$ | $P$=precipitation, $A$=area, $E$=evaporation |
| 6 | **Landfill Capacity** | $C = V_{daily} \times L_f \times t$ | $L_f$=compaction factor, $t$=life |
| 7 | **Neutralisation Stoichiometry** | $m_{base} = m_{acid} \times \dfrac{MW_{base}}{MW_{acid}} \times \dfrac{n_{base}}{n_{acid}}$ | Acid–base neutralisation |
| 8 | **Air Stripping Henry's Law** | $C_g = H \cdot C_l$ | VOC removal; $H$ = Henry's constant |
| 9 | **Vapor Pressure (Antoine)** | $\log P = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | Volatility assessment |
| 10 | **TCLP Threshold (selected)** | Pb = 5 mg/L; Cd = 1 mg/L; Hg = 0.2 mg/L; As = 5 mg/L | Toxicity classification |

> [!NOTE]
> **No pipes (`|`) in tables** — the absolute-value / concentration symbol has been replaced with $\vert \cdot \vert$ and $\cdot$ in the rows to preserve the markdown table structure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive analytical derivations** a KTU 14-mark question will demand — typically a numerical on either (a) incinerator DRE/quantity calculation, (b) leachate volume, (c) TCLP classification threshold, or (d) neutralisation stoichiometry.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Incinerator Destruction & Removal Efficiency with Feed-Composition Mass Balance

**Problem Setup:** A rotary kiln incinerator receives a feed of **2,500 kg/h** of a spent-solvent mixture containing **35 % w/w** toluene (C₇H₈), 25 % xylene, 20 % methylene chloride, and 20 % non-hazardous diluents. Stack-gas analysis reports a **Total Organic Carbon (TOC) of 14 mg/Nm³** in a flue-gas flow of **18,000 Nm³/h** at 1 atm and 25 °C. Determine (a) the mass flow of unburned TOC out of the stack, (b) the Destruction & Removal Efficiency (DRE), and (c) state whether the incinerator meets the USEPA **99.99 % DRE** criterion for principal organic hazardous constituents (POHCs).

---

#### Step 1 — Compute the mass flow of organic feed (W_in)

The feed contains toluene + xylene + methylene chloride = 35 % + 25 % + 20 % = **80 %** hazardous organics.

$$
W_{in, organic} = 2{,}500 \ \text{kg/h} \times 0.80 = 2{,}000 \ \text{kg/h}
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the volumetric concentration of TOC in the flue gas (already given)

$$
C_{TOC} = 14 \ \text{mg/Nm}^3
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the mass flow of unburned organic (TOC) leaving the stack

$$
W_{out} = C_{TOC} \times Q_{gas} = 14 \ \dfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{Nm}^3} \times 18{,}000 \ \dfrac{\text{Nm}^3}{\text{h}}
$$

$$
W_{out} = 14 \times 18{,}000 \ \dfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{h}} = 252{,}000 \ \dfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{h}}
$$

#### Step 4 — Convert mg/h to kg/h

$$
W_{out} = 252{,}000 \ \dfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{h}} \times \dfrac{1 \ \text{kg}}{10^6 \ \text{mg}} = 0.252 \ \text{kg/h}
$$

#### Step 5 — Apply the DRE formula

$$
DRE = \dfrac{W_{in} - W_{out}}{W_{in}} \times 100\,\%
$$

$$
DRE = \dfrac{2{,}000 - 0.252}{2{,}000} \times 100\,\%
$$

$$
DRE = \dfrac{1{,}999.748}{2{,}000} \times 100\,\% = 99.9874\,\%
$$

#### Step 6 — Compliance check

USEPA RCRA mandate for POHCs in hazardous-waste incinerators: **DRE ≥ 99.99 %**.

$$
99.9874\,\% < 99.99\,\%
$$

> **Result:** The incinerator **fails the 99.99 % DRE benchmark by 0.0026 %** — marginally non-compliant. The operator must either increase combustion temperature (currently ~850 °C; should be > 1,000 °C), increase residence time (need ≥ 2 s in secondary chamber), or improve turbulence.

> [!NOTE]
> **In a 14-mark solution, present Step 6 as a clear "Conclusion" callout box.** Examiners award at least 2 marks for "inference / inference quality".

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Acid-Leachate Neutralisation Stoichiometry (Chemical Treatment)

**Problem Setup:** A dye-industry wastewater stream generates **5,000 L/day** of acidic leachate with **concentration of H₂SO₄ = 1.5 M**. The plant wants to neutralise with **solid Ca(OH)₂ (lime, MW = 74 g/mol, purity 90 %)** to a target pH of 7. Calculate (a) theoretical lime requirement, (b) actual lime required accounting for purity, (c) the mass of CaSO₄·2H₂O (gypsum, MW = 172 g/mol) precipitate generated per day.

---

#### Step 1 — Write the balanced chemical equation

$$
\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 \downarrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

Stoichiometry: **1 mole of H₂SO₄ reacts with 1 mole of Ca(OH)₂** to give 1 mole of CaSO₄.

#### Step 2 — Moles of H₂SO₄ in the daily feed

$$
n_{H_2SO_4} = C \times V = 1.5 \ \dfrac{\text{mol}}{\text{L}} \times 5{,}000 \ \text{L} = 7{,}500 \ \text{mol/day}
$$

#### Step 3 — Theoretical mass of pure Ca(OH)₂ required

$$
m_{\text{theoretical}} = n \times MW = 7{,}500 \ \text{mol} \times 74 \ \dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{mol}} = 555{,}000 \ \text{g/day} = 555 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

#### Step 4 — Adjust for 90 % purity of commercial lime

$$
m_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{theoretical}}}{0.90} = \dfrac{555}{0.90} = 616.67 \ \text{kg/day of commercial lime}
$$

#### Step 5 — Moles of CaSO₄·2H₂O (gypsum) generated

From the stoichiometry, 1 mol H₂SO₄ → 1 mol CaSO₄ → 1 mol CaSO₄·2H₂O.

$$
n_{\text{gypsum}} = 7{,}500 \ \text{mol/day}
$$

$$
m_{\text{gypsum}} = 7{,}500 \ \text{mol} \times 172 \ \dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{mol}} = 1{,}290{,}000 \ \text{g/day} = 1{,}290 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

#### Step 6 — Mass-balance check (optional, for full marks)

Total mass in = mass of H₂SO₄ + mass of lime  
Mass of H₂SO₄ = 7,500 mol × 98 g/mol = 735,000 g = 735 kg/day  
Total in = 735 + 616.67 = 1,351.67 kg/day  
Total out (gypsum + water) = 1,290 kg gypsum + (7,500 × 2 × 18) g = 270 kg water + (residual moisture) ≈ 1,351 kg/day ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: TCLP-Based Toxicity Classification (Decision Matrix)

The **Toxicity Characteristic Leaching Procedure (TCLP)** is a USEPA Method 1311 that simulates a landfill-leachate attack on a solid waste. The steps are:

1. **Particle size reduction:** Waste is crushed to < 9.5 mm.
2. **Extraction fluid selection:** 
   - If waste is alkaline (pH > 5), use **Extraction Fluid #1** (acetic acid + NaOH, pH 4.93 ± 0.05).
   - Otherwise, use **Extraction Fluid #2** (acetic acid, pH 2.88 ± 0.05).
3. **Extraction:** Mix waste with fluid at **L/S = 20 (1:20 w/w)**. Rotate in extractor for **18 ± 2 hours** at **30 ± 2 rpm**.
4. **Filtration & analysis:** Filter through 0.6 – 0.8 µm glass-fibre filter. Analyse extract for **40 regulated contaminants** by AAS/ICP or GC-MS.
5. **Decision rule:** If the concentration of *any* of the 40 contaminants **exceeds the regulatory threshold (D004–D043 codes)**, the waste is **TOXIC (Hazardous)**.

**Worked Numerical:** A solid waste sample weighing **100 g** is extracted with **2,000 mL** of Extraction Fluid #2 (L/S = 20). The extract is analysed and yields:
- Lead (Pb) = **6 mg/L** (regulatory limit = 5 mg/L)
- Cadmium (Cd) = **0.6 mg/L** (limit = 1 mg/L)
- Mercury (Hg) = **0.05 mg/L** (limit = 0.2 mg/L)

**Conclusion:** Lead is **above** the threshold, so the waste **fails the TCLP** and is classified as **D008 (Toxic) hazardous waste** under USEPA RCRA.

> **Mass of leached Pb:** 6 mg/L × 2 L = **12 mg Pb** leached from 100 g waste ⇒ leaching concentration = 12 mg/100 g = **120 mg/kg waste**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Bioconcentration & Biomagnification Calculations

**Problem Setup:** A lake has DDT concentration of **0.003 µg/L** in water. Algae contain **0.04 µg/g**, zooplankton contain **0.83 µg/g**, small fish contain **2.07 µg/g**, and predatory birds contain **22.8 µg/g**. Calculate (a) BCF at each trophic level relative to water, (b) BMF between successive trophic levels.

**Step (a) — BCF at each level (using density of water = 1 g/mL):**

For algae: 0.04 µg/g ≈ 0.04 µg/mL = 40 µg/L
$$
BCF_{algae} = \dfrac{40 \ \mu g/L}{0.003 \ \mu g/L} = 13{,}333
$$

For zooplankton: 0.83 µg/g = 830 µg/L
$$
BCF_{zooplankton} = \dfrac{830}{0.003} = 2.77 \times 10^5
$$

For small fish: 2.07 µg/g = 2,070 µg/L
$$
BCF_{small\ fish} = \dfrac{2{,}070}{0.003} = 6.90 \times 10^5
$$

For birds: 22.8 µg/g = 22,800 µg/L
$$
BCF_{birds} = \dfrac{22{,}800}{0.003} = 7.60 \times 10^6
$$

**Step (b) — BMFs:**

$$
BMF_{algae/water} = \dfrac{0.04}{0.003} \approx 13
$$
$$
BMF_{zoo/algae} = \dfrac{0.83}{0.04} \approx 21
$$
$$
BMF_{fish/zoo} = \dfrac{2.07}{0.83} \approx 2.5
$$
$$
BMF_{bird/fish} = \dfrac{22.8}{2.07} \approx 11
$$

> **Inferences:** Biomagnification is steepest in lipid-rich organisms. Lipophilic pollutants with $\log K_{ow} > 5$ are of greatest concern. The bird tissue concentration is **> 7-million-fold** that of the water.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Treatment Selection Decision Tree (KTU 14-Mark)

| Waste Property | Recommended Treatment(s) | Why? | Residue Disposal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ignitable (flash < 60 °C)** | Incineration / Co-processing in cement kiln | Combustion is the only way to destroy the matrix | Ash to SHWL |
| **Corrosive (pH ≤ 2 or ≥ 12.5)** | Neutralisation + chemical precipitation | Reaches safe pH, then precipitates heavy metals | Sludge to SHWL |
| **Reactive (unstable)** | Stabilisation/detoxification (e.g., reduction of Cr⁶⁺ → Cr³⁺) | Makes it inert | SHWL |
| **Toxic (TCLP-failing)** | S/S (cement-based) or thermal | Encapsulates pollutants | SHWL or as fuel in cement kiln |
| **Biomedical / Infectious** | Autoclave + shredding, or incineration | Kills pathogens | Disinfected to MSW stream or ash to SHWL |
| **E-Waste (Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr-VI)** | Pyrometallurgy / hydrometallurgy / mech. separation | Recovers valuable metals | Residue to SHWL |
| **Used oil** | Re-refining (distillation, hydro-treatment) | Recovers base oil | Re-use, sludge to TSDF |
| **Spent solvents (organic)** | Solvent recovery (distillation) / incineration | Recycles or destroys | None / flue-gas treatment |
| **Lead-acid batteries** | Smelting (pyrometallurgy) | Recovers Pb, plastic, acid | Plastic to recycler, acid neutralised |
| **Pesticide waste** | High-temperature incineration (≥ 1,100 °C) | Dioxin destruction | Ash to SHWL |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
