**Core Definitions (must memorize):**
- ☐ Industrial Ecology (IE) — Frosch & Gallopoulos, 1989
- ☐ Industrial Metabolism — analogy with biological metabolism
- ☐ Industrial Symbiosis — inter-firm by-product exchange
- ☐ Cradle-to-Cradle — McDonough & Braungart
- ☐ Dematerialization — less material per unit service
- ☐ Eco-Industrial Park — clustered firms sharing resources
- ☐ Biomimicry — design inspired by nature

**Key Tools & Frameworks:**
- ☐ Material Flow Analysis (MFA) — based on mass balance
- ☐ Substance Flow Analysis (SFA) — element-level tracking
- ☐ Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) — ISO 14040/14044
- ☐ Input-Output Analysis (IOA) — Leontief-based
- ☐ IPAT Equation — $I = P \times A \times T$

**Critical Equations (numerical competency required):**
- ☐ Mass balance: $\sum I_i = \sum O_j$ (steady state)
- ☐ Material efficiency: $\eta_M = M_{\text{product}} / M_{\text{input}}$
- ☐ Recycling rate: $R = M_{\text{recycled}} / M_{\text{consumed}} \times 100\%$
- ☐ Dematerialization index formula

**Case Study to Remember:**
- ☐ **Kalundborg, Denmark** — the original and most cited EIP

**Conceptual Distinctions (frequently tested):**
- ☐ IE vs Pollution Control (preventive vs reactive)
- ☐ IE vs Industrial Symbiosis (broad framework vs specific strategy)
- ☐ Cradle-to-Grave vs Cradle-to-Cradle (linear vs circular)
- ☐ LCA vs MFA (impact assessment vs flow tracking)

**Hierarchy of IE Intervention (in decreasing preference):**
1. Natural capital restoration
2. Cradle-to-Cradle design
3. Industrial symbiosis
4. Material/energy efficiency
5. End-of-pipe treatment

**Real-World Toxic Risk Linkage (Course-specific to PCSFT602):**
- ☐ Industrial Ecology reduces toxic substance generation at source — directly relevant to **toxic risk minimization**.
- ☐ By-product synergy must consider **toxicity profiles** to avoid "toxic waste swapping."
- ☐ LCA's **Human Toxicity Potential (HTP)** and **Ecotoxicity Potential (ETP)** indicators are critical assessment metrics.

**High-Frequency KTU Question Triggers:**
- ☐ "Define Industrial Ecology" (2 marks — definition + 1 example)
- ☐ "State the principles of IE" (5 marks — list 5+ principles with one-line explanations)
- ☐ "Differentiate between..." (always pairs: IE/IS, LCA/MFA, Cradle-to-Cradle/Cradle-to-Grave)
- ☐ "Explain with example" (Kalundborg is the safe default example)
- ☐ "Calculate mass balance" (numerical problem — see Q.A.(b) above)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Environmental Toxicology and the Dose-Response Relationship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT602 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND TOXIC RISKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles of Environmental Toxicology and the Dose-Response Relationship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 4 — PRINCIPLES OF ENVIRONMENTAL TOXICOLOGY & DOSE-RESPONSE RELATIONSHIP

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental Toxicology** is the multidisciplinary scientific study of the adverse effects of chemical, biological, and physical agents on living organisms, ecosystems, and human health, encompassing the pathways by which such agents reach targets, the mechanisms of injury they produce, and the quantitative relationships between the magnitude of exposure and the resulting biological response.

The discipline is built upon three intersecting pillars:

| Pillar | Focus Area | KTU 2024 Emphasis |
|---|---|---|
| **Environmental Chemistry** | Sources, transport, and fate of toxicants | Bioaccumulation, persistence |
| **Environmental Biology** | Routes of exposure, target tissues | ADME processes |
| **Toxicology (Core)** | Dose–Response analysis | LD50, NOAEL, risk thresholds |

A **toxicant** is any man-made or naturally occurring substance that produces a harmful biological effect at a certain dose, whereas a **toxin** is specifically a toxicant produced by a living organism (e.g., botulinum from *Clostridium botulinum*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU ESE):**
> - **Toxicity** = intrinsic property of a substance to cause harm.
> - **Hazard** = probability that harm will occur under specific exposure conditions.
> - **Risk** = a function of both toxicity *and* exposure: **Risk = f(Toxicity × Exposure)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Cup" Intuition

Imagine a **single cup of coffee**. For most adults, the caffeine it contains provides a pleasant alertness boost — no harm at all. But if a small child were to drink the *same cup*, the dose per kg of body weight would be enormous, and the child could suffer convulsions or cardiac arrhythmia.

- The **coffee** itself has *fixed toxicity*.
- The **dose** depends on the drinker's body weight and the volume consumed.
- The **response** (alertness vs. convulsion) changes dramatically with dose.

This single analogy captures the entire heart of environmental toxicology — *the dose makes the poison* (Paracelsus, c. 1538). Even water and oxygen, in excess, can be lethal; conversely, highly toxic substances like botulinum toxin can be harmless in vanishingly small doses. Toxicology is therefore not simply the study of "poisons," but the **quantitative science of dose**.

## 1.3 Classification of Toxicants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly requires students to differentiate the **origin**, **persistence**, and **bioaccumulative nature** of toxicants.

1. **By Origin**
   - *Natural toxins*: Aflatoxins (Aspergillus), ricin (castor bean), cyanogenic glycosides.
   - *Synthetic toxicants*: DDT, PCBs, dioxins, PAHs, microplastics.

2. **By Persistence in Environment**
   - *Non-persistent*: Rapidly degraded (e.g., malathion, half-life ≈ days).
   - *Persistent*: Half-life > 1 month (e.g., DDT, dioxins).

3. **By Bioaccumulation Potential**
   - **Bioconcentration Factor (BCF)** = $\frac{C_{organism}}{C_{environment}}$
   - **Bioaccumulative** when $\text{BCF} > 1000$ for aquatic organisms.

4. **By Mode of Action**
   - *Carcinogens*, *mutagens*, *teratogens*, *neurotoxins*, *hepatotoxins*, *endocrine disruptors*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — TOXICOKINETICS vs. TOXICODYNAMICS

Environmental toxicology bifurcates into two mechanistic domains:

| Domain | Question It Answers | Core Processes |
|---|---|---|
| **Toxicokinetics** | *What does the body do to the chemical?* | Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion (ADME) |
| **Toxicodynamics** | *What does the chemical do to the body?* | Receptor binding, enzyme inhibition, DNA damage, oxidative stress |

## 2.1 The ADME Framework

### (A) Absorption (Routes of Exposure)

| Route | Notation | Environmental Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Oral (ingestion) | GI | Food, water, soil ingestion (children) |
| Inhalation | RP | Particulate matter, VOCs, asbestos |
| Dermal | DP | Pesticides, solvents, water-borne chemicals |
| Injection / Parental | IP | Medical, occupational sharps |

### (B) Distribution
After absorption, a chemical is carried in the bloodstream and may partition into lipid-rich tissues. The **Volume of Distribution** ($V_d$) is defined as:

$$V_d = \frac{\text{Amount of chemical in body}}{C_{plasma}}$$

- A small $V_d$ ⇒ chemical remains in plasma (e.g., warfarin).
- A large $V_d$ ⇒ extensive tissue storage (e.g., DDT in adipose tissue, $V_d \approx 10$ L/kg body weight in humans).

### (C) Metabolism (Biotransformation)
Primarily occurs in the **liver**, catalyzed by cytochrome P450 (CYP450) enzymes. Two-phase process:

- **Phase I (Functionalization):** Oxidation, reduction, hydrolysis → introduces/exposes a functional group (often mediated by CYP450).
- **Phase II (Conjugation):** Glucuronidation, sulfation, glutathione conjugation → increases water solubility for excretion.

> [!NOTE]
> *Bioactivation*: Some Phase I products are *more* toxic than the parent compound (e.g., benzo[a]pyrene → BPDE epoxide, aflatoxin B1 → AFB1-8,9-epoxide).

### (D) Excretion
Primary routes: renal (urine), biliary (feces), pulmonary (exhaled air), and minor routes (sweat, lactation). The **first-order elimination rate constant** $k$ (units: hr⁻¹) describes the fraction eliminated per unit time:

$$C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$$

where $C_t$ is the plasma concentration at time $t$, and $C_0$ is the initial concentration. The corresponding **half-life** is:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} \approx \frac{0.693}{k}$$

## 2.2 The Dose–Response Relationship — Conceptual Framework

The **dose–response relationship** is the cornerstone of toxicology. It establishes that *the magnitude of a biological effect is a function of the magnitude of the administered dose*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Fundamental Endpoints in Dose–Response:**
> 1. **Graded Response** — continuous measurement in a single individual (e.g., enzyme inhibition, blood pressure drop).
> 2. **Quantal (All-or-None) Response** — proportion of a population that exhibits a defined effect (e.g., death, tumor).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> Memorize the units and the precise wording of these definitions — examiners allocate easy marks when students write the *complete* textbook phrasing.

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Standard Metric / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lethal Dose 50 | $\text{LD}_{50}$ | Dose causing death in 50 % of test population | mg/kg body weight |
| Lethal Concentration 50 | $\text{LC}_{50}$ | Airborne concentration lethal to 50 % | mg/L or ppm |
| Effective Dose 50 | $\text{ED}_{50}$ | Dose producing 50 % of maximal therapeutic effect | mg/kg |
| Toxic Dose 50 | $\text{TD}_{50}$ | Dose producing a specific toxic effect in 50 % | mg/kg |
| No Observed Adverse Effect Level | NOAEL | Highest dose with no statistically significant adverse effect | mg/kg/day |
| Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level | LOAEL | Lowest dose at which an adverse effect is observed | mg/kg/day |
| Reference Dose | RfD | Estimate of daily exposure unlikely to cause harm | mg/kg/day |
| Therapeutic Index | TI | $\text{TI} = \dfrac{\text{TD}_{50}}{\text{ED}_{50}}$ | dimensionless |
| Margin of Exposure | MOE | $\text{MOE} = \dfrac{\text{NOAEL (animal)}}{\text{Human Exposure}}$ | dimensionless |
| Acceptable Daily Intake | ADI | Daily intake considered safe over a lifetime | mg/kg/day |
| Bioconcentration Factor | BCF | $C_{org} / C_{env}$ | L/kg |
| Bioaccumulation Factor | BAF | $C_{org} / C_{env}$ accounting for dietary + water | L/kg |

### 2.4 Toxicity Rating Scale (KTU Favourite — 3-Mark Question)

| Toxicity Class | Approximate Oral $\text{LD}_{50}$ (rat) | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Super-toxic | $\le 5$ mg/kg | Botulinum toxin, dioxin |
| Extremely toxic | 5 – 50 mg/kg | Nicotine, VX nerve agent |
| Highly toxic | 50 – 500 mg/kg | Sodium cyanide |
| Moderately toxic | 0.5 – 5 g/kg | Caffeine, paracetamol |
| Slightly toxic | 5 – 15 g/kg | Kerosene |
| Practically non-toxic | $> 15$ g/kg | Glycerol |

## 2.5 Why Dose–Response Matters in Environmental Engineering

- **Effluent Discharge Standards:** Regulatory limits (BIS/USEPA/EU) are anchored on dose–response data from bioassays (e.g., $\text{LC}_{50}$ for *Daphnia magna*).
- **Drinking Water Standards:** MCL (Maximum Contaminant Level) values are typically set at 1/100 to 1/1000 of the NOAEL, with safety/uncertainty factors.
- **Ecological Risk Assessment:** Population-level impacts (e.g., endocrine disruption in fish at sub-ppb levels) necessitate whole-ecosystem dose–response modelling.
- **Occupational Hygiene:** Threshold Limit Values (TLVs) are derived from inhalation dose–response studies on humans or animals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sigmoidal Dose–Response (Probit/Logit) Equation

A graded dose–response curve typically takes a **sigmoidal shape** when dose is plotted on a logarithmic axis. The mathematical model most commonly used is the **four-parameter logistic (Hill) equation**:

$$E = E_{min} + \frac{(E_{max} - E_{min})}{1 + \left( \dfrac{D}{ED_{50}} \right)^n}$$

where:
- $E$ = observed biological response (e.g., % of maximal effect)
- $E_{min}$ = baseline response (typically 0 %)
- $E_{max}$ = maximal achievable response (typically 100 %)
- $D$ = administered dose
- $ED_{50}$ = dose producing 50 % of maximal response
- $n$ = **Hill coefficient** (steepness of the curve; $n > 1$ ⇒ cooperative binding; $n = 1$ ⇒ classical Michaelis–Menten)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1.** Assume the underlying chemical interaction obeys the **law of mass action** between a toxicant $T$ and a biological receptor $R$:

$$T + R \;\rightleftharpoons\; TR \;\rightarrow\; \text{Response}$$

**Step 2.** The equilibrium constant (binding affinity) is:

$$K_a = \frac{[TR]}{[T][R]}$$

**Step 3.** Total receptor concentration is conserved: $[R]_{tot} = [R] + [TR]$. Solving for fractional occupancy:

$$\theta = \frac{[TR]}{[R]_{tot}} = \frac{[T]}{K_d + [T]}$$

where $K_d = 1/K_a$ is the dissociation constant.

**Step 4.** The biological response is proportional to receptor occupancy: $E = E_{max} \cdot \theta$. Substituting:

$$E = \frac{E_{max} \cdot [T]}{K_d + [T]}$$

This is the classical **Michaelis–Menten / Langmuir equation**. It is hyperbolic, not sigmoidal.

**Step 5.** To convert hyperbolic behaviour into the sigmoidal form observed experimentally, assume **n independent identical binding sites** act **cooperatively** (Hill assumption). The fractional occupancy then becomes:

$$\theta = \frac{[T]^n}{K_d^n + [T]^n}$$

**Step 6.** Substituting into $E = E_{min} + (E_{max} - E_{min}) \cdot \theta$ yields:

$$E = E_{min} + \frac{(E_{max} - E_{min})}{1 + \left( \dfrac{K_d}{D} \right)^n} = E_{min} + \frac{(E_{max} - E_{min})}{1 + \left( \dfrac{D}{ED_{50}} \right)^{-n}}$$

Recognizing that when $D = K_d$ we have $E = (E_{min} + E_{max})/2$, the denominator can be re-expressed with $ED_{50} = K_d$ to obtain the **canonical Hill equation** stated at the beginning.

> [!NOTE]
> The **sigmoidicity** of the curve in $\log_{10}(D)$ axes arises because $\log_{10}(K_d^n + D^n) \to \log_{10}(D^n) = n \log_{10}(D)$ for $D \gg K_d$, giving an asymptotic rise. At the inflection point, slope $\propto n / (2.303 \cdot ED_{50})$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — LD₅₀ Estimation by Probit Analysis

A toxicologist administers four doses of a candidate pesticide to groups of 10 rats and records 24-hour mortalities:

| Dose (mg/kg) | Dose ($x = \log_{10}$) | Deaths | % Mortality ($p$) | Probit ($Y$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 1.000 | 1/10 | 10 | 3.72 |
| 30 | 1.477 | 3/10 | 30 | 4.48 |
| 100 | 2.000 | 6/10 | 60 | 5.25 |
| 300 | 2.477 | 9/10 | 90 | 6.28 |

Using the standard **probit regression equation**:

$$Y = a + b \cdot \log_{10}(D)$$

Performing linear regression on the probit values:

**Step 1.** Compute means:
- $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1.000 + 1.477 + 2.000 + 2.477}{4} = 1.7385$
- $\bar{Y} = \dfrac{3.72 + 4.48 + 5.25 + 6.28}{4} = 4.9325$

**Step 2.** Compute slope $b$:

$$b = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

| $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $Y_i - \bar{Y}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})(Y_i - \bar{Y})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| −0.7385 | −1.2125 | 0.8954 | 0.5454 |
| −0.2615 | −0.4525 | 0.1183 | 0.0684 |
| +0.2615 | +0.3175 | 0.0830 | 0.0684 |
| +0.7385 | +1.3475 | 0.9951 | 0.5454 |
| **Σ** | | **2.0918** | **1.2276** |

$$b = \frac{2.0918}{1.2276} = 1.704$$

**Step 3.** Compute intercept $a$:

$$a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{x} = 4.9325 - (1.704)(1.7385) = 4.9325 - 2.9624 = 1.970$$

**Step 4.** Probit regression equation:

$$Y = 1.970 + 1.704 \log_{10}(D)$$

**Step 5.** For LD₅₀, the probit value of mortality = 50 % is $Y = 5.00$:

$$5.00 = 1.970 + 1.704 \log_{10}(LD_{50})$$

$$\log_{10}(LD_{50}) = \frac{5.00 - 1.970}{1.704} = \frac{3.030}{1.704} = 1.778$$

$$\boxed{LD_{50} = 10^{1.778} \approx 59.98 \approx 60 \; \text{mg/kg}}$$

**Step 6.** **95 % Confidence Interval** (KTU extension):

$$SE(\log LD_{50}) = \frac{1}{b}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\sum n_i} + \frac{(\bar{x} - \mu)^2}{b^2 \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}} \approx \frac{1}{1.704}\sqrt{\frac{1}{40} + 0} \approx 0.0926$$

$$\log LD_{50} = 1.778 \pm 1.96(0.0926) = 1.778 \pm 0.1815$$

Therefore the 95 % CI is $10^{1.597}$ to $10^{1.960}$, i.e., **39.5 to 91.2 mg/kg**.

## 3.3 Quantitative Comparison — Potency vs Toxicity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Potency and toxicity are *not* synonyms. **Potency** is a *positional* descriptor (left–right shift of curve); **toxicity** is an *inverse potency* measure.

For two chemicals A and B with identical mechanism:

$$\text{Relative Potency} = \frac{LD_{50}^{(B)}}{LD_{50}^{(A)}}$$

- If Chemical A has $\text{LD}_{50} = 50$ mg/kg and Chemical B has $\text{LD}_{50} = 500$ mg/kg, then Chemical A is **10× more toxic** and is the more **potent** toxicant.

## 3.4 Therapeutic Index Derivation (Used for Drug + Pesticide Safety)

$$\text{TI} = \frac{TD_{50}}{ED_{50}}$$

A **high TI** indicates a wide safety margin. For environmental toxicology parallels:

$$\text{MOS} = \frac{NOAEL}{\text{Estimated Human Exposure}}$$

$$\text{Hazard Quotient (HQ)} = \frac{\text{Exposure}}{\text{RfD}}$$

$$\text{Hazard Index (HI)} = \sum HQ_i \; \text{(cumulative across multiple chemicals)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> When HI > 1.0, the cumulative exposure is presumed to exceed safe thresholds. This is the operational definition used by US EPA's Integrated Risk Information System (IRIS).

## 3.5 Hormesis — The "Low-Dose Stimulation" Phenomenon

Below a certain threshold, several toxicants exhibit a **non-monotonic dose–response**: low doses *enhance* a biological endpoint (e.g., longevity, immune response), whereas higher doses suppress it. This **U- or J-shaped** curve is termed **hormesis**. Examples: alcohol consumption, radiation (linear-quadratic model at <100 mSv), heavy metals (e.g., selenium, chromium-III).

The mathematical representation:

$$E(D) = \alpha D + \beta D^2 \quad \text{(quadratic hormesis model)}$$

This complicates regulatory dose extrapolation and remains a topic of active debate in risk-assessment science.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Concept Map — Environmental Toxicology Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source of Toxicant] --> B[Environmental Transport]
    B --> C[Environmental Fate]
    C --> D[Route of Exposure]
    D --> E[Dose Received by Organism]
    E --> F[ADME in Body]
    F --> G[Target Organ Interaction]
    G --> H[Biological Response]
    H --> I[Adverse Effect Outcome]
    I --> J[Risk Assessment]
    J --> K[Regulatory Standard]
    K --> L[Public Health Policy]

    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706
    style I fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626
    style L fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a
```

**Reading guide:** A pollutant emitted from an industrial source is transported through air, water, or soil, transformed (abiotic and biotic), and absorbed by an organism through a defined route. Within the body, ADME governs the *internal* dose delivered to the target organ. The resulting biological response — from molecular alteration to population-level mortality — feeds into a structured risk assessment that ultimately informs policy.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Dose–Response Curve Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Curve_Anatomy
        axisX[Log10 Dose Axis] --- axisY[Response Percent Axis]
    end

    P1[Threshold NOAEL Region] --> P2[Sub-threshold Zero Response Zone]
    P2 --> P3[Linear Ascending Limb]
    P3 --> P4[Inflection at ED50 or LD50]
    P4 --> P5[Plateau of Maximum Response]
    P5 --> P6[Saturation Zone]

    axisY --- P1
    axisY --- P2
    axisY --- P3
    axisY --- P4
    axisY --- P5
    axisY --- P6

    style P1 fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1
    style P4 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309
    style P5 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c
```

**Interpretation:** Below the NOAEL, no statistically detectable response occurs. Beyond the threshold, the response rises along the steep portion of the sigmoid. The **midpoint** of the steep portion is the $\text{ED}_{50}$ / $\text{LD}_{50}$. As dose continues to climb, the system saturates and the response plateaus (maximal receptor occupancy or maximal physiological effect).

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Threshold vs Non-Threshold Toxicants

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Threshold_Model
        TH1[Dose 0] --> TH2[NOAEL Threshold]
        TH2 --> TH3[Linear Risk Increase Above NOAEL]
    end

    subgraph NonThreshold_Model
        NT1[Any Dose Greater Than Zero] --> NT2[Linear Risk Increase]
        NT2 --> NT3[No Safe Threshold e.g. Carcinogens]
    end

    TH3 ---|"Contrast"| NT1

    style TH2 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d
    style NT3 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Regulatory Implication:** For non-threshold toxicants (genotoxic carcinogens), regulators typically use a **Linear Extrapolation** model, computing a risk-specific dose (e.g., $\text{Cancer Slope Factor} \times \text{Exposure} = \text{Lifetime Cancer Risk}$). No NOAEL is defined.

## 4.4 Hierarchical Toxicity Tree — Major Categories

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Toxic Agents]
    T --> C1[Chemical Toxicants]
    T --> C2[Biological Toxicants]
    T --> C3[Physical Toxicants]

    C1 --> S1[Organic Pesticides]
    C1 --> S2[Heavy Metals]
    C1 --> S3[Persistent Organic Pollutants]
    C1 --> S4[Volatile Organic Compounds]

    C2 --> S5[Bacterial Toxins]
    C2 --> S6[Fungal Mycotoxins]
    C2 --> S7[Marine Biotoxins]
    C2 --> S8[Plant Alkaloids]

    C3 --> S9[Ionizing Radiation]
    C3 --> S10[Non-Ionizing Radiation]
    C3 --> S11[Noise Vibration]

    S1 --> A1[Organochlorines DDT]
    S2 --> A2[Lead Mercury Cadmium]
    S3 --> A3[Dioxins PCBs]
    S5 --> A4[Botulinum Tetanus]
    S6 --> A5[Aflatoxin Ochratoxin]
    S9 --> A6[Alpha Beta Gamma]

    style T fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
    style C1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af
    style C2 fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d
    style C3 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Q1a]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **toxicity**, **hazard**, and **risk** in the context of environmental toxicology.

**Model Answer:**
- **Toxicity** is the *intrinsic biological property* of a chemical to cause harm; it is a fixed characteristic of the substance (e.g., botulinum is highly toxic regardless of context).
- **Hazard** is the *probability* that a substance will cause harm under specific use/exposure conditions.
- **Risk** is the *quantitative product* of hazard and exposure, often expressed as:

$$\text{Risk} = \text{Hazard} \times \text{Exposure}$$

A chemical can be highly toxic but pose minimal risk if exposure is controlled (e.g., cyanide in a sealed laboratory). Conversely, a mildly toxic substance (e.g., sugar) can pose risk if exposure is excessive. *(3 marks: 1 mark each for definition + example)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q1b]**

**Q2.** Define $\text{LD}_{50}$, **NOAEL**, and **Therapeutic Index**. State one practical limitation of each.

**Model Answer:**
- **$\text{LD}_{50}$** — the single dose that kills 50 % of the test population; *limitation*: requires large numbers of animal deaths, ignores sub-lethal chronic effects, and cannot be extrapolated cleanly across species.
- **NOAEL** — highest dose at which no statistically significant adverse effect is observed; *limitation*: dependent on dose spacing, sample size, and end-point sensitivity; chronic low-dose effects below NOAEL are possible (hormesis).
- **Therapeutic Index** — $\text{TI} = \text{TD}_{50} / \text{ED}_{50}$; *limitation*: ignores slope variability and individual susceptibility; some compounds have narrow TI (e.g., digoxin, warfarin).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester, Module 4, Full Module Question Choice Pattern]**

### Question A — (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 ESE, Module 4 — Q8a]**

**Q8a (i)** With the help of a clearly labelled diagram, explain the **graded dose–response relationship** and discuss the significance of the **threshold dose**, **$ED_{50}$**, and **maximal response**.

**Q8a (ii)** Describe the **ADME processes** in toxicokinetics and explain how each step influences the internal dose delivered to the target organ.

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 ESE, Module 4 — Q8b]**

**Q8b (i)** Define **dose–response curve**. Distinguish between **quantal** and **graded** dose–response relationships. Explain the concept of **potency** and how it differs from **toxicity**.

**Q8b (ii)** A pesticide is tested on rats in five dose groups (n = 8 each). The 24-hour mortalities are recorded as follows. Estimate the **$\text{LD}_{50}$** using probit analysis and state its 95 % confidence interval.

| Dose (mg/kg) | 50 | 100 | 200 | 400 | 800 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Deaths | 1/8 | 3/8 | 4/8 | 6/8 | 7/8 |
| % Mortality | 12.5 | 37.5 | 50.0 | 75.0 | 87.5 |

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solution — Question A

### Solution to Q8a (i) — Graded Dose–Response Curve

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
A graded dose–response curve depicts the magnitude of a *continuous* biological response (e.g., enzyme inhibition, blood pressure change, percent contraction of an isolated muscle) in a single individual (or averaged population) plotted against the logarithm of the administered dose.

**Step 2 — Curve Anatomy (3 marks):**

```
Response %  |_______________________
            |                        _______ E_max plateau
            |                  ___---
   E_max    |                _-
            |              _-
   E_max/2  |-------------+--------------  ED_50 inflection point
            |           _-
            |         _-
            |       _-
            |     _-
       0    |____-__________________________  log10 Dose (mg/kg)
            0  NOAEL                 EC_100
            |<----->|<------->|<------>|
            Thresh.   Linear    Plateau
                       rise     region
```

**Step 3 — Significance of Key Points (2 marks):**
- **Threshold dose (NOAEL):** Dose below which no measurable effect is produced; forms the basis of safety standards.
- **$\text{ED}_{50}$:** Dose at which the response equals 50 % of $E_{max}$. Used to compare relative potency of compounds.
- **Maximal response ($E_{max}$):** The ceiling effect; further dose increase yields no additional response (receptor saturation).

### Solution to Q8a (ii) — ADME Processes

**(a) Absorption (2 marks):** Passage of the chemical across biological membranes (GI tract, alveolar epithelium, dermis). Governed by Fick's law: $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$. Lipid-soluble, low-molecular-weight compounds are absorbed most readily.

**(b) Distribution (2 marks):** Reversible partitioning between plasma and tissues, characterized by $V_d$. Plasma protein binding (especially to albumin) restricts distribution. Lipophilic compounds accumulate in adipose tissue (DDT, dioxins), creating long-term internal reservoirs.

**(c) Metabolism (2 marks):** Liver-mediated Phase I (CYP450 oxidation) and Phase II (conjugation). Outcomes: bioactivation (parent → toxic metabolite) or detoxification (parent → excretable metabolite). Polymorphisms in CYP genes cause large inter-individual variation.

**(d) Excretion (1 mark):** Primarily renal (small, water-soluble compounds) and biliary (larger, conjugated metabolites). Other routes: pulmonary (volatile organics, CO₂), sweat, milk.

**(e) Integrated Influence on Internal Dose (1 mark):** The amount reaching the target organ equals the *bioavailable fraction* that survives metabolism and is not excreted. ADME collectively determines whether a large environmental dose yields a small or large *target-tissue dose*.

---

## 5.4 Complete Model Solution — Question B

### Solution to Q8b (i) — Definitions and Distinctions

**Definition (2 marks):**
A dose–response curve is a graphical/mathematical representation of the relationship between the magnitude of an administered dose and the resulting biological response in an organism or population.

**Graded vs Quantal (3 marks):**

| Feature | Graded | Quantal |
|---|---|---|
| Measured in | Single individual | Population |
| Output type | Continuous (e.g., % change) | All-or-none (death/tumor yes/no) |
| Plot | Response vs log Dose | % responding vs log Dose |
| Statistical model | Regression (Hill) | Probit / Logit |

**Potency vs Toxicity (2 marks):**
- **Potency** is the position of the dose–response curve along the dose axis. A more potent chemical reaches its $\text{ED}_{50}$ at a *lower* dose.
- **Toxicity** is the inverse of potency in the context of adverse effects; a more toxic compound has a *lower* $\text{LD}_{50}$.
- Two chemicals can have identical *maximum effect* ($E_{max}$) but differ in potency — therefore toxicants must be compared on **dose** not "strength."

### Solution to Q8b (ii) — Probit $\text{LD}_{50}$ Estimation

**Step 1 — Convert Dose and Mortality to $\log_{10}(D)$ and Probit (1 mark):**

| Dose | $\log_{10}(D)$ = $x$ | Mortality $p$ (%) | Probit $Y$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 50 | 1.6990 | 12.5 | 3.85 |
| 100 | 2.0000 | 37.5 | 4.68 |
| 200 | 2.3010 | 50.0 | 5.00 |
| 400 | 2.6021 | 75.0 | 5.67 |
| 800 | 2.9031 | 87.5 | 6.15 |

**Step 2 — Means (1 mark):**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1.699 + 2.000 + 2.301 + 2.602 + 2.903}{5} = 2.301$$

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{3.85 + 4.68 + 5.00 + 5.67 + 6.15}{5} = 5.07$$

**Step 3 — Slope $b$ (2 marks):**

| $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $Y_i - \bar{Y}$ | Product | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| −0.602 | −1.220 | 0.7344 | 0.3624 |
| −0.301 | −0.390 | 0.1174 | 0.0906 |
| 0.000 | −0.070 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 |
| +0.301 | +0.600 | 0.1806 | 0.0906 |
| +0.602 | +1.080 | 0.6502 | 0.3624 |
| **Σ** | | **1.6826** | **0.9060** |

$$b = \frac{1.6826}{0.9060} = 1.857$$

**Step 4 — Intercept $a$ (1 mark):**

$$a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{x} = 5.07 - (1.857)(2.301) = 5.07 - 4.273 = 0.797$$

**Step 5 — Probit Equation (1 mark):**

$$Y = 0.797 + 1.857 \log_{10}(D)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\text{LD}_{50}$ (2 marks):** Setting $Y = 5.00$:

$$\log_{10}(LD_{50}) = \frac{5.00 - 0.797}{1.857} = \frac{4.203}{1.857} = 2.263$$

$$\boxed{LD_{50} = 10^{2.263} \approx 183 \; \text{mg/kg}}$$

**Step 7 — 95 % Confidence Interval (1 mark):** Standard error of $\log_{10}(\text{LD}_{50})$:

$$SE = \frac{1}{b}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\sum n_i}} = \frac{1}{1.857}\sqrt{\frac{1}{40}} = \frac{1}{1.857}(0.1581) = 0.0851$$

$$\log LD_{50} = 2.263 \pm 1.96(0.0851) = 2.263 \pm 0.167$$

$$\text{95 \% CI} = 10^{2.096} \;\text{to}\; 10^{2.430} = 124.7 \;\text{to}\; 269.2 \; \text{mg/kg}$$

**Final Answer (3 marks for full computation):**
$$\text{LD}_{50} = 183 \; \text{mg/kg} \quad (95\% \; \text{CI}: 125 - 269 \; \text{mg/kg})$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Avoid These Common Errors:**
> 1. **Confusing % with Probit:** Many students directly use mortality percent instead of converting to probit. The probit value of 50 % is **5.00**, not 50.
> 2. **Forgetting to take $\log_{10}$ of Dose:** Linear dose axis yields a hyperbolic curve; probit regression requires $\log_{10}(D)$ for linearity.
> 3. **Not stating units:** Always write "**mg/kg body weight**" after $\text{LD}_{50}$. Examiners deduct ½ mark for missing units.
> 4. **Mixing Potency and Toxicity:** Potency refers to dose position; toxicity refers to harm potential. Do *not* use them interchangeably.
> 5. **Skipping ADME explanations:** When asked about toxicity, 70 % of students ignore ADME — but dose–response *starts* at the target organ concentration, which ADME determines.
> 6. **No diagram in long answers:** A hand-drawn sigmoid curve with labelled axes earns 1–2 free marks.

---

## 5.5 TOPIC RECAP — HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Read this section **twice** the night before the exam. Every line below corresponds to a high-frequency question topic.

- ✅ **Paracelsus Principle:** *"Sola dosis facit venenum"* — only the dose makes the poison.
- ✅ Three pillars: **Environmental Chemistry + Biology + Toxicology**.
- ✅ **Toxicant** (substance) vs **Toxin** (biogenic) vs **Toxicagogue** (induces toxin production).
- ✅ Toxicity = intrinsic property; Hazard = exposure scenario; Risk = Toxicity × Exposure.
- ✅ ADME = Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion.
- ✅ Phase I = Functionalization (CYP450); Phase II = Conjugation (glucuronidation, sulfation).
- ✅ **Volume of Distribution** $V_d = \dfrac{A_{body}}{C_{plasma}}$.
- ✅ First-order elimination: $C_t = C_0 e^{-kt}$; $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$.
- ✅ **Threshold toxicants** exhibit a NOAEL; **non-threshold** (genotoxic) toxicants do not.
- ✅ Graded response = continuous; Quantal response = all-or-none.
- ✅ Hill equation parameters: $E_{min}, E_{max}, ED_{50}, n$.
- ✅ Hill coefficient $n$ describes slope/steepness of the sigmoid.
- ✅ $\text{LD}_{50}$, $\text{LC}_{50}$, $\text{ED}_{50}$, $\text{TD}_{50}$ — *all are quantal* median-effect doses.
- ✅ NOAEL < LOAEL; ADI uses NOAEL/UF; RfD ≈ ADI.
- ✅ **Therapeutic Index** $\text{TI} = \text{TD}_{50}/\text{ED}_{50}$; high TI ⇒ safer.
- ✅ **Hazard Quotient** $\text{HQ} = \text{Exposure}/\text{RfD}$; $\text{HI} = \sum HQ_i$.
- ✅ Probit regression: $Y = a + b \log_{10}(D)$; solve at $Y = 5.00$ for $\text{LD}_{50}$.
- ✅ BCF > 1000 ⇒ bioaccumulative; BAF accounts for dietary uptake.
- ✅ Persistence + bioaccumulation + toxicity = "PBT" criteria under REACH (EU).
- ✅ Endocrine disruptors operate at *parts-per-trillion* levels — hormesis relevance.
- ✅ Bioactivation example: Benzo[a]pyrene → BPDE; Aflatoxin B₁ → AFB₁-epoxide.
- ✅ Relative potency = $\text{LD}_{50}^{(B)}/\text{LD}_{50}^{(A)}$; ratio > 1 ⇒ A is more potent.
- ✅ Dose–response curves plotted on **log dose** axis appear sigmoidal; linear dose axis yields hyperbolic curve.
- ✅ Confidence interval uses standard error of the slope: $SE(\log LD_{50}) = (1/b)\sqrt{1/\sum n_i}$.
- ✅ Hormesis: low-dose stimulation, high-dose inhibition ⇒ J- or U-shaped curve.
- ✅ Memory aid for probit of 50 %: *Probit = 5 by definition* (5 standard deviations above mean = 50 % cumulative).
- ✅ Lipophilic toxicants → adipose storage → prolonged body burden even after exposure ends.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Toxicity Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT602 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND TOXIC RISKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Toxicity Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Toxicity Testing** is a systematic, standardized set of bioanalytical procedures used to quantitatively and qualitatively determine the harmful effects (acute, sub-chronic, or chronic) of a chemical substance, physical agent, or environmental sample on living organisms (biota). It establishes the **cause-and-effect relationship between the dose of a toxicant and the biological response** in target organisms, providing the empirical foundation for environmental risk assessment (ERA) under KTU Module 4.

In the context of **Environmental Engineering**, toxicity testing evaluates the impact of:
- Industrial effluents
- Pesticides and agrochemicals
- Heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr)
- Emerging contaminants (microplastics, PFAS, pharmaceuticals)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Toxicity testing is the standardized experimental determination of the adverse effects of chemicals or effluents on test organisms, providing data on lethal concentrations (LC₅₀), effective concentrations (EC₅₀), no-observed-effect concentrations (NOEC), and bioconcentration factors (BCF) for use in hazard identification and dose-response assessment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine giving **graded doses of a new medicine** to a group of patients to find out the highest dose that doesn't cause harm and the lowest dose that becomes dangerous. That is exactly what toxicity testing does for the environment.

Think of a **swimming pool** filled with water containing an unknown pollutant. To know whether fish can survive in it, we don't dump thousands of fish directly. Instead, we take small fish (like *Daphnia* or *fathead minnows*) and expose them to **different dilutions** of the water in separate tanks. The tank where **50% of the fish die** tells us the toxic threshold. This is the heart of toxicity testing — **controlled exposure to determine the dose that produces a measurable biological effect**.

| Concept | Real-World Parallel |
|---|---|
| Test organism | Volunteer patient in clinical trial |
| Toxicant dose | mg of medicine given |
| LC₅₀ | Dose at which 50% patients react severely |
| NOEC | Highest dose with no side effects |
| Control group | Patient receiving a placebo |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in Bold:**
> - **LD₅₀ = 0** for non-toxic substances (e.g., pure water)
> - **LC₅₀ expressed in mg/L** for aquatic toxicity
> - **Test duration**: 24 h, 48 h, 72 h, or **96 h** (standard for fish acute test)
> - **Temperature**: maintained at **25 ± 1 °C** for tropical bioassays

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Toxicity Tests

### A. Based on Duration of Exposure

1. **Acute Toxicity Test** — Single or short-term exposure (≤ 96 h)
   - Determines **LC₅₀ / LD₅₀**
   - High dose, short duration
   - Endpoint: mortality

2. **Sub-acute (Sub-chronic) Toxicity Test** — Repeated exposure (28–90 days)
   - Lower doses
   - Endpoints: growth, reproduction, behavior

3. **Chronic Toxicity Test** — Long-term exposure (≥ 1 year, or full life cycle)
   - Very low doses
   - Endpoints: carcinogenicity, teratogenicity, mutagenicity, reproductive success

### B. Based on Test System

- **In vivo testing** — On whole living organisms (fish, rats, *Daphnia*)
- **In vitro testing** — On isolated cells, tissues, or bacterial cultures (e.g., Ames test)

### C. Based on Endpoint

- **Lethal tests** → LC₅₀, LD₅₀ (mortality)
- **Sublethal tests** → EC₅₀ (immobilization, enzyme inhibition)
- **Reproductive tests** → NOEC, LOEC

## 2.2 Key Endpoints and Definitions

| Endpoint | Full Form | Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LC₅₀** | Lethal Concentration 50 | mg/L | Concentration causing 50% mortality |
| **LD₅₀** | Lethal Dose 50 | mg/kg body weight | Single dose causing 50% death |
| **EC₅₀** | Effective Concentration 50 | mg/L | Concentration causing 50% effect (e.g., immobilization) |
| **NOEC** | No Observed Effect Concentration | mg/L | Highest tested conc. with no observable effect |
| **LOEC** | Lowest Observed Effect Concentration | mg/L | Lowest tested conc. that produces an effect |
| **TLV** | Threshold Limit Value | ppm or mg/m³ | Safe airborne exposure for humans |
| **BCF** | Bioconcentration Factor | L/kg | Ratio of chemical in organism to that in water |

## 2.3 Dose–Response Relationship

The dose–response curve is the **backbone of toxicology**. It follows a **sigmoidal (S-shaped)** pattern when plotted on a log-dose axis.

- At **low doses**: No observable effect (sub-threshold region)
- At **moderate doses**: Linear or semi-log region (response proportional to log dose)
- At **high doses**: Plateau (saturation — all organisms affected)

This relationship is mathematically modeled using the **Probit** or **Logit** functions:

$$P(\text{response}) = \Phi(\alpha + \beta \log_{10}(D))$$

where Φ is the cumulative standard normal distribution function, D is the dose, α is the intercept, and β is the slope.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All symbols below are KTU-board-approved. Use \vert or \mid to represent absolute values inside the table (never the pipe character).**

| Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $LC_{50} = \dfrac{\sum (m_i \cdot c_i)}{\sum m_i}$ | Moving Average Method | mg/L | Direct computation of LC₅₀ |
| $BCF = \dfrac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}$ | Bioconcentration Factor | L/kg | Bioaccumulation assessment |
| $TU = \dfrac{100}{NOEC\,(\%)}$ | Toxic Units | dimensionless | Effluent toxicity ranking |
| $ADI = \dfrac{NOEL}{SF}$ | Acceptable Daily Intake | mg/kg/day | Human risk assessment |
| $HQ = \dfrac{ADD}{RfD}$ | Hazard Quotient | dimensionless | Non-cancer risk |
| $P = \Phi(\alpha + \beta \log_{10} D)$ | Probit Response Model | probability | Dose–response curve fitting |
| $\text{LC}_{50}(f) = \text{LC}_{50}(t) \cdot \left(\dfrac{f}{t}\right)^{0.15}$ | Toxicity-Time Relationship | mg/L | Extrapolating LC₅₀ across durations |

where:
- $m_i$ = number of organisms dead (or responding) at concentration $c_i$
- $C_{organism}$, $C_{water}$ = chemical concentration in organism and water
- $SF$ = Safety Factor (typically **100** for non-cancer effects)
- $RfD$ = Reference Dose

## 2.5 Engineering Relevance

Toxicity testing data drives regulatory decisions in:
- **Industrial effluent discharge** permits (CPCB/SPCB norms in India)
- **Pesticide registration** (Insecticides Act, 1968)
- **Chemical REACH compliance** (EU)
- **Drinking water safety** (BIS 10500, WHO guidelines)
- **Bioremediation efficacy validation**
- **Solid waste leachate classification** (hazardous vs. non-hazardous)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Calculation of LC₅₀ Using the Trimmed Spearman–Karber Method

This is the **board-favorite derivation** for KTU environmental engineering papers.

### Given:
A bioassay is conducted on **fathead minnows** with five concentrations of an industrial effluent. The test duration is **96 hours**. Results are tabulated as follows:

| Concentration (mg/L) | No. of Fish Exposed | No. of Deaths at 96 h |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | 10 | 0 |
| 18 | 10 | 2 |
| 32 | 10 | 5 |
| 56 | 10 | 8 |
| 100 | 10 | 10 |

### Step 1: Calculate the Mortality Proportion (pᵢ)

The mortality proportion is the number of deaths divided by the number exposed.

$$
p_i = \dfrac{\text{No. of deaths}}{\text{No. exposed}}
$$

$$
p_1 = \dfrac{0}{10} = 0.00, \quad p_2 = \dfrac{2}{10} = 0.20, \quad p_3 = \dfrac{5}{10} = 0.50, \quad p_4 = \dfrac{8}{10} = 0.80, \quad p_5 = \dfrac{10}{10} = 1.00
$$

### Step 2: Convert Each Concentration to log₁₀

$$
\log_{10}(10) = 1.000, \quad \log_{10}(18) = 1.255, \quad \log_{10}(32) = 1.505, \quad \log_{10}(56) = 1.748, \quad \log_{10}(100) = 2.000
$$

### Step 3: Determine Successive Differences in log-concentration (Δ)

$$
\Delta_1 = 1.255 - 1.000 = 0.255
$$

$$
\Delta_2 = 1.505 - 1.255 = 0.250
$$

$$
\Delta_3 = 1.748 - 1.505 = 0.243
$$

$$
\Delta_4 = 2.000 - 1.748 = 0.252
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Trimmed Spearman–Karber Equation

The general form of the method is:

$$
\log_{10}(LC_{50}) = \log_{10}(c_{\max}) + \dfrac{1}{2} \Delta_1 \left( \dfrac{p_1 + p_2}{2} \right) + \sum_{i=2}^{k-1} \Delta_i \, p_i
$$

where $c_{\max}$ is the highest concentration tested (here, 100 mg/L) and the sum excludes the **0%** and **100%** mortality groups.

### Step 5: Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\log_{10}(LC_{50}) = \log_{10}(100) + \dfrac{1}{2}(0.255)(0.10) + (0.250)(0.20) + (0.243)(0.50) + (0.252)(0.80)
$$

Note that $\dfrac{p_1 + p_2}{2} = \dfrac{0.00 + 0.20}{2} = 0.10$

$$
\log_{10}(LC_{50}) = 2.000 + \dfrac{0.255 \times 0.10}{2} + 0.050 + 0.122 + 0.202
$$

$$
\log_{10}(LC_{50}) = 2.000 + 0.013 + 0.050 + 0.122 + 0.202
$$

$$
\log_{10}(LC_{50}) = 2.387
$$

### Step 6: Take Antilogarithm

$$
LC_{50} = 10^{2.387} = 243.7 \text{ mg/L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The concentration of the industrial effluent that is lethal to 50% of fathead minnows in 96 hours is **243.7 mg/L**. Since this value is well above 100 mg/L, the effluent can be classified as **practically non-toxic to fish** under GESAMP hazard ranking (LC₅₀ > 100 mg/L → Category "Non-toxic").

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bioconcentration Factor (BCF) Calculation

### Given:
A fish is exposed to a polluted lake. After 28 days of equilibration:
- Chemical concentration in water ($C_w$) = 0.05 mg/L
- Chemical concentration in fish tissue ($C_f$) = 12.5 mg/kg (wet weight)

### Step 1: Apply the BCF Formula

$$
BCF = \dfrac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}
$$

### Step 2: Convert Units to Consistent Basis

Since $C_w$ is in mg/L and $C_f$ is in mg/kg, the BCF has units of L/kg.

$$
BCF = \dfrac{12.5 \,\text{mg/kg}}{0.05 \,\text{mg/L}}
$$

### Step 3: Solve

$$
BCF = 250 \,\text{L/kg}
$$

### Step 4: Interpret Using KTU/CEFIC Classification

| BCF Range | Bioaccumulation Potential |
|---|---|
| BCF < 10 | Low |
| 10 ≤ BCF < 100 | Moderate |
| 100 ≤ BCF < 1000 | High |
| BCF ≥ 1000 | Very High (B-compound under EU REACH) |

Since BCF = **250 L/kg** → **High bioaccumulation potential**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Toxic Unit (TU) for Effluent Classification

### Given:
An industrial effluent has NOEC = 8% (i.e., the highest concentration causing no effect in *Daphnia magna* is 8% v/v of the effluent).

### Step 1: Apply the TU Formula

$$
TU = \dfrac{100}{NOEC\,(\%)}
$$

### Step 2: Solve

$$
TU = \dfrac{100}{8} = 12.5
$$

### Step 3: Classify Using Sprague's Chronic Toxicity Scale

| TU Range | Toxicity Class |
|---|---|
| TU < 0.4 | No chronic toxicity |
| 0.4 ≤ TU < 1 | Threshold of chronic toxicity |
| 1 ≤ TU < 10 | Moderate chronic toxicity |
| 10 ≤ TU < 100 | High chronic toxicity |
| TU ≥ 100 | Very high chronic toxicity |

Since TU = **12.5** → **High chronic toxicity**; effluent requires dilution or treatment before discharge.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Test Type | Duration | Endpoint | Species | Stat. Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acute aquatic | 96 h | LC₅₀ | Fathead minnow, *Daphnia* | Spearman–Karber / Probit |
| Chronic aquatic | 28 d | NOEC, LOEC | *Ceriodaphnia*, fish larvae | ANOVA + Dunnett |
| Ames mutagenicity | 48–72 h | Revertant colonies | *Salmonella typhimurium* | Mutagenic ratio |
| Acute oral | 14 d | LD₅₀ | Wistar rats | Litchfield–Wilcoxon |
| Bioconcentration | 28 d | BCF | Bluegill sunfish | Steady-state ratio |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Toxicity Testing Workflow — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Definition and Toxicant Identification] --> B[Selection of Test Organism and Test Type]
    B --> C[Range Finding Test - Preliminary Bioassay]
    C --> D[Definitive Test Setup - Multiple Concentrations with Control]
    D --> E[Exposure Phase - Monitor Mortality and Sublethal Endpoints]
    E --> F{Endpoint Achieved?}
    F -- No --> G[Continue Exposure and Record Data]
    G --> F
    F -- Yes --> H[Statistical Analysis - LC50, EC50, NOEC Determination]
    H --> I[Interpretation and Toxicity Classification]
    I --> J[Reporting and Regulatory Submission]
```

## 4.2 Dose–Response Curve Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Dose Region] -->|No Effect| B[Sub Threshold Zone]
    B -->|Increasing Dose| C[Linear Log Dose Response Zone]
    C -->|High Dose| D[Saturation Plateau Zone 100 Percent Effect]
    D --> E[LC50 Located at Inflection Point]
    E --> F[NOEC Lies in Sub Threshold Zone]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Toxicity Test Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Toxicity Testing Methods] --> B[In Vivo Whole Organism Tests]
    A --> C[In Vitro Cell Based Tests]
    A --> D[In Silico Computational Models]
    B --> E[Acute Test 96 Hours]
    B --> F[Sub Acute Test 28 to 90 Days]
    B --> G[Chronic Test Life Cycle]
    C --> H[Ames Mutagenicity Test]
    C --> I[Cell Culture Cytotoxicity]
    C --> J[Receptor Binding Assays]
    D --> K[QSAR Quantitative Structure Activity]
    D --> L[Read Across Methods]
    E --> M[LC50 and LD50 Determination]
    F --> N[NOEC and LOEC Determination]
    G --> O[Carcinogenicity and Reproductive Studies]
```

## 4.4 Ames Test — Mutagenicity Detection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Test Chemical] --> B[Mix with Salmonella Strain TA98 or TA100]
    B --> C[Add S9 Liver Enzyme Fraction for Metabolic Activation]
    C --> D[Plate on Minimal Agar with Histidine]
    D --> E[Incubate 48 Hours at 37 Degrees Celsius]
    E --> F[Count Revertant Colonies]
    F --> G{Colony Count Greater Than 2x Negative Control}
    G -- Yes --> H[Mutagenic Positive Result]
    G -- No --> I[Non Mutagenic Negative Result]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the S9 fraction?** Many chemicals are not mutagenic in their parent form but become carcinogenic after liver-mediated metabolism (e.g., benzo[a]pyrene). The **S9 fraction** mimics this mammalian metabolic activation in vitro, making the Ames test a powerful proxy for human carcinogenicity screening.

## 4.5 Test Organism Selection Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Test Organism Selection] --> B[Trophic Level Criteria]
    A --> C[Sensitivity Criteria]
    A --> D[Availability and Cost Criteria]
    B --> E[Producer Algae Selenastrum]
    B --> F[Primary Consumer Daphnia magna]
    B --> G[Secondary Consumer Fathead Minnow]
    C --> H[Standardized Strains per OECD Guidelines]
    D --> I[Year Round Availability from Culture Labs]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term LC₅₀. Explain its significance in aquatic toxicity testing.**

**Model Answer:**

LC₅₀ (Lethal Concentration 50) is the concentration of a toxicant in the surrounding medium (usually water) that causes mortality in **50% of the test organisms** within a specified exposure period, typically 96 hours for acute fish bioassays.

**Significance:**
- It is the **most widely used statistical endpoint** for comparing the relative acute toxicity of chemicals.
- It allows ranking of chemicals as **highly toxic (LC₅₀ < 1 mg/L), toxic (1–10 mg/L), moderately toxic (10–100 mg/L), or practically non-toxic (>100 mg/L)**.
- It is the **basis for water quality criteria** and discharge consent limits under the EPA and CPCB.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between NOEC and LOEC with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | NOEC | LOEC |
|---|---|---|
| Full form | No Observed Effect Concentration | Lowest Observed Effect Concentration |
| Meaning | Highest tested conc. at which **no statistically significant effect** is observed | Lowest tested conc. at which a **statistically significant effect** first appears |
| Position on dose–response curve | Lies in the no-effect region | Lies just above the threshold |
| Example | For Zn in *Daphnia*, NOEC = 0.05 mg/L | LOEC = 0.10 mg/L |

The **NOEC is always less than the LOEC**, and both are determined by hypothesis testing (typically ANOVA followed by Dunnett's test) at a **95% confidence level (p < 0.05)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **procedure for conducting an acute toxicity test (96 h)** using *Daphnia magna* as the test organism. State the statistical method for determining the LC₅₀. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data were obtained from an acute static bioassay using *Daphnia magna* exposed to a textile effluent for 48 hours. Determine the **48-h LC₅₀** using the **Trimmed Spearman–Karber method**. **(7 marks)**

| Effluent Concentration (% v/v) | No. of *Daphnia* Exposed | No. Immobilized (Dead) |
|---|---|---|
| 5  | 20 | 0  |
| 10 | 20 | 4  |
| 20 | 20 | 10 |
| 40 | 20 | 16 |
| 80 | 20 | 20 |

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Test organism selection:** Use **neonates (< 24 h old)** of *Daphnia magna* from a single clone, cultured parthenogenetically at 20 ± 1 °C in reconstituted hard water, with 16 h light: 8 h dark photoperiod.

**Step 2 — Range-finding test:** Conduct a preliminary test with widely spaced concentrations (e.g., 1, 10, 100 mg/L or 1%, 10%, 100% effluent) to bracket the LC₅₀.

**Step 3 — Definitive test setup:**
- Use **at least 5 concentrations** plus a control (clean water) in geometric series.
- Maintain **20 neonates per concentration** (in 4 replicates of 5 each).
- Volume: ≥ 50 mL per organism.
- Temperature: **20 ± 1 °C**; DO ≥ 60% saturation; pH 7.0–8.0.

**Step 4 — Exposure and observation:** Record immobilization (no movement for 15 s after gentle agitation) at **24 h, 48 h, 72 h, and 96 h**. Do not feed during the test.

**Step 5 — Statistical analysis:** Apply the **Trimmed Spearman–Karber method** or **Probit analysis** to compute LC₅₀ with 95% confidence limits.

**Block diagram:** Refer to the workflow in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Mortality proportions (pᵢ):**

$$
p_1 = \dfrac{0}{20} = 0.00, \quad p_2 = \dfrac{4}{20} = 0.20, \quad p_3 = \dfrac{10}{20} = 0.50, \quad p_4 = \dfrac{16}{20} = 0.80, \quad p_5 = \dfrac{20}{20} = 1.00
$$

**Step 2 — Log₁₀ concentrations:**

$$
\log_{10}(5) = 0.699, \quad \log_{10}(10) = 1.000, \quad \log_{10}(20) = 1.301, \quad \log_{10}(40) = 1.602, \quad \log_{10}(80) = 1.903
$$

**Step 3 — Successive Δ in log concentration:**

$$
\Delta_1 = 0.301, \quad \Delta_2 = 0.301, \quad \Delta_3 = 0.301, \quad \Delta_4 = 0.301
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Trimmed Spearman–Karber Equation:**

$$
\log_{10}(LC_{50}) = \log_{10}(c_{\max}) + \dfrac{1}{2}\Delta_1 \left( \dfrac{p_1 + p_2}{2} \right) + \Delta_2 p_2 + \Delta_3 p_3 + \Delta_4 p_4
$$

$$
\log_{10}(LC_{50}) = 1.903 + \dfrac{1}{2}(0.301)\left(\dfrac{0.00 + 0.20}{2}\right) + (0.301)(0.20) + (0.301)(0.50) + (0.301)(0.80)
$$

$$
\log_{10}(LC_{50}) = 1.903 + \dfrac{0.301 \times 0.10}{2} + 0.060 + 0.151 + 0.241
$$

$$
\log_{10}(LC_{50}) = 1.903 + 0.015 + 0.060 + 0.151 + 0.241
$$

$$
\log_{10}(LC_{50}) = 2.370
$$

**Step 5 — Antilog:**

$$
LC_{50} = 10^{2.370} = 23.4 \,\% \text{ v/v effluent}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 48-h LC₅₀ of the textile effluent to *Daphnia magna* is approximately **23.4% v/v**, indicating that the undiluted effluent would require a **dilution of ~4.3×** to fall within safe discharge limits for aquatic ecosystems.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** What is the **Bioconcentration Factor (BCF)**? Explain its importance in environmental risk assessment. A fish was exposed to a pesticide in water at 0.02 mg/L for 28 days, after which its whole-body concentration was measured as 6.0 mg/kg. Calculate the BCF and comment on the bioaccumulation potential. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Ames mutagenicity test** in detail. List the standard tester strains used and explain the role of the **S9 metabolic activation system**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Definition:** The Bioconcentration Factor (BCF) is the ratio of the concentration of a chemical in an organism's tissues to its concentration in the surrounding water, measured under steady-state conditions.

$$
BCF = \dfrac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}
$$

**Importance in ERA:**
- Identifies chemicals that **accumulate in the food chain**.
- Used by regulatory bodies (EU REACH, EPA TSCA, India's Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules) to classify **persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic (PBT)** substances.
- BCF ≥ 2000 L/kg → "very bioaccumulative" (vB) under EU CLP.

**Calculation:**

Given: $C_w = 0.02 \,\text{mg/L}$, $C_f = 6.0 \,\text{mg/kg}$

$$
BCF = \dfrac{6.0}{0.02} = 300 \,\text{L/kg}
$$

**Interpretation:** BCF = **300 L/kg** falls in the range **100–1000 L/kg**, indicating **high bioaccumulation potential**. The pesticide is **bioaccumulative** and should be restricted in use near aquatic bodies.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Principle:** The Ames test (developed by **Bruce Ames, 1973**) detects the **mutagenic potential** of a chemical by using histidine-requiring mutant strains of *Salmonella typhimurium* that can revert to histidine-independent (prototrophic) growth upon mutation.

**Procedure:**
1. Cultivate mutant strains (e.g., TA98, TA100, TA1535, TA1537) in nutrient broth.
2. Mix test chemical with the bacterial culture, **with and without S9 liver homogenate** (for metabolic activation).
3. Plate on **minimal agar medium lacking histidine**.
4. Incubate at **37 °C for 48 hours**.
5. Count **revertant colonies** and compare with negative (solvent) and positive controls.

**Standard Tester Strains:**

| Strain | Mutation Type | Detects |
|---|---|---|
| TA98 | Frameshift (hisD3052) | Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons |
| TA100 | Base-pair substitution (hisG46) | Base mutagens, nitrosamines |
| TA1535 | Base-pair substitution | Alkylating agents |
| TA1537 | Frameshift | 9-aminoacridine, ICR compounds |

**Role of S9 Metabolic Activation:**
- The S9 fraction is the **supernatant of liver homogenate** centrifuged at 9,000 × g, containing microsomal enzymes (especially **cytochrome P450**).
- Many procarcinogens (e.g., benzo[a]pyrene, aflatoxin B₁) are **not mutagenic per se** but become **electrophilic intermediates** after Phase I metabolism.
- S9 activation mimics this in vivo conversion, allowing detection of **indirect mutagens** that would otherwise test negative.

**Positive result:** A test chemical is judged **mutagenic** if the number of revertant colonies is **≥ 2× the negative control** (Mutagenic Index ≥ 2), with a **dose–response relationship**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Toxicity Testing Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the time dimension in LC₅₀:** Always specify the exposure duration (e.g., "96-h LC₅₀"), not just "LC₅₀". Examiners deduct 1 mark for this omission.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors in BCF:** $C_w$ in mg/L and $C_f$ in mg/kg give BCF in L/kg. Do not convert to the same basis unless the question demands it.
> 3. **Wrong sign convention in Probit:** The Probit transform requires log₁₀ of dose, not ln. Use of natural log instead of log₁₀ leads to a numerical error worth 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the control group:** In any toxicity test, mention the use of a **negative control (clean dilution water)** and **positive control (reference toxicant like K₂Cr₂O₇)**.
> 5. **Ames test confusion:** Students often forget that TA98 detects **frameshifts** while TA100 detects **base-pair substitutions**. Mixing them up costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Misinterpretation of NOEC/LOEC:** NOEC and LOEC are **tested concentrations**, not mathematically extrapolated values. They depend on the chosen test concentrations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Toxicity testing** = controlled exposure of organisms to graded doses of a toxicant to quantify biological response.
- **Three pillars of aquatic toxicity testing**: *Daphnia magna* (invertebrate), fathead minnow (vertebrate), *Selenastrum capricornutum* (algae) — OECD-recommended triad.
- **LC₅₀** is the **gold-standard acute endpoint**; always report with 95% confidence limits.
- **NOEC/LOEC** are determined by **ANOVA + post-hoc Dunnett test** at p < 0.05.
- **Spearman–Karber** and **Probit analysis** are the two approved statistical methods for LC₅₀.
- **BCF ≥ 2000 L/kg** → very bioaccumulative (EU REACH vB criterion); **BCF ≥ 1000** → REACH B-criterion.
- **Toxic Units (TU)** = 100 / NOEC (%) → used for effluent ranking; TU ≥ 10 → high chronic toxicity.
- **Ames test** uses *Salmonella typhimurium* His⁻ mutants; **S9 fraction** provides mammalian metabolic activation.
- **ADI = NOEL / SF**, where SF = 100 (default) for non-cancer effects.
- **Dose–response curve** is sigmoidal on log-dose axis; **probit model** fits it mathematically.
- **Static vs. renewal vs. flow-through** bioassays: choose based on toxicant persistence and volatility.
- **Reference toxicants** (K₂Cr₂O₇, CdCl₂, NaPCP) validate test sensitivity — must give LC₅₀ within **2 standard deviations** of control chart mean.
- **KTU Module 4 linkage**: Toxicity testing feeds directly into **Module 5 (Risk Characterization)** — LC₅₀, NOEC, and BCF are direct inputs to HQ and cancer slope factor calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism and Excretion) and Toxicokinetics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFT602 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND TOXIC RISKS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism and Excretion) and Toxicokinetics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **ADME** is the foundational pharmacokinetic and toxicokinetic framework describing the four sequential physiological processes a xenobiotic (foreign chemical/toxicant) undergoes after entering a biological system. In environmental toxicology, ADME quantifies the **fate of pollutants, pesticides, heavy metals, and industrial chemicals** inside living organisms. | Term | Strict Definition | |------|-------------------| | **Absorption** | The process by which a toxicant crosses biological membranes and enters the systemic circulation. | | **Distribution** | The reversible translocation of the absorbed toxicant from blood into tissues and organs. | | **Metabolism** | The enzymatic biotransformation of the parent toxicant into metabolites (often Phase I & Phase II reactions). | | **Excretion** | The irreversible removal of the toxicant or its metabolites from the body. | **Toxicokinetics** is the quantitative, time-dependent study of ADME processes specifically for toxic substances, applying mathematical models to predict the concentration of a toxicant in plasma/tissues over time at sub-toxic or toxic doses. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Toxicokinetics is treated as the *mathematical backbone* of risk assessment. Understanding compartmental models is essential for evaluating **NOAEL, LOAEL, and Reference Dose (RfD)** calculations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bus Journey" Model Imagine a toxicant as a **traveler** and the human body as a **large city**: - **Absorption** 🚏 = The traveler enters the city through designated gateways (lungs, skin, gut). - **Distribution** 🚌 = The traveler boards buses (blood) that transport them to different neighborhoods (organs/tissues). Some travelers stay in one neighborhood for a long time (fat, bone). - **Metabolism** 🔧 = The traveler passes through a transformation plant (liver) where their appearance/identity changes. - **Excretion** ✈️ = The transformed traveler leaves the city through specific exits (kidneys, feces, breath). This "Bus Journey" analogy is widely used in KTU board answers to score **2 of the 3 marks** in short-answer questions. > [!NOTE] > **Bioavailability (F)** is defined as the fraction (0 ≤ F ≤ 1) of the administered toxicant that reaches systemic circulation in an unchanged form. For intravenous (IV) administration, **F = 1** by definition. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Detailed Process Breakdown #### **A. Absorption** Routes of exposure (in order of KTU frequency): 1. **Inhalation** — Gases, vapors, particulates via alveoli (Surface Area ≈ 70 m²). 2. **Oral (Ingestion)** — Gastrointestinal tract; subject to first-pass hepatic metabolism. 3. **Dermal** — Skin penetration, often lipid-soluble chemicals. 4. **Parenteral** — Injection/wounds (occupational). **Mechanisms of membrane transport:** - **Passive Diffusion** (Lipid-soluble, follows Fick's Law) - **Facilitated Diffusion** (Carrier-mediated, no ATP) - **Active Transport** (Against gradient, ATP-dependent) - **Endocytosis/Exocytosis** (Bulk transport, particulates) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fick's Law of Diffusion** governs passive absorption: > $$\text{Flux} = \frac{D \cdot A \cdot (C_1 - C_2)}{h}$$ > where $D$ = diffusion coefficient, $A$ = surface area, $h$ = membrane thickness. #### **B. Distribution** Once absorbed, the toxicant partitions between plasma proteins (especially **albumin**) and tissues. - **Volume of Distribution (Vd)**: Apparent volume needed to contain the total dose at the observed plasma concentration. - Tissues with high blood flow (brain, liver, kidneys) equilibrate faster than poorly perfused tissues (fat, bone). **Lipophilic chemicals** (e.g., DDT, dioxins) accumulate in **adipose tissue** — a phenomenon called **bioaccumulation**. #### **C. Metabolism (Biotransformation)** Primarily occurs in the **liver**, mediated by the **Cytochrome P450 (CYP450)** enzyme superfamily. | Phase | Reaction Type | Consequence | |-------|---------------|-------------| | **Phase I** | Oxidation, Reduction, Hydrolysis (CYP450) | Adds/unmasks functional groups; may activate pro-toxins (e.g., benzo[a]pyrene → epoxide). | | **Phase II** | Conjugation (Glucuronidation, Sulfation, Glutathione, Acetylation) | Increases water solubility for excretion. | > [!WARNING] > **Bioactivation vs. Detoxification**: Phase I reactions can convert *benign* parent compounds into *highly reactive electrophiles* (ultimate carcinogens). This is the molecular basis of chemical carcinogenesis and is a frequent 7-mark question topic. #### **D. Excretion** Major routes: 1. **Renal (Kidneys)** — Primary route for water-soluble metabolites. 2. **Biliary/Fecal** — For molecules > 500 Da with polar groups. 3. **Pulmonary** — Volatile substances (e.g., organic solvents, CO₂). 4. **Minor routes** — Sweat, saliva, breast milk, hair. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Symbol | Parameter | Formula | Units | |--------|-----------|---------|-------| | $C_t$ | Plasma concentration at time $t$ | $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_e t}$ | mg/L | | $k_e$ | Elimination rate constant | $k_e = \frac{\ln(C_1/C_2)}{t_2 - t_1}$ | h⁻¹ | | $t_{1/2}$ | Half-life | $t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k_e}$ | h | | $V_d$ | Volume of distribution | $V_d = \frac{\text{Dose}}{C_0}$ | L | | $CL$ | Total body clearance | $CL = k_e \cdot V_d = \frac{\text{Rate}}{C_{ss}}$ | L/h | | $AUC$ | Area under curve | $AUC = \frac{F \cdot \text{Dose}}{CL}$ | mg·h/L | | $F$ | Bioavailability | $F = \frac{AUC_{\text{oral}}}{AUC_{\text{IV}}}$ | dimensionless | | $C_{ss}$ | Steady-state concentration | $C_{ss} = \frac{F \cdot \text{Rate}}{CL}$ | mg/L | > [!NOTE] > **Use `\vert` for absolute values** in exam answers — e.g., $V_d = \frac{\text{Dose}}{\vert C_0 \vert}$, NOT $\vert C_0 \vert$ in plain text, to avoid table parsing errors. ### 2.3 Engineering & Real-World Utility - **Risk Assessment**: ADME parameters determine internal dose, which is more accurate than administered dose for predicting toxicity. - **Drug/Pesticide Design**: Pharmacokineticists optimize $F$, $V_d$, $t_{1/2}$ for efficacy and safety. - **Environmental Forensics**: Bioaccumulation factor (BAF) and bioconcentration factor (BCF) are derived from $V_d$ concepts. - **Occupational Health**: Used to set Threshold Limit Values (TLV) and Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Elimination Kinetics **Assumption**: Rate of elimination is proportional to plasma concentration. **Step 1: State the differential equation** The rate of change of toxicant amount in the body is: $$\frac{dA}{dt} = -k_e \cdot A(t)$$ Here, $A(t)$ is the amount of toxicant remaining at time $t$, and $k_e$ is the first-order elimination rate constant (units: **h⁻¹** or **min⁻¹**). **Step 2: Separate variables** $$\frac{dA}{A} = -k_e \cdot dt$$ **Step 3: Integrate both sides from initial amount $A_0$ to $A(t)$ over time 0 to $t$** $$\int_{A_0}^{A(t)} \frac{dA}{A} = -k_e \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ **Step 4: Evaluate the definite integral** $$\ln A(t) - \ln A_0 = -k_e \cdot t$$ **Step 5: Solve for $A(t)$** $$\ln\left(\frac{A(t)}{A_0}\right) = -k_e \cdot t$$ $$A(t) = A_0 \cdot e^{-k_e t}$$ **Step 6: Convert amount to concentration using $V_d$** $$C_t = \frac{A(t)}{V_d} = \frac{A_0}{V_d} \cdot e^{-k_e t}$$ $$C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_e t}$$ This is the **hallmark equation** for a one-compartment model following IV bolus administration. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Half-Life ($t_{1/2}$) **Step 1**: At $t = t_{1/2}$, the concentration falls to half of initial: $C_t = \frac{C_0}{2}$. **Step 2**: Substitute into the first-order equation: $$\frac{C_0}{2} = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_e t_{1/2}}$$ **Step 3**: Divide both sides by $C_0$: $$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k_e t_{1/2}}$$ **Step 4**: Take natural logarithm on both sides: $$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -k_e t_{1/2}$$ **Step 5**: Since $\ln(1/2) = -\ln 2 \approx -0.693$: $$-\ln 2 = -k_e t_{1/2}$$ **Step 6**: Solve for $t_{1/2}$: $$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_e} = \frac{0.693}{k_e}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap**: Half-life depends ONLY on $k_e$ (and hence on $V_d$ and clearance), **NOT** on the administered dose. Doubling the dose does NOT double the half-life in first-order kinetics — a classic 7-mark conceptual question. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: One-Compartment Model — IV Bolus **Set-up**: - Single dose $D$ injected at $t = 0$. - Instantaneous distribution throughout body volume $V_d$. - First-order elimination. **Equation derivation**: $$C_t = \frac{D}{V_d} \cdot e^{-k_e t}$$ **Analytical Matrix — Concentration at Different Times**: | Time ($t$) | Multiples of $t_{1/2}$ | $C_t / C_0$ | % Remaining | |------------|------------------------|-------------|-------------| | 0 | 0 | 1.000 | 100.0% | | $t_{1/2}$ | 1 | 0.500 | 50.0% | | $2 t_{1/2}$ | 2 | 0.250 | 25.0% | | $3 t_{1/2}$ | 3 | 0.125 | 12.5% | | $4 t_{1/2}$ | 4 | 0.0625 | 6.25% | | $5 t_{1/2}$ | 5 | 0.03125 | 3.125% | > [!NOTE] > After **5 half-lives**, ~97% of the toxicant is eliminated — used as the practical endpoint in clinical and environmental toxicology. ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Two-Compartment Model (Brief, for 14-mark questions) When distribution is NOT instantaneous (lipophilic compounds, e.g., DDT), the body is split into: - **Central Compartment** (blood, well-perfused organs) — volume $V_1$ - **Peripheral Compartment** (fat, poorly perfused) — volume $V_2$ The bi-exponential equation is: $$C_t = A \cdot e^{-\alpha t} + B \cdot e^{-\beta t}$$ where: - $\alpha$ = distribution rate constant (rapid phase) - $\beta$ = elimination rate constant (slow terminal phase) - $A$ and $B$ are intercept coefficients. **Terminal half-life** (the one reported in toxicology): $$t_{1/2\beta} = \frac{0.693}{\beta}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 ADME Overview — Body-Level Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR EX["EXPOSURE ROUTES
Inhalation | Oral | Dermal"] ABS["ABSORPTION
Membrane Crossing
Fick's Law"] DIST["DISTRIBUTION
Blood + Plasma Proteins
Tissue Partitioning"] MET["METABOLISM
Liver CYP450
Phase I + Phase II"] EFF["EFFECT SITE
Receptor Binding
Toxic Response"] EXC["EXCRETION
Kidney | Bile | Lung"] EX --> ABS ABS --> DIST DIST --> MET MET --> EFF MET --> EXC DIST --> EFF ``` ### 4.2 Toxicokinetics Compartmental Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CENTRAL["CENTRAL COMPARTMENT (V1)"] BLOOD["Blood Plasma
Highly Perfused Organs
Liver, Kidney, Brain"] end subgraph PERIPHERAL["PERIPHERAL COMPARTMENT (V2)"] FAT["Adipose Tissue
Bone
Slow Equilibration"] end EXTERNAL["EXTERNAL DOSE
D = 100 mg"] -->|k_a Absorption| BLOOD BLOOD -->|k_12 Distribution| FAT FAT -->|k_21 Redistribution| BLOOD BLOOD -->|k_e Elimination| URINE["Urine/Feces
Excretion"] style CENTRAL fill:#e1f5ff style PERIPHERAL fill:#fff4e1 ``` ### 4.3 Biotransformation Pathway Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR PARENT["PARENT COMPOUND
Lipophilic
Non-polar"] P1["PHASE I
CYP450
Oxidation
Reduction
Hydrolysis"] INTER["INTERMEDIATE
Reactive Metabolite
May be toxic epoxide"] P2["PHASE II
Conjugation
Glucuronidation
Glutathione"] FINAL["WATER-SOLUBLE
METABOLITE
Excreted via Kidney/Bile"] PARENT --> P1 P1 --> INTER INTER --> P2 P2 --> FINAL INTER -.->|Bioactivation| TOX["DNA/Protein
Adduct Formation
Carcinogenesis"] style P1 fill:#ffe1e1 style P2 fill:#e1ffe1 style TOX fill:#ff9999 ``` ### 4.4 Plasma Concentration-Time Curve (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PHASES["PHARMACOKINETIC PHASES AFTER IV BOLUS"] P1["ABSORPTIVE PHASE
(Not Applicable for IV)
t = 0: C_max = C_0"] P2["DISTRIBUTIVE PHASE
Rapid Decline
alpha phase"] P3["ELIMINATIVE PHASE
Slow Terminal Decline
beta phase"] end P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> END["After 5 x t1/2
>97% Eliminated"] style P1 fill:#fff4cc style P2 fill:#cce5ff style P3 fill:#ccffcc ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)** --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define ADME. List any two routes of exposure for environmental toxicants. **Model Answer:** ADME stands for **Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, and Excretion** — the four sequential physiological processes that determine the fate of a xenobiotic inside the body. The four components are: - **Absorption**: Entry of toxicant into systemic circulation via lungs, skin, or GIT. - **Distribution**: Reversible transport from blood to tissues. - **Metabolism**: Enzymatic biotransformation (Phase I & II), primarily in the liver. - **Excretion**: Irreversible removal via kidneys, bile, or lungs. **Two routes of exposure:** 1. **Inhalation** — via alveolar absorption (e.g., CO, SO₂, particulates). 2. **Dermal contact** — through skin penetration (e.g., pesticides, organic solvents). *(3 marks — full marks for clear definition + 2 distinct routes)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** What is the volume of distribution ($V_d$)? Mention its significance. **Model Answer:** The **apparent volume of distribution ($V_d$)** is the theoretical volume of plasma in which the total administered dose of a toxicant would need to be uniformly distributed to produce the observed plasma concentration. $$V_d = \frac{\text{Dose administered}}{C_0}$$ where $C_0$ is the initial plasma concentration. **Significance:** - A large $V_d$ (> total body water) indicates extensive **tissue binding or accumulation** (e.g., lipophilic toxicants in fat). - A small $V_d$ indicates the toxicant remains largely in plasma (e.g., highly protein-bound chemicals like warfarin). - $V_d$ is essential for calculating **loading dose** and interpreting **half-life**. *(3 marks)* --- ### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)** > **Internal Choice**: Answer **ANY ONE** of the following. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain in detail the various **routes of absorption** of toxicants in the human body, with a note on **Fick's Law of Diffusion**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **Phase I and Phase II biotransformation reactions** with suitable examples. Why is Phase I considered a double-edged sword? **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** **Routes of Absorption:** 1. **Inhalation (Pulmonary Route)**: - Most rapid and significant for airborne toxicants (gases, vapors, aerosols). - Large surface area (~70 m²) and thin alveolar membrane (~0.5 µm) facilitate rapid diffusion. - Example: CO, benzene, NO₂, ozone. 2. **Oral (Gastrointestinal Route)**: - Toxicants ingested via food, water, or contaminated hands. - Subject to **first-pass hepatic metabolism** before reaching systemic circulation. - pH-dependent absorption: weak acids absorbed in stomach, weak bases in intestine. - Example: Pesticide residues, heavy metals (Pb, Cd). 3. **Dermal Route**: - Skin is a multi-layered barrier; toxicants must traverse the **stratum corneum**. - Lipid-soluble compounds penetrate more easily. - Example: Organophosphates, phenolic compounds, aniline dyes. 4. **Parenteral Route**: - Direct entry via injection, wounds, or bites. - Bypasses absorption barriers entirely. **Fick's Law of Diffusion:** $$\text{Flux} = \frac{D \cdot A \cdot (C_1 - C_2)}{h}$$ where: - $D$ = diffusion coefficient (cm²/s) - $A$ = surface area available (cm²) - $(C_1 - C_2)$ = concentration gradient - $h$ = membrane thickness (cm) **Application**: Explains why thin membranes (alveoli) and high surface areas (intestines) accelerate absorption. Toxicants with higher lipid solubility (greater $D$) are absorbed faster. *(7 marks: 1 mark per route + 2 marks for Fick's Law derivation + 1 mark for application)* --- **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Phase I Reactions:** - Functionalization reactions that introduce or unmask a polar group. - Catalyzed primarily by the **Cytochrome P450 (CYP450)** monooxygenase system in the liver. - Reactions: **Oxidation** (e.g., hydroxylation of benzene to phenol), **Reduction** (e.g., nitro group → amine), **Hydrolysis** (e.g., esters → acid + alcohol). - Example: Conversion of ethanol → acetaldehyde by **alcohol dehydrogenase**. **Phase II Reactions:** - Conjugation reactions that covalently link endogenous molecules to Phase I products. - Significantly increase water solubility. - Reactions: **Glucuronidation** (UDP-glucuronosyltransferase), **Sulfation**, **Glutathione conjugation**, **Acetylation**, **Methylation**. - Example: Bilirubin → bilirubin glucuronide for biliary excretion. **Why Phase I is a "Double-Edged Sword":** - **Detoxification edge**: Converts lipophilic toxicants into more water-soluble, excretable metabolites. - **Bioactivation edge**: Phase I can generate **reactive intermediates** (electrophilic epoxides, quinones, free radicals) that covalently bind to DNA, proteins, and lipids — causing **mutagenesis, carcinogenesis, and tissue necrosis**. - Example: **Benzo[a]pyrene** (pro-carcinogen) → **BPDE-7,8-diol-9,10-epoxide** (ultimate carcinogen) via CYP1A1. Thus Phase I is beneficial when detoxification occurs but hazardous when activation occurs. *(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 marks distribution)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a)** Derive the **first-order elimination equation** for a one-compartment toxicokinetic model. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A worker is accidentally exposed to **200 mg** of an organic solvent via IV route. The toxicant has an apparent **volume of distribution $V_d$ = 40 L** and an **elimination rate constant $k_e$ = 0.05 h⁻¹**. Calculate: **(i)** Initial plasma concentration $C_0$, **(ii)** Half-life $t_{1/2}$, **(iii)** Plasma concentration after **10 hours**, and **(iv)** Time to eliminate **95%** of the dose. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** *(Refer to Section 3.1 — Derivation 1 for the complete step-by-step derivation.)* **Final derived equation:** $$C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_e t}$$ where $C_0 = \dfrac{D}{V_d}$. The derivation proceeds from the differential equation: $$\frac{dA}{dt} = -k_e A$$ through separation, integration, and conversion to concentration form.
