This equation assumes: - Single IV bolus dose. - Instantaneous distribution (one compartment). - First-order elimination. *(7 marks: 2 marks for setup, 3 marks for integration, 2 marks for final equation)* --- **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Given:** - Dose $D = 200$ mg - $V_d = 40$ L - $k_e = 0.05$ h⁻¹ **(i) Initial plasma concentration $C_0$:** $$C_0 = \frac{D}{V_d} = \frac{200 \text{ mg}}{40 \text{ L}}$$ $$C_0 = 5 \text{ mg/L}$$ **(ii) Half-life $t_{1/2}$:** $$t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k_e} = \frac{0.693}{0.05 \text{ h}^{-1}}$$ $$t_{1/2} = 13.86 \text{ h}$$ **(iii) Plasma concentration after 10 hours:** $$C_{10} = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_e t}$$ $$C_{10} = 5 \cdot e^{-(0.05)(10)}$$ $$C_{10} = 5 \cdot e^{-0.5}$$ $$C_{10} = 5 \cdot 0.6065$$ $$C_{10} \approx 3.03 \text{ mg/L}$$ **(iv) Time to eliminate 95% (i.e., $C_t = 5\%$ of $C_0$):** $$0.05 \cdot C_0 = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_e t}$$ $$0.05 = e^{-0.05 t}$$ $$\ln(0.05) = -0.05 t$$ $$-2.996 = -0.05 t$$ $$t = \frac{2.996}{0.05} \approx 59.91 \text{ h}$$ Alternatively, since $0.05 \approx (1/2)^{5.36}$, $t \approx 5.36 \times t_{1/2} \approx 74.3$ h. Using exact log gives **59.91 h**. *(7 marks: ~1.75 marks per sub-part)* --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (as per KTU Board Pattern):** > 1. **Confusing $V_d$ with actual body water volume** — $V_d$ is *apparent*, not real. Always write the units (L) and units are often misinterpreted. > 2. **Forgetting the unit of $k_e$** — Must be in **h⁻¹** or **min⁻¹**; examiners deduct **½ mark** if missing. > 3. **Mixing zero-order and first-order kinetics** — Alcohol (ethanol) follows **zero-order (Michaelis-Menten)** kinetics at high concentrations; most other toxicants follow **first-order**. > 4. **Using $e$ directly without intermediate log step** in derivation — Examiners award **step marks** for each integration step; do not skip. > 5. **Stating "Phase II = detoxification"** — This is FALSE. Some Phase II reactions (e.g., N-hydroxylation of aromatic amines via NAT) can also activate pro-toxins. > 6. **Forgetting AUC units** — Must be **mg·h/L**, not just mg/L. > 7. **No mention of "first-pass effect"** when discussing oral absorption — A guaranteed **1-mark loss** in 7-mark questions. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **ADME** governs the *internal dose* of every toxicant — it is the bridge between exposure and effect. - ✅ **Fick's Law**: $\text{Flux} = \dfrac{D \cdot A \cdot \Delta C}{h}$ — thin + large + lipid-soluble = rapid absorption. - ✅ **Three major exposure routes**: Inhalation, Oral, Dermal. - ✅ **CYP450** in liver → **Phase I** (oxidation/reduction/hydrolysis) → **Phase II** (conjugation). - ✅ **Phase I bioactivation** is the molecular origin of many chemical carcinogens (e.g., BPDE from benzo[a]pyrene). - ✅ **One-compartment model** first-order equation: $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_e t}$. - ✅ **Half-life**: $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{k_e}$ — independent of dose. - ✅ **Volume of distribution**: $V_d = \dfrac{D}{C_0}$ — large $V_d$ = tissue accumulation. - ✅ **Clearance**: $CL = k_e \cdot V_d$ — fundamental to steady-state calculations. - ✅ **AUC** directly proportional to **bioavailability (F)** and **dose**, inversely proportional to **clearance**. - ✅ **After 5 half-lives**, ~97% of the toxicant is eliminated. - ✅ **Steady-state concentration**: $C_{ss} = \dfrac{F \cdot \text{Rate}_{inf}}{CL}$ — critical for chronic exposure risk assessment. - ✅ **Two-compartment model** uses bi-exponential $C_t = A e^{-\alpha t} + B e^{-\beta t}$ for lipophilic, slowly equilibrating toxicants. - ✅ **Bioaccumulation** is observed with high-$V_d$, lipophilic chemicals (DDT, dioxins, PCBs).

topics.title: Classes of Environmental Chemicals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFT602 - ENVIRONMENTAL ENGINEERING AND TOXIC RISKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classes of Environmental Chemicals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Environmental chemicals** are anthropogenic (human-made) or geogenic (naturally occurring) substances introduced into the environment in quantities, concentrations, or forms that exceed natural baseline levels, producing adverse ecological or human-health effects. In toxicological classification (per KTU Module 4 framework), these chemicals are systematically grouped into *classes* based on:

- **Chemical structure** (organic vs. inorganic; aromatic vs. aliphatic)
- **Persistence** (degradation half-life in environmental media)
- **Bioaccumulative potential** (Bioconcentration Factor, BCF)
- **Toxicological action** (mechanism of toxicity)
- **Regulatory status** (priority pollutants, POPs, hazardous air pollutants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Recall Box:**
> "Classes of Environmental Chemicals" refers to the systematic taxonomy of toxic substances into categories such as **heavy metals, pesticides, persistent organic pollutants (POPs), volatile organic compounds (VOCs), polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), and emerging contaminants (PFAS, microplastics, pharmaceuticals)** — each class exhibiting distinct environmental fate, transport, and toxicokinetic behaviour.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Tenants" Model

Imagine an **apartment building** representing the environment — air, water, and soil are the three wings.

- **Pests** (like cockroaches, ants) are unwanted tenants that arrive, multiply quickly, and are hard to remove. These represent **non-persistent, water-soluble chemicals** (e.g., ammonia) — they degrade fast but cause acute problems.
- **Squatters** are unwanted occupants who **refuse to leave** and accumulate furniture (toxins) in their bodies over time. These are **persistent bioaccumulative toxins (PBTs)** like DDT, mercury, dioxins.
- **Guests** visit briefly — these are **non-persistent chemicals** like caffeine in wastewater that pass through quickly with minimal effect.
- **Thieves** sneak in and steal (disrupt) the building's electrical system (hormones/enzymes). These are **endocrine disruptors** like BPA, phthalates, atrazine.

This analogy helps recall: **P**ersistent, **B**ioaccumulative, **T**oxic (PBT) chemicals are the most environmentally hazardous because they "refuse to leave" (long half-life) and "hoard toxins" (bioaccumulate).

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters (Engineering Relevance)

| Reason | Practical Implication |
|---|---|
| **Risk Assessment** | Different classes need different exposure limits (e.g., TLV for VOCs vs. MCL for metals) |
| **Treatment Design** | Activated carbon targets organics; ion-exchange/precipitation targets metals |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | EPA's Priority Pollutant List, Stockholm Convention POPs list |
| **Monitoring Strategy** | VOCs need sorbent tubes; metals need acid digestion + ICP-MS |
| **Remediation Technology** | POPs need thermal destruction; metals need stabilisation/solidification |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Stockholm Convention (2001)** identifies 12 initial "Dirty Dozen" persistent organic pollutants (POPs) — a foundational reference for the KTU Module 4 classification framework.

## 1.4 The Seven Master Classes (KTU 2024 Framework)

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures Module 4 around the following canonical classes:

1. **Heavy Metals & Metalloids** — Pb, Hg, Cd, As, Cr
2. **Pesticides** — Organochlorines, Organophosphates, Carbamates, Pyrethroids
3. **Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs)** — PCBs, dioxins, furans
4. **Volatile & Semi-Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs/SVOCs)** — benzene, toluene, TCE
5. **Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs)** — benzo[a]pyrene, naphthalene
6. **Emerging Contaminants (CECs)** — PFAS, microplastics, pharmaceuticals
7. **Radioactive Substances & Inorganic Gases** — radon, NOₓ, SO₂, CO

Each class will be unpacked in **Section 2** with structure, sources, and toxicity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Class 1 — Heavy Metals and Metalloids

### Structural Properties
- **Definition**: Metals with atomic density $\geq 5 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (classic definition) or, more broadly, metals/metalloids toxic at low concentrations.
- **Toxic mechanism**: Bind to sulfhydryl (–SH) groups of enzymes → enzyme inactivation; mimic essential elements (e.g., Cd²⁺ mimics Zn²⁺).

### Sub-classes and Examples
- **Lead (Pb)**: Mining, leaded gasoline (historical), plumbing. Neurotoxic, especially in children (IQ reduction at blood Pb $\geq 5 \mu$g/dL).
- **Mercury (Hg)**: Three forms — elemental (Hg⁰), inorganic (Hg²⁺), organic (methylmercury, CH₃Hg⁺). The Minamata disaster (1956, Japan) is the textbook case of methylmercury bioaccumulation.
- **Cadmium (Cd)**: Batteries, electroplating. Itai-itai disease (Japan, 1950s). Half-life in human kidney: **10–30 years**.
- **Arsenic (As)**: A metalloid. Groundwater contamination in West Bengal, Bangladesh. Carcinogen (Group 1, IARC).
- **Chromium (Cr)**: Cr(VI) is carcinogenic; Cr(III) is an essential nutrient.

### Key Operational Logic
- **Why heavy metals are dangerous**: They are **non-degradable** — they cannot be "destroyed," only transformed in speciation (e.g., Cr⁶⁺ → Cr³⁺ reduction).
- **How they bioaccumulate**: Biomagnification up the food chain — a classic example is mercury in fish, with concentration rising ~10× per trophic level.

## 2.2 Class 2 — Pesticides

### Structural Taxonomy
- **Organochlorines (OCs)**: Chlorinated hydrocarbon backbone. Examples: **DDT, aldrin, dieldrin, endrin, chlordane, heptachlor, lindane, endosulfan**.
  - **Why persistent**: Strong C–Cl bonds, lipophilic (high $\log K_{ow}$), resistant to microbial degradation.
  - **DDT half-life in soil**: ~15 years.
- **Organophosphates (OPs)**: Phosphotriester backbone. Examples: **malathion, parathion, chlorpyrifos, diazinon**.
  - **Mechanism**: Inhibit acetylcholinesterase (AChE) → acetylcholine accumulation → neurotoxicity.
  - **Why less persistent**: Hydrolysable P–O bonds, but **highly acutely toxic** (parathion is a WHO Class Ia "extremely hazardous" pesticide).
- **Carbamates**: Carbamate ester linkage. Examples: **carbofuran, aldicarb, carbaryl**.
  - **Mechanism**: Reversible AChE inhibition (vs. irreversible in OPs).
- **Pyrethroids**: Synthetic analogues of natural pyrethrins. Examples: **cypermethrin, deltamethrin, permethrin**.
  - **Mechanism**: Disrupt sodium channels in nerve axons.
  - **Advantage**: Low mammalian toxicity, biodegradable — favoured replacement for OCs.

### Why This Class Matters
- Pesticides are designed to be **biologically active** at low doses — this is the central paradox of pesticide toxicology.

## 2.3 Class 3 — Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs)

### The "Dirty Dozen" + New Additions
- **Polychlorinated Biphenyls (PCBs)**: 209 congeners, used as dielectric fluids in transformers/capacitors. Banned globally under Stockholm Convention (2001, originally) but still present in legacy equipment.
- **Polychlorinated Dibenzo-p-Dioxins (PCDDs)**: 75 congeners; **2,3,7,8-TCDD** is the most toxic (Seveso disaster, 1976, Italy).
- **Polychlorinated Dibenzofurans (PCDFs)**: 135 congeners; formed as PCB combustion by-products.
- **POP criteria** (Stockholm Convention):
  1. Persistence (half-life $>$ 2 months in water, $>$ 6 months in soil/sediment)
  2. Bioaccumulation (BCF or $\log K_{ow} \geq$ threshold)
  3. Long-range transport (detected far from source)
  4. Adverse effects

### The Why & How
- **Why so persistent**: Halogenation (Cl, Br) and aromatic rings resist photolytic, chemical, and microbial degradation.
- **How they cause harm**: Most act via **aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR)** binding, disrupting gene expression — leading to immunotoxicity, reproductive toxicity, and carcinogenesis.

## 2.4 Class 4 — Volatile & Semi-Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs/SVOCs)

- **VOCs**: Boiling point $\leq 250°C$ at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa); participate in atmospheric photochemical reactions.
- **Examples**: Benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, xylenes (BTEX); trichloroethylene (TCE); perchloroethylene (PCE); formaldehyde.
- **SVOCs**: Boiling point between 250–400°C; lower vapour pressure. Examples: PAHs, certain pesticides, PCBs (also POPs).

### Why Important
- **VOCs are indoor air quality drivers** — off-gassing from building materials, paints, furnishings.
- Benzene is a Group 1 IARC carcinogen (leukemogenic).

## 2.5 Class 5 — Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs)

- **Structure**: Two or more fused aromatic rings.
- **Examples**: Naphthalene (2 rings), anthracene (3), benzo[a]pyrene (5, BaP — the prototype carcinogen).
- **Sources**: Incomplete combustion of organic matter — vehicle exhaust, coal burning, charbroiled food, cigarette smoke, volcanic activity.
- **Toxicity**: BaP is metabolically activated by CYP450 to **BPDE (benzo[a]pyrene-7,8-dihydrodiol-9,10-epoxide)** — a potent DNA adduct-forming carcinogen.

## 2.6 Class 6 — Emerging Contaminants of Concern (CECs)

- **PFAS (Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances)**: "Forever chemicals" — C–F bond is one of the strongest in organic chemistry. Examples: PFOA, PFOS, GenX. Used in non-stick coatings, firefighting foams (AFFF).
- **Microplastics**: Plastic particles $< 5$ mm. Act as vectors for sorbed hydrophobic pollutants.
- **Pharmaceuticals and Personal Care Products (PPCPs)**: Antibiotics, hormones, fragrances detected in ng/L to μg/L in surface waters.
- **Endocrine Disruptors (EDCs)**: Bisphenol A (BPA), phthalates, atrazine. Mimic or block natural hormones (estrogen, androgen, thyroid).

## 2.7 Class 7 — Radioactive Substances & Inorganic Gases

- **Radon (Rn-222)**: Naturally occurring radioactive gas; second leading cause of lung cancer after tobacco.
- **NOₓ, SO₂, CO, O₃**: Criteria air pollutants regulated by US EPA NAAQS.
- **H₂S, NH₃**: Toxic gases from industrial/agricultural sources.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Bioconcentration Factor (BCF)** | $\text{BCF} = \dfrac{C_{\text{organism}}}{C_{\text{water}}}$ | Ratio of chemical concentration in organism to that in surrounding water (L/kg). BCF $>$ 5000 → high bioaccumulation. |
| **Octanol-Water Partition Coefficient** | $K_{ow} = \dfrac{C_{\text{octanol}}}{C_{\text{water}}}$ | Indicator of lipophilicity. High $\log K_{ow}$ → fat-soluble → bioaccumulative. |
| **Soil Organic Carbon Partition Coefficient** | $K_{oc} = \dfrac{K_d}{f_{oc}}$ | Governs soil mobility. $K_{oc} \geq 500$ → immobile. |
| **Henry's Law Constant** | $H = \dfrac{P}{C}$ | Predicts volatilisation. $H > 10^{-5} \text{ atm·m}^3/\text{mol}$ → volatile. |
| **Atmospheric Half-life (OH radical)** | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_{OH}[OH]}$ | Persistence in atmosphere. |
| **First-Order Degradation** | $C = C_0 e^{-kt}$ | Concentration decay over time. |
| **Biomagnification Factor (BMF)** | $\text{BMF} = \dfrac{C_{\text{predator}}}{C_{\text{prey}}}$ | Concentration increase across trophic levels. |
| **Lethal Dose 50** | $\text{LD}_{50}$ | Dose (mg/kg body weight) killing 50% of test population. |
| **Threshold Limit Value** | TLV (mg/m³ or ppm) | Airborne concentration safe for repeated 8-hr exposure. |
| **Maximum Contaminant Level** | MCL (mg/L) | EPA drinking-water regulatory limit. |

> [!NOTE]
> For a chemical to be classified as **bioaccumulative** under EU REACH, $\log K_{ow} \geq 4.5$ **OR** BCF $\geq 2000$ L/kg.

## 2.9 Cross-Class Comparison Summary

| Class | Persistence | Bioaccumulation | Primary Toxic Endpoint | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heavy Metals | Indefinite | High (esp. Hg, Cd) | Neuro, kidney, cancer | Methylmercury |
| Organochlorines | Years–decades | Very high (log K_ow $\sim$ 6) | Endocrine, cancer | DDT |
| Organophosphates | Days–weeks | Low | Acute neuro (AChE) | Parathion |
| VOCs | Hours–days | Low | CNS, cancer (benzene) | TCE |
| PAHs | Months | Moderate | Cancer (BaP) | Benzo[a]pyrene |
| PFAS | Decades | High | Cancer, immune | PFOA |
| Radioactive | Physical half-life | Variable | DNA damage, cancer | Radon-222 |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantitative Criterion for Bioaccumulation

The relationship between lipophilicity (octanol-water partition coefficient) and bioconcentration can be derived from thermodynamic equilibrium.

### Step 1: Definition of $K_{ow}$

$$K_{ow} = \frac{C_{\text{octanol}}}{C_{\text{water}}}$$

where $C_{\text{octanol}}$ is the equilibrium concentration in octanol (L/kg) and $C_{\text{water}}$ is the equilibrium concentration in water (mg/L).

**Textual Logic:** Octanol is used as a surrogate for biological lipid membranes. The ratio tells us how much chemical prefers fat over water.

### Step 2: Logarithmic Form

$$\log K_{ow} = \log C_{\text{octanol}} - \log C_{\text{water}}}$$

**Logic:** Since $K_{ow}$ values span many orders of magnitude ($\sim 10^{-1}$ to $\sim 10^{7}$), the logarithm is used for convenience.

### Step 3: Empirical Correlation to BCF

The **Veith-Korner relationship** (1979) is the standard empirical model relating BCF to $K_{ow}$ for non-metabolised, non-ionised organic chemicals:

$$\log \text{BCF} = 0.85 \cdot \log K_{ow} - 0.70$$

**Logic:** Chemicals with higher octanol affinity accumulate more in lipid tissues. The 0.85 slope reflects that BCF grows slightly slower than $K_{ow}$ due to membrane transport kinetics.

### Step 4: Worked Example — DDT

Given: $\log K_{ow}(\text{DDT}) = 6.91$ (literature value).

$$\log \text{BCF} = 0.85 \times 6.91 - 0.70$$

$$\log \text{BCF} = 5.8735 - 0.70 = 5.1735$$

$$\text{BCF} = 10^{5.1735} \approx 1.49 \times 10^{5} \text{ L/kg}$$

**Interpretation:** A BCF of ~149,000 L/kg for DDT confirms its **extreme bioaccumulation potential**, consistent with the Stockholm Convention POP classification.

## 3.2 Derivation: Persistence Criterion from First-Order Kinetics

### Step 1: Governing Equation

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -kC$$

where $k$ is the first-order degradation rate constant (per unit time).

### Step 2: Integration from $C_0$ to $C$

$$\int_{C_0}^{C} \frac{dC}{C} = -\int_{0}^{t} k\, dt$$

$$\ln C - \ln C_0 = -kt$$

$$C = C_0 e^{-kt}$$

### Step 3: Half-Life Expression

Setting $C = C_0/2$ at $t = t_{1/2}$:

$$\frac{C_0}{2} = C_0 e^{-k t_{1/2}}$$

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k t_{1/2}}$$

$$\ln 2 = k t_{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} \approx \frac{0.693}{k}}$$

### Step 4: Worked Example — Atrazine Degradation

Atrazine half-life in soil: $t_{1/2} = 60$ days. Compute the rate constant.

$$k = \frac{\ln 2}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{60} = 0.01155 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

If initial concentration is $C_0 = 10$ mg/kg, what is the concentration after 1 year (365 days)?

$$C = 10 \times e^{-0.01155 \times 365} = 10 \times e^{-4.215}$$

$$e^{-4.215} = 0.01479$$

$$C = 10 \times 0.01479 = 0.148 \text{ mg/kg}$$

**Interpretation:** After 1 year, ~98.5% of the atrazine has degraded — characteristic of a **moderately persistent** herbicide (well below the POP threshold of $t_{1/2} \geq 6$ months in soil).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Toxic Equivalency Factor (TEF) for Dioxins

For dioxins and dioxin-like PCBs, toxicity is normalised using **Toxic Equivalency Factors (TEFs)** relative to 2,3,7,8-TCDD (TEF = 1.0).

| Congener | TEF (WHO 2005) |
|---|---|
| 2,3,7,8-TCDD | 1.0 |
| 1,2,3,7,8-PeCDD | 1.0 |
| 1,2,3,4,7,8-HxCDD | 0.1 |
| 2,3,7,8-TCDF | 0.1 |
| PCB-126 (dioxin-like) | 0.1 |
| PCB-169 | 0.03 |

**Total Toxic Equivalent (TEQ):**

$$\text{TEQ} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (C_i \times \text{TEF}_i)$$

where $C_i$ is the concentration of congener $i$ and $\text{TEF}_i$ is its TEF.

### Step-by-Step Calculation Example
Given a sample contains:
- 2,3,7,8-TCDD: $C_1 = 2$ pg/g, $\text{TEF}_1 = 1.0$
- 1,2,3,7,8-PeCDD: $C_2 = 5$ pg/g, $\text{TEF}_2 = 1.0$
- PCB-126: $C_3 = 50$ pg/g, $\text{TEF}_3 = 0.1$

**Step 1: Contribution of 2,3,7,8-TCDD**

$$\text{TEQ}_1 = 2 \text{ pg/g} \times 1.0 = 2 \text{ pg TEQ/g}$$

**Step 2: Contribution of PeCDD**

$$\text{TEQ}_2 = 5 \times 1.0 = 5 \text{ pg TEQ/g}$$

**Step 3: Contribution of PCB-126**

$$\text{TEQ}_3 = 50 \times 0.1 = 5 \text{ pg TEQ/g}$$

**Step 4: Total TEQ**

$$\text{TEQ}_{\text{total}} = 2 + 5 + 5 = 12 \text{ pg TEQ/g}$$

**Interpretation:** This sample exceeds typical soil screening levels (often 1–10 pg TEQ/g depending on land use), triggering remediation requirements.

## 3.4 Mercury Speciation Equilibrium

The environmental behaviour of mercury is controlled by redox speciation.

### Key Reactions

**Oxidation (in oxygenated water):**

$$\text{Hg}^{0}_{(aq)} + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Hg}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2$$

**Reduction (in anaerobic sediments, mediated by Hg-resistant bacteria):**

$$\text{Hg}^{2+} + \text{NADPH} \xrightarrow{\text{MerA}} \text{Hg}^{0} + \text{NADP}^+ + \text{H}^+$$

**Methylation (sulfate-reducing bacteria in anaerobic sediments):**

$$\text{Hg}^{2+} + \text{CH}_3\text{Co} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{Hg}^+ + \text{Co}$$

**Logic:** Methylmercury (CH₃Hg⁺) is the form that bioaccumulates in fish and crosses the blood-brain barrier. Anaerobic sediments with high organic matter are hotspots for methylation.

### Bioaccumulation Calculation
For a fish in a lake with [Hg²⁺] = 0.05 ng/L water:
$$\text{BCF}_{\text{fish}} \approx 10^{5} \text{ L/kg (literature)}$$

$$C_{\text{fish}} = \text{BCF} \times C_{\text{water}} = 10^{5} \times 0.05 \text{ ng/L} = 5000 \text{ ng/kg} = 5 \mu\text{g/kg}$$

This exceeds the US FDA action level of **1.0 mg/kg (1000 μg/kg)** if biomagnification across multiple trophic levels is included.

## 3.5 Risk Quotient (RQ) — Screening-Level Risk Assessment

A foundational equation for environmental risk assessment of any chemical class:

$$\text{RQ} = \frac{\text{PEC}}{\text{PNEC}}$$

where:
- **PEC** = Predicted Environmental Concentration
- **PNEC** = Predicted No-Effect Concentration

**Decision rule:**
- $\text{RQ} < 0.1$ → **Negligible risk**
- $0.1 \leq \text{RQ} < 1$ → **Low risk** (further evaluation)
- $\text{RQ} \geq 1$ → **High risk** (remediation or regulation required)

### Worked Example — Atrazine in River Water
- $\text{PEC} = 5 \mu\text{g/L}$ (measured)
- $\text{PNEC} = 0.6 \mu\text{g/L}$ (from EC50/AF = 600 μg/L ÷ 1000)

$$\text{RQ} = \frac{5}{0.6} = 8.33$$

**Interpretation:** $\text{RQ} = 8.33 \gg 1$ → **High environmental risk**; mitigation measures warranted.

## 3.6 Persistence–Bioaccumulation–Toxicity (PBT) vPvB Decision Matrix

A chemical is classified as **PBT** if it meets all three criteria:

| Criterion | PBT Threshold | vPvB Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| **P**ersistence (half-life) | Marine water $>$ 40 d; Freshwater $>$ 40 d; Soil $>$ 120 d; Sediment $>$ 120 d; Air $>$ 2 d | Same but stricter in all media |
| **B**ioaccumulation | BCF $\geq 2000$ L/kg | BCF $\geq 5000$ L/kg |
| **T**oxicity | NOEC $\leq 0.01$ mg/L (chronic) | Same |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A "vPvB" (very Persistent, very Bioaccumulative) substance can be flagged as a "Substance of Very High Concern" (SVHC) under REACH even without the T criterion.
