## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**

**Q1. Define neurotoxicology. List any four common environmental neurotoxicants.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Neurotoxicology is the study of adverse effects of physical, chemical, and biological agents on the structure and function of the nervous system.
Four common environmental neurotoxicants are:
1. Lead (Pb)
2. Methylmercury (MeHg)
3. Manganese (Mn)
4. Organophosphate pesticides (e.g., parathion, malathion)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**

**Q2. What is the Blood–Brain Barrier (BBB)? Why is it important in neurotoxicology?**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The Blood–Brain Barrier (BBB) is a specialised, selectively permeable interface formed by **tight junctions between brain capillary endothelial cells**, supported by pericytes and astrocyte end-feet. It regulates the passage of substances from systemic circulation into the brain parenchyma.
**Importance in neurotoxicology:**
1. It governs whether an environmental pollutant reaches the brain.
2. Lipophilic, low-MW toxicants (e.g., MeHg) readily cross it → high neurotoxicity.
3. It is **immature in fetuses and neonates**, making them highly vulnerable to neurodevelopmental toxicants such as lead.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the mechanisms of neurotoxicity induced by heavy metals in the environment, with special reference to lead and methylmercury. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A community obtains drinking water with **Pb concentration 0.05 mg/L**. If the daily water intake is 2 L/day and body weight is 60 kg, calculate the ADD and Hazard Quotient (HQ), given that the EPA RfD for lead is **0.0015 mg/kg/day**. Comment on the risk. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a) — Mechanisms of Pb and MeHg Neurotoxicity

**Lead (Pb):**
- **Mimics Ca²⁺:** Pb²⁺ enters neurons through voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels and disrupts Ca²⁺-dependent processes (neurotransmitter release, synaptogenesis).
- **Disrupts BBB:** Pb opens BBB tight junctions → vasogenic oedema, especially in children → encephalopathy.
- **Inhibits ALA-Dehydratase (ALA-D)** and **ferrochelatase** in the heme synthesis pathway → anaemia + oxidative stress.
- **Inhibits NMDAR and protein kinase C** → learning deficits, lowered IQ.
- **Hypomyelination:** Disrupts oligodendrocyte function → delayed nerve conduction.

**Methylmercury (MeHg):**
- **Lipophilic & high-affinity for –SH groups** of cysteine in tubulin → microtubule depolymerisation → **block of fast axonal transport** → dying-back axonopathy.
- **Cerebellar damage:** Atrophy of granule cell layer → ataxia, dysmetria.
- **Visual cortex and auditory cortex degeneration** → visual field constriction, hearing loss.
- **Fetal vulnerability:** Concentrates in fetal brain via amino acid transporters → Minamata disease, cerebral palsy.
- **Oxidative stress:** Depletes GSH; generates ROS → neuronal apoptosis.

#### Model Solution (b) — ADD and HQ for Lead

**Step 1:** Given values:
- $C = 0.05$ mg/L
- $IR = 2$ L/day
- $BW = 60$ kg
- $RfD = 0.0015$ mg/kg/day
- $EF, ED, AT$ → assume continuous exposure → $EF = 365$ days/year, $AT = 365$ days × 70 years = 25,550 days (or simply 1 day for non-cancer, ADD = C·IR/BW)

**Step 2:** For non-cancer risk, use:
$$ADD = \frac{C \cdot IR}{BW} = \frac{(0.05)(2)}{60}$$

$$ADD = \frac{0.1}{60} = 1.67 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mg/kg/day}$$

**Step 3:** Compute HQ:
$$HQ = \frac{ADD}{RfD} = \frac{1.67 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.11$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation:
Since **HQ = 1.11 > 1**, the community's exposure to lead through drinking water **exceeds the reference dose**, indicating a **potential neurotoxic risk**, particularly for children. Remedial actions: install point-of-use RO filters, replace lead service lines, and switch to corrosion-resistant pipes.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a)** Describe the Blood–Brain Barrier, its components, and the factors that determine whether a pollutant can cross it. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of Michaelis–Menten kinetics, derive the rate equation for acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibition in the presence of a competitive organophosphate inhibitor, and explain why this inhibition is considered **"irreversible"** in practice. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (a) — BBB Structure and Permeability

**Components of the BBB:**
1. **Brain capillary endothelial cells** — joined by tight junctions (claudin-5, occludin, ZO-1).
2. **Pericytes** — embedded in the basement membrane, regulate capillary blood flow.
3. **Astrocyte end-feet** — ensheath > 99% of the capillary surface; induce and maintain tight junctions via sonic hedgehog (Shh) signalling.
4. **Basement membrane** — type IV collagen and laminin.
5. **Neurons and microglia** — contribute to the **neurovascular unit**.

**Factors governing pollutant crossing:**
- **Molecular weight < 400 Da** → favour crossing.
- **Log P (lipophilicity)** between 1.5–2.5 → optimum.
- **Hydrogen bonding** — < 5 donors, < 10 acceptors.
- **Charge** — neutral species cross better than ionised ones.
- **Active transport hijack** — e.g., MeHg uses the L-type amino acid transporter (LAT1) disguised as methionine.
- **P-glycoprotein (Pgp) efflux** — pumps many drugs back; its saturation by a pollutant increases brain burden.

#### Model Solution (b) — AChE Inhibition Derivation

(See full derivation in Section 3.1.)

**Key result:**
$$v_i = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m\left(1 + \frac{[I]}{K_i}\right) + [S]}$$

**Why "irreversible" in practice:**
- Organophosphates (e.g., paraoxon, sarin) form a **covalent phosphoester bond** with the active-site serine (Ser203) of AChE.
- The P–O bond is **chemically stable** and is not readily hydrolysed.
- The half-life of recovery equals the rate of **de novo AChE synthesis** (~ weeks).
- After "aging" (loss of an alkyl side chain), the enzyme becomes **permanently resistant to oxime reactivation**.
- Hence, although the inhibitor is technically removable, in physiological time-frames the inhibition is effectively irreversible.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units in ADD/HQ problems.** Always carry mg/kg/day — examiners mark 1 mark off if missing.
> 2. **Confusing AChE inhibition kinetics** — write the *correct* formula (with $K_{m,app}$ or pseudo-first-order loss).
> 3. **BBB question trap:** Don't just say "lipid-soluble pollutants cross." Always mention MW < 400 Da, Log P, transporter hijack, and Pgp efflux.
> 4. **Lead vs. MeHg confusion:** Lead causes demyelination and IQ deficits; MeHg causes axonopathy and Minamata disease. Examiners check this distinction.
> 5. **In derivation of HQ:** Show each substitution step. Skipping the numeric plug-in loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Mn vs. MPTP:** Students often wrongly classify Mn as a "Parkinson's agent." Correct answer: Mn damages the *globus pallidus* → manganism, a distinct disorder.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

- [ ] **Neurotoxicology** = study of toxicant effects on the nervous system.
- [ ] **Neurons are vulnerable** because of high lipid content, high O₂ demand, and limited regeneration.
- [ ] **BBB components** = tight-junction endothelium + pericytes + astrocyte end-feet + basement membrane.
- [ ] **BBB crossing depends on** MW, Log P, H-bonding, charge, and transporter interactions.
- [ ] **Five cardinal mechanisms**: ion channel disruption, neurotransmitter disruption, oxidative stress, axonal transport failure, myelin damage.
- [ ] **Lead** mimics Ca²⁺; inhibits ALA-D, NMDAR; causes encephalopathy and IQ deficit.
- [ ] **MeHg** binds –SH, disrupts microtubules → Minamata disease.
- [ ] **Mn** damages globus pallidus → manganism (responds poorly to L-DOPA).
- [ ] **Organophosphates** irreversibly phosphorylate AChE → cholinergic crisis; treat with **atropine + 2-PAM + diazepam**.
- [ ] **PCBs/dioxins** disrupt thyroid hormone → neurodevelopmental toxicity.
- [ ] **Formulas to memorise**: RfD, ADD, HQ, $P_{app}$, $k_i$ for AChE inhibition, Hill equation.
- [ ] **HQ > 1** → potential risk; **HQ < 1** → generally acceptable.
- [ ] **Biomarkers**: Blood Pb, urinary As, blood Hg, hair Hg, RBC AChE, MRI for Mn.
- [ ] **Stockholm Convention** bans/restricts 12 initial POPs (the "Dirty Dozen") to limit neurodevelopmental toxicants.
- [ ] **Minamata Convention on Mercury (2013)** aims to control anthropogenic Hg emissions globally.
- [ ] **Engineering controls**: RO, ion exchange, GAC, wet scrubbers, electrostatic precipitators, PPE, and closed-system handling of pesticides.
